reliability.text: 2nd gen's 2.4L Ecotec is a known oil-burner (class-action level, below); the 3.6L LFX is the more durable option. 3rd gen is mid-pack for the segment; the 1.5L LYX shows a pattern of owner complaints (below) but no major recall crisis; diesel LH7 was discontinued after 2019 on low demand, not defect. 4th gen too new for meaningful data (2025+). Consumer Reports / J.D. Power / IIHS scores: Not verified — needs OEM source.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: 2nd gen: 2.4L Ecotec excessive oil consumption from stuck/worn piston rings — multiple class actions (Berman v. GM, Buchholz v. GM) with warranty extensions and settlements; dealer fix is ring/piston replacement; low-oil engine damage is the common kill mode (no low-oil warning before knock). Timing-chain wear on high-mileage 2.4s. 3rd gen: 1.5L LYX — documented pattern of cold-climate turbocharger oiling faults/check-engine lights and sensor failures (service-trade documentation); oil dilution/consumption complaints on early builds; 9T50 9-speed (2.0L) harsh-shift complaints. 4th gen: no significant pattern as of mid-2026 — monitor.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per OLM; dexos1 Gen 2/Gen 3 — 5W-30 (2.4L/3.6L), 0W-20 (1.5T LYX/LSD); dexos2 for the 1.6L diesel. Oil capacity 5.3 qt (LYX, per lubricant lookup data), 5.0 qt (LEA), 6.0 qt (LFX). Spark plugs: iridium ~97,500 mi. Coolant: Dex-Cool 5 yr/150,000 mi. Transmission fluid: 6T40 uses GM AF6 fluid (total fill ~9 qt); severe-service 45,000 mi; 9T50/CVT/8-spd per severe-service schedule. Brake fluid: inspect; no fixed GM interval. All engines timing-chain (no belt).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: Not verified — needs OEM source (2.0T 3rd gen was the quick one, period tests ~6.5–7 sec; 1.5T models ~9 sec). No factory performance trim; RS (2022+) is appearance-only. The 1.6L diesel was the efficiency play (577-mile highway range advertised).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Light scene: Trifecta and other OBD flash tunes exist for 2.4L/3.6L 2nd gen and 1.5T/2.0T 3rd gen (Stage 1 tunes for the 2.0T), drop-in filters, cat-back exhausts on the 2.0T. Mostly cosmetic mods (wheels, leveling). The 4th-gen ACTIV has spawned the usual light off-road dress-up parts.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: 2nd gen rode the Theta platform shared with the GMC Terrain — conventional transverse four/V6 with a 6-speed. The 3rd gen (2018) moved to the D2XX architecture (shared with Terrain/Envision), cutting ~400 lbs and switching to an all-turbo lineup including the only diesel in the US compact-crossover class at the time; 2022 brought a facelift with revised fascia and the RS trim. The 4th gen (2025) keeps the 1.5L turbo formula but splits drivetrains — CVT for FWD, a conventional 8-speed for AWD — and moves to an 11.3-inch Google-based cockpit; the all-electric Equinox EV is a separate Ultium-based model line.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Not an enthusiast vehicle in any generation — the community is owner-DIY oriented, focused on the 2.4L oil-consumption fix and keeping the 1.5T on strict oil intervals. The 2.0T (2018–2020) has a small tuning following as the "sleeper" family crossover. Diesel 3rd-gens have a niche hypermiler following.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda CR-V, Toyota RAV4, Ford Escape, Mazda CX-5, Hyundai Tucson. The Equinox sells on price and interior room, not driving polish — the CR-V and CX-5 out-drive it, and the RAV4 (especially hybrid) beats it on reliability reputation and resale. The 2nd-gen 3.6L and 3rd-gen 2.0T are the picks for power; avoid neglected 2.4L cars.

chevrolet-malibu.subtitle: Midsize sedan, 2015 (8th gen final year, Epsilon II), 2016–2025 (9th gen, E2XX platform; production ended November 2024 — 2025 was the last model year)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 8th gen (2015): 2.5L LKW Ecotec I4, NA, DI (196 hp @ 6,300 rpm / 191 lb-ft @ 4,400); 2.0L LTG turbo I4 (259 hp @ 5,300 / 295 lb-ft). 9th gen (2016–2025): 1.5L LFV turbo I4 (163 hp / 184 lb-ft); 2.0L LTG turbo I4 (250 hp / 258–260 lb-ft, 2016–2022, Premier); 1.8L LKN I4 + two-motor electric drive Hybrid (182 hp combined, 2016–2019). Note: one GM database summary mislabels the LFV as naturally aspirated — it is turbocharged.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 8th gen: Hydra-Matic 6T40/6T70 6-speed automatic. 9th gen: 6T40 6-speed (1.5T, 2016–2018); CVT (1.5T, 2019–2025); 8L45 8-speed automatic (2.0T, 2016–2018); 9T50 9-speed automatic (2.0T, 2019–2022); Voltec-style EVT two-motor drive unit (Hybrid).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 196 (LKW) / 259 (LTG 8th gen) / 163 (LFV) / 250 (LTG 9th gen) / 182 combined (Hybrid).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD only, all years, all trims. No AWD ever offered on US 8th/9th gen.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 2015 (8th gen): 191.5 L x 73.0 W x 57.6 H in; wheelbase 107.8 in; curb ~3,393–3,660 lbs (2013 figures, same body). 9th gen: 193.8 L (194.2 in from 2019 facelift) x 73.0 W x 57.3–57.6 H in; wheelbase 111.4 in; curb 3,086–3,388 lbs (2016–2018), 3,126–3,223 lbs (2019+).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: 9th gen is one of GM's more trouble-free passenger cars of the era — the 1.5T/6-speed and Hybrid powertrains have modest complaint counts vs. the trucks; the headline defect is the "Shift to Park" shifter fault (below), which reached a class-action settlement. 2.0T/9T50 cars carry the usual GM 9-speed shift complaints. 8th gen (2015) is a simple, durable powertrain. Consumer Reports / J.D. Power scores: Not verified — needs OEM source.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: "Shift to Park" message / no-shutoff defect (shifter microswitch and harness) on 2016–2019 Malibu — class action Emmrich v. GM; settled (Jefferson v. GM settlement: $500 cash + up to $375 repair reimbursement); complaint pattern continued on 2020–2022 cars per later filings (Green v. GM). 1.5L LFV: electronic wastegate actuator/turbo faults and sensor issues documented in the service trade; CVT (2019+) shudder/complaints on high-mile cars. Hybrid: Voltec-family electronics generally solid; 12V battery/BECM-family faults shared with Volt-era parts. 8th gen: electric power-steering and intake-actuator faults at high mileage.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per OLM; dexos1 Gen 2/Gen 3 — 5W-30 (2.5L LKW, 2.0T LTG), 0W-20 (1.5T LFV, Hybrid). Spark plugs: iridium ~97,500 mi. Coolant: Dex-Cool 5 yr/150,000 mi. Transmission fluid: 6T40 uses AF6; CVT/9T50/8L45 per severe-service 45,000-mi schedule; Hybrid drive unit fluid per schedule. Brake fluid: inspect, no fixed GM interval. All engines timing-chain (LKW/LTG/LFV/LKN all chain-driven).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: Not verified — needs OEM source (period tests: 2.0T Premier ~6.4 sec; 1.5T ~8.5 sec). No factory performance trim; RS (2019+) is appearance-only. Hybrid was the tech flagship ( Volt-derived drive unit, 1.5 kWh pack ).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Minimal scene. Trifecta/ZZP-style OBD tunes exist for the 2.0T (LTG is shared with Camaro/ATS, so bolt-ons — intake, downpipe, intercooler — cross over); 1.5T tunes exist but gains are modest. Otherwise wheels, tint, and cosmetics. No factory performance variant.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The 8th-gen car (Epsilon II, shared with Buick Regal) ended its run in 2015 with the direct-injected 2.5L replacing the old port-injected 2.4L. The 9th gen (2016) moved to the lighter E2XX architecture (~300 lbs lighter, 3.6-in-longer wheelbase), introduced the 1.5T/CVT small-displacement formula, and borrowed the Volt's two-motor drive unit for a genuinely efficient Hybrid (46 mpg combined). The 2019 facelift swapped the 1.5T's 6-speed for a CVT and made the 2.0T Premier-only; GM ended Malibu production in November 2024, ending Chevy's US sedan line.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Little enthusiast cachet — the Malibu is fleet/family transportation. The LTG 2.0T cars get mild tuner attention because the engine is shared with the Camaro 2.0T. The Hybrid has a small efficiency-nerd following as a "Volt sedan without the plug." In the used market it's valued as cheap, simple transportation.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota Camry, Honda Accord, Nissan Altima, Hyundai Sonata, Kia K5. The Malibu undercuts all of them on used-market price and the 1.5T matches segment fuel economy, but the Camry/Accord beat it on resale, interior quality, and long-term reliability reputation; the 2.0T Premier is an underrated value as a quick used sedan. Discontinued — parts and service support continue via GM.

chevrolet-traverse.subtitle: Three-row midsize/full-size crossover SUV, 2015–2017 (1st gen, Lambda platform), 2018–2023 (2nd gen, C1XX platform), 2024–2026 (3rd gen, C1-2 platform)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 1st gen (2015–2017): 3.6L LLT High-Feature V6, NA, DI — 281 hp / 266 lb-ft (single exhaust) or 288 hp / 270 lb-ft (dual exhaust). 2nd gen (2018–2023): 3.6L LFY V6, NA, DI (310 hp @ 6,800 / 266 lb-ft @ 2,800); 2.0L LTG turbo I4 (257 hp / 295 lb-ft, RS trim only, 2018–2019). 3rd gen (2024–2026): 2.5L LK0 turbo I4 (328 hp @ 5,500 / 326 lb-ft @ 3,500).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 1st gen: Hydra-Matic 6T75 6-speed automatic. 2nd gen: 9T65 9-speed automatic. 3rd gen: 8-speed automatic (GM's 9-speed architecture with one forward gear removed, per GM Authority).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 281/288 (LLT, exhaust-dependent) / 310 (LFY) / 257 (LTG) / 328 (LK0). (Third-party listings quoting 315–316 hp for the LK0 reflect metric or preliminary figures; 328 hp is the US SAE rating.)

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD standard, AWD optional on 1st and 2nd gen (2nd-gen High Country AWD-only; LS FWD-only). 3rd gen: FWD standard, AWD optional; Z71 gets a twin-clutch off-road-capable AWD system with Terrain Mode and hill-descent control.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 1st gen: 203.7 L x 78.5 W x 69.9 H in; wheelbase 118.9 in; curb 4,647–4,956 lbs. 2nd gen: 204.3 L (205.9 in for 2022–2023 facelift) x 78.6 W x 70.7 H in; wheelbase 120.9 in; curb 4,310–4,362 lbs FWD (AWD ~4,500+ lbs). 3rd gen: 204.5 L x 79.6 W (87.9 with mirrors) x 69.6–69.9 H in; wheelbase 121.0 in; curb ~4,793 lbs (AWD per KBB 2026 specs; FWD lower — exact FWD figure Not verified — needs OEM source).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: (EPA city/hwy/combined, fueleconomy.gov) 1st gen: FWD 15/22/18; AWD 15/22/17. 2nd gen: 3.6L FWD 18/27/21; 3.6L AWD 17/25/20; 2.0T RS FWD 20/26/22. 3rd gen: FWD 20/27/23; AWD 19/24/21.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: 1st gen: the LLT timing-chain issue (below) is the defining fault; otherwise the 6T75/LLT powertrain is durable when maintained, and 2013+ builds had updated chains/tensioners. 2nd gen: mid-pack; 9T65 shift-quality complaints are the main pattern; LFY chain drive is improved over LLT. 3rd gen: too new for statistical data — the LK0 is a new engine family (a shorter-stroke relative of the Silverado's 2.7L L3B), so longevity is unproven as of mid-2026. Consumer Reports / J.D. Power scores: Not verified — needs OEM source.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: 1st gen: 3.6L LLT timing-chain stretch (codes P0008/P0016/P0017) — the well-documented GM 3.6 HFV6 issue; chain/tensioner/phaser replacement is a common big-ticket job; wave-plate/shift faults on high-mile 6T75s; power-steering and water-pump failures at mileage. 2nd gen: 9T65 9-speed jerking/hesitation/rough shifting (widely reported by owners and service publications); "Shift to Park" shifter defect on 2018–2019 (included in the Jefferson v. GM settlement); some LFY timing-chain and front-camera/module faults. 3rd gen: early-build software/infotainment recalls reported; no mechanical pattern established as of mid-2026.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per OLM; dexos1 — 5W-30 (LLT, LFY), 0W-20 (LK0, dexos1 Gen 3). Oil capacity 5.3 qt (LK0 class), 6.0 qt (3.6L). Spark plugs: iridium ~97,500 mi. Coolant: Dex-Cool 5 yr/150,000 mi. Transmission fluid: 6T75/9T65/8-spd — severe-service 45,000 mi (AF6-family fluids; 6T75 total fill ~9 qt class). AWD transfer case/rear drive module fluids per severe schedule. Brake fluid: inspect, no fixed GM interval. All engines timing-chain.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 3rd gen 2.5T — ~7.6 sec 0–100 km/h (≈7.3 sec 0–60) per published 2025–2026 spec listings; other generations Not verified — needs OEM source (period tests: 2nd-gen 3.6L ~6.7–7.0 sec). No factory performance trim; RS is appearance; Z71 (2024+) is the off-road trim with 5,000-lb tow rating.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Minimal — this is a family hauler. Typical owner mods: trailer hitches/wiring, brake upgrades for towing, all-terrain tires and small lifts on Z71s, OBD tunes for the 2.0T RS (LTG) and interest in LK0 tuning as the 2.7L L3B aftermarket ecosystem grows. No factory performance variant.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: 1st gen was GM's Lambda-platform minivan replacement (shared with Enclave/Acadia/Outlook): transverse 3.6L HFV6, 6-speed, up to 8 seats. The 2nd gen (2018) moved to the long-wheelbase C1XX architecture with meaningful weight reduction (~350 lbs), the updated LFY V6 and a 9-speed; a 2022 facelift added standard active-safety content. The 3rd gen (2024) is the biggest change: V6 dropped for the 2.5L LK0 turbo four (more power and torque than the V6), C1-2 platform with GM's Global B electrical architecture enabling OTA updates, a standard 17.7-inch touchscreen, and a Z71 off-road variant.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: No cult following — the Traverse community is owners' forums focused on the LLT timing-chain job and 9T65 behavior. The 2.0T RS (2018–2019) is a curiosity for LTG tuners. The 3rd-gen Z71 is earning respect as a family adventure rig. DIY community is strong because timing-chain and trans work is expensive at dealers.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda Pilot, Toyota Highlander/Grand Highlander, Ford Explorer, Kia Telluride, Hyundai Palisade. The Traverse consistently wins on interior/cargo space per dollar; the Telluride/Palisade beat it on refinement and warranty, the Highlander on reliability reputation and resale. 3rd-gen power/fuel-economy gains (328 hp, 23 mpg combined FWD) make it more competitive on paper; long-term LK0 durability is the open question.

chevrolet-tahoe.subtitle: Full-size body-on-frame SUV, 2015–2020 (4th gen, GMT K2XX), 2021–2026 (5th gen, GMT T1XX; 2025 mid-cycle refresh)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 4th gen (2015–2020): 5.3L L83 EcoTec3 V8, NA, DI, AFM (355 hp @ 5,600 rpm / 383 lb-ft @ 4,100) — sole engine 2015–2017; 6.2L L86 EcoTec3 V8 (420 hp @ 5,600 / 460 lb-ft @ 4,100) added for RST 6.2L Performance Edition (2018) and Premier (2019–2020). 5th gen (2021–2026): 5.3L L84 V8 (DFM, 355/383); 6.2L L87 V8 (DFM, 420/460); 3.0L LM2 Duramax turbo-diesel I6 (277 hp @ 3,750 / 460 lb-ft @ 1,500, 2021–2024); 3.0L LZ0 Duramax I6 (305 hp / 495 lb-ft, 2025–2026).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 4th gen: Hydra-Matic 6L80 6-speed automatic (5.3L, all years); 10L90 10-speed automatic (6.2L, 2018–2020). 5th gen: Hydra-Matic 10L80 10-speed automatic (RPO MHS gas / MQC diesel) across the board.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: RWD standard, 4WD optional every year/trim (AutoTrac 2-speed transfer case; Z71 off-road package both gens). No AWD-only variant. Max towing 8,200–8,400 lbs depending on engine/drivetrain (5th gen).

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 4th gen: 204.0 L x 80.5 W x 74.4 H in; wheelbase 116.0 in; curb 5,308–5,631 lbs. 5th gen: 210.7 L x 81.1 W x 75.8 H in; wheelbase 120.9 in; curb 5,473–5,845 lbs.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: 4th gen: fundamentally durable (6L80 + L83 is a proven combo), but shares the AFM lifter/oil-consumption exposure of the K2XX trucks; A/C condenser failures are a well-known service item. 5th gen: elevated complaint/recall exposure — the 6.2L L87 is covered by NHTSA recall 25V-274 plus two open federal investigations (RQ26001, EA25007); DFM lifter failures documented on L84/L87; sibling-platform NHTSA data (T1XX Yukon: 1,238 complaints/20 recalls over 2021–2025, L87 = 894 of them) indicates the complaint load is concentrated in the 6.2L, with the 5.3L and especially the 3.0L diesel materially lower. Consumer Reports / J.D. Power / IIHS scores: Not verified — needs OEM source.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: 4th gen: AFM lifter collapse/oil consumption (5.3L); A/C condenser and refrigerant-line failures (very common service item); 6L80 torque-converter shudder at mileage; cracked dash (cosmetic, widely reported); brake vacuum-pump recall-era issues on late K2 trucks/SUVs. 5th gen: L87 connecting-rod/crankshaft recall 25V-274 (2021–2024 builds, ~598K US vehicles incl. Tahoe; engines built 3/2021–5/2024) and post-remedy failure investigation RQ26001 (Jan 2026); EA25007 probing additional 2019–2021 L87s; DFM lifter failures (shared with Silverado); 10L80 valve-body/harsh-shift complaints; 3.0L LM2 long-crank/fuel-prime complaints. Diesel LZ0 currently under a production constraint (2026) — availability, not a defect.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per OLM; dexos1 Gen 2/Gen 3 5W-30 (L83/L84 gas V8s) — L87 recall remedy updated inspected engines to Mobil 1 Supercar 0W-40 fill spec; dexosD 0W-20 for 3.0L diesels. Spark plugs: iridium ~97,500 mi. Coolant: Dex-Cool 5 yr/150,000 mi. Transmission fluid: 6L80/10L80 — severe-service 45,000 mi (towing = severe service). Transfer-case and front/rear axle fluids per severe schedule (~50,000 mi). Brake fluid: inspect, no fixed GM interval. Air-suspension (Air Ride Adaptive, 5th gen) and Magnetic Ride Control require no scheduled fluid service but inspect lines/struts. All engines timing-chain.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: Not verified — needs OEM source (period instrumented tests: 6.2L RST ~5.7–5.9 sec; 5.3L ~7.1 sec; diesel ~7.8 sec). Performance variants: RST (2018+, 6.2L + 10L90 + Magnetic Ride on Performance Edition), Z71 (off-road), 5th-gen RST with available 6.2L, and dealer/aftermarket supercharger packages. Max tow 8,400 lbs (5.3L 2WD, 5th gen).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Strong scene: Whipple/Magnuson/ProCharger supercharger kits for 5.3/6.2 (600+ hp builds are common), ECM/TCM tunes, AFM/DFM disablers and delete kits, long-tube headers/cat-backs, lowering kits (street Tahoes), 2–4-in lifts and 33–35-in tires on Z71s, brake upgrades. The 6.2L/10-speed RST is the community's factory-hot-rod baseline.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: 4th gen K2XX was a traditional body-on-frame SUV on the Silverado K2 architecture — boxed frame, EcoTec3 DI V8s, solid rear axle with 5-link coil suspension, Magnetic Ride Control available. The 5th gen T1XX (2021) was the Tahoe's biggest redesign in decades: ~4.9 in longer wheelbase, an independent rear suspension (first on a Tahoe) unlocking a much larger third row and cargo floor, available four-corner Air Ride Adaptive Suspension, and the addition of the 3.0L Duramax inline-six diesel. The 2025 refresh brought the 17.7-inch screen, updated LZ0 diesel, and new driver-assist content.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Tahoe has a genuine dual personality in the scene: street/performance crowd (lowered 6.2L builds, superchargers) and overland/tow crowd (Z71 lifts). The 2018–2020 RST 6.2L Performance Edition is a modern cult trim — the closest thing to a factory Tahoe SS. Diesel 5th-gens are prized by high-mileage towers. Forum consensus: buy the 5.3L unless you want the 6.2's power — the L87 recall history hangs over 2021–2024 6.2s.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Ford Expedition, Toyota Sequoia, Nissan Armada, GMC Yukon (twin), Jeep Wagoneer. The Tahoe leads the class in cargo/packaging since the IRS redesign and in powertrain choice (only body-on-frame SUV with a diesel option); Expedition counters with a stronger twin-turbo V6 torque curve, Sequoia with hybrid-only powertrain and Toyota resale. Reliability-wise the 5.3L/diesel Tahoes are competitive; the 6.2L's recall record is a 2021–2024 used-buy checkpoint.

ford-f-150.subtitle: Full-size pickup (regular/SuperCab/SuperCrew), 2015–2020 (13th gen, P552 aluminum body), 2021–2026 (14th gen, P702)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 13th gen (2015–2020): 3.5L Ti-VCT "Cyclone" NA V6 (2015–2017 base); 2.7L EcoBoost twin-turbo V6 (CGI block); 3.5L EcoBoost twin-turbo V6 (Gen 1 2015–16, Gen 2 with port+direct injection 2017+); 5.0L Ti-VCT "Coyote" NA V8 (dual injection from 2018); 3.3L Ti-VCT NA V6 (replaced 3.5 NA, 2018–2020); 3.0L Power Stroke turbo-diesel V6 (2018–2021); 3.5L EcoBoost High-Output (Raptor, 2017–2020). 14th gen (2021–2026): 3.3L V6 (2021–2023, dropped for 2024), 2.7L EcoBoost, 5.0L Coyote, 3.5L EcoBoost, 3.5L PowerBoost full-hybrid V6, H.O. 3.5L (Raptor), 5.2L supercharged V8 (Raptor R, 2023+).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 6R80 6-speed automatic (2015–2017 lineup; 3.3L base through 2020); 10R80 10-speed automatic (Raptor from 2017; 2.7/3.5/5.0/diesel from 2018; sole transmission on 14th gen incl. PowerBoost). Co-developed with GM; Mercon ULV fluid.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 13th gen: 3.5 NA 283 hp; 2.7 EcoBoost 325 hp; 3.5 EcoBoost 365 hp (2015–16) / 375 hp (2017+); 5.0L 385 hp (2015–17) / 395 hp (2018–20); 3.3L 290 hp; 3.0L diesel 250 hp; H.O. 3.5 (Raptor) 450 hp. 14th gen: 2.7 325 hp; 5.0 400 hp; 3.5 400 hp; PowerBoost 430 hp (total system); H.O. 3.5 450 hp; Raptor R 700 hp (2023) / 720 hp (2024+).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 13th gen: 3.5 NA 255 lb-ft; 2.7 375 lb-ft (2015–17) / 400 lb-ft (2018+); 3.5 EB 420 lb-ft (2015–16) / 470 lb-ft (2017+); 5.0L 387 lb-ft (2015–17) / 400 lb-ft (2018–20); 3.3L 265 lb-ft; 3.0L diesel 440 lb-ft; H.O. 3.5 510 lb-ft. 14th gen: 2.7 400 lb-ft; 5.0 410 lb-ft; 3.5 500 lb-ft; PowerBoost 570 lb-ft; Raptor 510 lb-ft; Raptor R 640 lb-ft.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: 4x2 (RWD) standard / 4x4 optional on mainstream trims both generations; 4x4 standard on King Ranch/Limited/Tremor/Raptor (14th gen); part-time 4WD with available electronic locking rear diff; Torsen front diff on Raptor.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 13th gen: length 209.3–250.5 in, width 79.9 in, height 75.2–76.9 in, wheelbase 122.4–163.7 in, curb approx. 4,050–5,700 lb depending on cab/bed/drivetrain. 14th gen: same basic envelope (regular cab ~209 in to SuperCrew 6.5-ft ~243+ in; wheelbase ~122.8–163.9 in), curb approx. 4,200–5,900 lb — exact per configuration: Not verified — needs OEM source (varies widely by cab/bed/engine).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: 2023 2.7 EcoBoost 2WD: 20 city / 26 hwy / 22 comb (fueleconomy.gov). 2024 5.0L 4WD: 18/23/20 (fueleconomy.gov). 2025 ratings per Ford dealer spec sheets: 2.7 19/25/21, 3.5 EB 17/25/20, PowerBoost 22/24/23, 5.0 16/24/19. 3.0L Power Stroke diesel EPA figures: Not verified — needs OEM source (widely reported ~22/30/25 for 2WD).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Mixed record. 2021–2022 F-150 Hybrid (PowerBoost) made Consumer Reports' "used cars to avoid" list and was among CR's least-reliable models in 2022–2023 surveys; CR later ranked the 2026 F-150 Hybrid #1 in its segment on early-life data — long-term 10-speed durability still in question per owner reports. 5.0L V8 generally regarded as the most durable engine option.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) 3.5L EcoBoost cam-phaser rattle on cold start — Ford Customer Satisfaction Program 21N03 (NHTSA MC-10204046) covers phaser replacement. (2) 10R80 10-speed: harsh/erratic shifting, E/F clutch wear and valve-body/lead-frame faults; multiple class actions and high-mileage failure reports; subject of extended-service actions. (3) 5.0L Coyote 2018–2020 excessive oil consumption — TSB 19-2365 (PCM reprogram; short-block replacement in severe cases). (4) 2018+ 5.0L "typewriter tick" — SSM 49761; often characteristic but can indicate VCT/valvetrain faults. (5) Gen-1 3.5 EcoBoost (2015–16) timing-chain stretch.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Intelligent Oil-Life Monitor; do not exceed 1 yr / 10,000 mi between oil changes (5W-30 for EcoBoost/5.0; severe-duty towing intervals shorter). Spark plugs 100,000 mi. Coolant: initial 6 yr / 100,000 mi, then 3 yr / 50,000 mi. Automatic transmission fluid 150,000 mi (10R80 uses Mercon ULV; many shops service at 60k). Front/rear axle and transfer-case fluid 150,000 mi (severe duty sooner). Brake fluid every 3 years (2021+ owner manuals). All engines in scope are timing-chain — no belt service.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: Raptor (H.O. 3.5, 450 hp) 0–60 ~5.3 s; Raptor R (5.2L SC, 700–720 hp) 0–60 ~3.6–3.9 s; 5.0L SuperCrew 0–60 ~5.7–6.1 s; PowerBoost ~5.4 s. Tremor off-road package (2021+).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Huge aftermarket: ECU tunes (5 Star, Livernois, SCT) on EcoBoost trucks, Whipple supercharger kits for 5.0L, cold-air intakes (S&B, Injen, aFe), intercooler and turbo upgrades, leveling/lift kits, 33–37-in tires; 10R80 built-transmission upgrades (Suncoast, BD) common for tuned/tow rigs.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The 2015 (13th-gen) F-150 switched to a military-grade aluminum-alloy body on a high-strength steel boxed frame, cutting up to ~700 lb — the segment's biggest materials gamble. The 2021 (14th-gen) redesign kept the aluminum-body/steel-frame formula, added the PowerBoost series-parallel hybrid (35 kW motor in the 10R80 bell housing) with Pro Power Onboard generator (up to 7.2 kW), SYNC 4, and OTA capability. Gen-2 3.5 EcoBoost (2017) added port injection alongside DI to combat intake-valve carbon and raise output.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Raptor is the cult trim — the benchmark factory off-road truck; EcoBoost trucks are popular tuner platforms (strong low-end torque, big gains from tunes); 5.0 Coyote favored by the supercharger crowd. Enormous forum support (f150forum, f150ecoboost.net) and deep parts ecosystem.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Ram 1500 (smoother ride, mild-hybrid eTorque, similar 8-speed reliability questions), Chevrolet Silverado/GMC Sierra 1500 (simpler powertrains, V8 AFM/DFM lifter issues), Toyota Tundra (better long-term reliability reputation, less capability per dollar). F-150 wins on capability breadth, towing tech, and parts availability; trails Tundra on long-term reliability reputation.

ford-escape.subtitle: Compact SUV, 2015–2016 (3rd gen C520 pre-facelift), 2017–2019 (3rd gen facelift), 2020–2026 (4th gen CX482)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 3rd gen (2015–2019): 2.5L Duratec iVCT NA I4; 1.6L EcoBoost turbo I4 (2015–2016); 1.5L EcoBoost turbo I4 (2017–2019, replaced 1.6); 2.0L EcoBoost twin-scroll turbo I4. 4th gen (2020–2026): 1.5L EcoBoost turbo I3 (cylinder deactivation); 2.0L EcoBoost twin-scroll I4; 2.5L iVCT Atkinson-cycle I4 full hybrid (FHEV); 2.5L plug-in hybrid (PHEV, FWD only).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 3rd gen: 6F35 6-speed automatic (all gas engines). 4th gen: 8F35 8-speed automatic (1.5/2.0 gas); eCVT planetary hybrid transaxle (HF45 family) on Hybrid/PHEV.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 3rd gen: 2.5L 168 hp; 1.6L 178 hp; 1.5L 179 hp; 2.0L 245 hp. 4th gen: 1.5L I3 181 hp @ 6,000 rpm; 2.0L 250 hp; Hybrid 200 hp (total system); PHEV 221 hp (total system).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 3rd gen: 2.5L 170 lb-ft; 1.6L 184 lb-ft; 1.5L 177 lb-ft; 2.0L 275 lb-ft. 4th gen: 1.5L 190 lb-ft; 2.0L 280 lb-ft; Hybrid engine-side 155 lb-ft (system total not published by Ford — Not verified — needs OEM source).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: 3rd gen: FWD standard; Intelligent AWD optional on 1.5L and 2.0L EcoBoost trims (2.5L FWD only). 4th gen: FWD standard on 1.5 gas and Hybrid; AWD optional (2.0L AWD-only in most years); PHEV FWD only.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 3rd gen: length ~178.1 in, width ~72.4 in, height ~66.3 in, wheelbase 105.9 in, curb ~3,515–3,840 lb (per automobile-catalog spec sheets). 4th gen: length 180.5 in, width 74.1 in, height 65.9–66.1 in, wheelbase 106.7 in, curb ~3,300–3,890 lb (hybrid heaviest).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: 3rd gen: 2.5 FWD 21/29; 1.5 FWD 23/30; 2.0 AWD 20/27 (J.D. Power 2018 spec page). 4th gen: 1.5 FWD 28/34/30; Hybrid 44/37/41 (FWD); PHEV 105 MPGe combined with ~37 mi EV range (2021–2022); 2.0 AWD approx. 23/31/26 (EPA, per fueleconomy data aggregators — cross-check advised).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: 3rd gen is average; its EcoBoost engines carry the coolant-intrusion defect (below). 4th gen improved over the 2020 launch year (Ford's Chicago launch QC problems of 2019–2020 were widely reported); 2020–2021 cars subject to a NHTSA investigation over front-door check-arm spot-weld failures (~346,000 vehicles). CR rates recent Escapes around average; the Hybrid is the pick of the range.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) Coolant intrusion into cylinders on 1.5L/1.6L/2.0L EcoBoost — cracked cylinder heads/blocks, overheating, engine failure; class action (Reed et al. v. Ford) covering 2013–2019 Escape; Ford SSMs 47625 (10/2018) and 47849 (3/2019) describe coolant-consumption/white-smoke diagnostics on 2017–2019 Escape 1.5L. (2) 6F35 6-speed: valve-body/solenoid wear, harsh shifts (widespread aftermarket replacement units). (3) 2020–2021: NHTSA investigation into front door check-arm attachment weld failure. (4) 4th-gen 1.5L I3: cracked fuel-injector recall (fire risk) — verify coverage by VIN at NHTSA.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: IOLM oil changes, max 1 yr / 10,000 mi (5W-30 1.5/2.0; 0W-20 hybrid). Spark plugs 100,000 mi. Coolant 6 yr / 100,000 mi initial, then 3 yr / 50,000 mi. 6F35/8F35 ATF "filled for life" on paper; Ford schedule lists 150,000 mi — 60k service is prudent shop practice. Hybrid eCVT fluid per owner manual. Brake fluid every 3 years (later manuals). All engines timing-chain.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 3rd-gen 2.0L AWD 0–60 ~6.8–7.2 s; 4th-gen 2.0L AWD ~5.7–6.5 s (quickest in class when new); 1.5 I3 ~8.5–9 s; Hybrid ~8.7 s. No factory performance trim; ST-Line is appearance-only.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Modest scene: ECU tunes for the 2.0L EcoBoost (shared with Focus ST architecture — Garrett PowerMax drop-in turbo supports up to ~350 bhp), intake/exhaust bolt-ons, lowering springs; 1.5 I3 largely ignored by tuners.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: 3rd gen (C520) rode on Ford's C1 platform (Focus-derived) with a torque-vectoring-style AWD system; 2017 facelift swapped the 1.6 for the 1.5 EcoBoost and added auto stop-start. 4th gen (2020, CX482) moved to the C2 platform, dropped ~200 lb, went to a 3-cylinder base engine, and reintroduced hybrid/PHEV variants using a revised Atkinson 2.5L with a planetary eCVT.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Not an enthusiast vehicle; community interest centers on the 2.0L EcoBoost as a "sleeper" (Focus ST powertrain kinship) and on keeping high-mileage hybrids running cheaply. Strong owner-forum documentation of the coolant-intrusion failure pattern.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota RAV4 (reliability benchmark), Honda CR-V (space/refinement), Mazda CX-5 (driving feel), Subaru Forester (AWD standard). Escape counters with strong 2.0L performance and 44-mpg hybrid, but its EcoBoost coolant-intrusion history and launch-year quality hurt its reliability standing vs. RAV4/CR-V.

ford-explorer.subtitle: Mid-size 3-row SUV, 2015–2019 (5th gen U502, facelift from 2016), 2020–2026 (6th gen U625, CD6; facelift 2025)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 5th gen (2015–2019): 3.5L Ti-VCT "Cyclone" NA V6; 2.3L EcoBoost turbo I4 (from 2016); 3.5L EcoBoost twin-turbo V6 (Sport/Platinum). 6th gen (2020–2026): 2.3L EcoBoost I4; 3.0L EcoBoost twin-turbo V6 (Platinum 365 hp tune / ST 400 hp tune); 3.3L NA V6 full hybrid (2020–2023 retail; police PIU continues).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 5th gen: 6F50/6F55 6-speed automatic. 6th gen: 10R80 10-speed automatic (all engines incl. hybrid, which sandwiches an e-motor ahead of the 10-speed).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 5th gen: 3.5 NA 290 hp @ 6,500; 2.3 EB 280 hp; 3.5 EB 365 hp @ 5,500. 6th gen: 2.3 EB 300 hp; 3.0 EB 365 hp (Platinum) / 400 hp (ST); Hybrid 318 hp (combined).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 5th gen: 3.5 NA 255 lb-ft; 2.3 EB 310 lb-ft; 3.5 EB 350 lb-ft @ 3,500. 6th gen: 2.3 EB 310 lb-ft; 3.0 EB 380 lb-ft (365-hp tune) / 415 lb-ft (ST); Hybrid 322 lb-ft.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: 5th gen: FWD standard, Intelligent 4WD (on-demand AWD) optional, standard on Sport/Platinum. 6th gen: RWD standard architecture, Intelligent 4WD optional (standard on ST); hybrid available RWD or 4WD.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 5th gen: length ~197.1–198.3 in, width 78.9 in, height ~69.5–70.4 in, wheelbase 112.6 in, curb ~4,350–4,890 lb. 6th gen: length 198.7–198.9 in, width 78.9 in, height ~69.9–70.2 in, wheelbase 119.1 in, curb ~4,345–4,970 lb (hybrid/ST heaviest).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: 6th gen (fueleconomy.gov, 2023 RWD): 2.3 EB 21/28/24; 3.0 EB 18/26/21. Hybrid (2020–2022 4WD) approx. 23/26/25 — cross-check advised. 5th gen EPA ratings (fueleconomy.gov historical): 3.5 FWD ~17/24/20, 2.3 FWD ~19/27/22, Sport 3.5 EB 4WD ~16/22/18.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Weak spot in the lineup. The 2020 (6th-gen) launch was one of Ford's most troubled: CR named Explorer one of the lowest-scoring models from any manufacturer in its 2020 reliability survey (transmission, engine, electrical complaints), and launch quality was blamed for a 2019 earnings miss. 5th gen is average mechanically (watch the 3.5's internal water pump) but was dogged by the exhaust-odor-in-cabin saga.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: 5th gen: (1) Exhaust/CO odor in cabin under hard acceleration — NHTSA investigation and Ford customer-satisfaction repair program (police PIU scrutiny). (2) 3.5L internal chain-driven water-pump failure — coolant into oil, engine-killer if ignored (well documented across Cyclone V6 applications). (3) PTU (transfer case) fluid breakdown/leaks on AWD models. 6th gen: (1) 228,297-vehicle recall (2020–2021) for 360-degree backup-camera image failure (software). (2) Multiple rollaway-related recalls; (3) 10R80 harsh-shift complaints; (4) water leaks into cargo/spare-tire well (sunroof drains, liftgate/lamp seals) — inspect under cargo floor on used examples.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: IOLM oil, max 1 yr / 10,000 mi (5W-30; 3.0 EB takes 0W-20? — Not verified — needs OEM source, check oil cap/manual). Spark plugs 100,000 mi. Coolant 6 yr / 100,000 mi initial, then 3 yr / 50,000 mi. ATF 150,000 mi scheduled (10R80 Mercon ULV; 60k service recommended for tow use). PTU/rear-drive-unit fluid: severe-duty intervals apply — treat 30–60k as shop best practice (notorious for dark, broken-down fluid). Brake fluid every 3 years. All engines timing-chain.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 6th-gen ST (400 hp) 0–60 ~5.2–5.5 s — quickest mainstream 3-row at launch; 5th-gen Sport 0–60 ~6.0 s; 2.3 RWD ~7.0–7.5 s. ST gets sport-tuned suspension, bigger brakes, 21-in wheels.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Explorer ST has a real tuning scene: ECU calibrations (5 Star, Livernois, AED), Whipple Mega Cooler intercooler, downpipes, intakes — mid-11s quarter-miles on tune+bolt-ons. 5th-gen Sport similarly tunable (3.5 EB responds well to tune). Tow packages and brake upgrades common family-use mods.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: 5th gen was a FWD-based unibody (D4 platform, Taurus-derived) with transverse engines. The 2020 (6th-gen) switch to the longitudinal RWD-based CD6 architecture (shared with Lincoln Aviator) restored a longitudinal engine layout, improved weight distribution and tow manners, and enabled the modular hybrid — a 44-hp motor inside the 10-speed housing feeding a 3.3L V6. 2025 facelift brought a revised interior (Ford Digital Experience) and trimmed the lineup.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The ST is the community darling — "sleeper family hauler" with strong tuner support and an active ST owner community. Older Sports have a cult following as cheap speed. Police Interceptor Utility parts interchanges make some fleet-grade upgrades cheap.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota Highlander (reliability king, dull), Honda Pilot (packaging, V6 simplicity), Kia Telluride/Hyundai Palisade (value and warranty), Jeep Grand Cherokee L. Explorer offers the best powertrain spread (300–400 hp, RWD dynamics, hybrid) but trails Highlander/Pilot/Telluride on predicted reliability — the CR-documented 2020–2021 launch problems weigh on resale.

ford-fusion.subtitle: Mid-size sedan (CD391, 2nd gen), 2015–2016 (pre-facelift), 2017–2020 (facelift; production ended July 2020 — no 2021+ US model)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2016: 2.5L Duratec iVCT NA I4; 1.5L EcoBoost turbo I4; 2.0L EcoBoost twin-scroll I4; 2.0L Atkinson I4 hybrid (FHEV) and Energi plug-in (PHEV). 2017–2020 (facelift): same four-cylinder line-up plus Sport 2.7L EcoBoost twin-turbo V6 (AWD); 1.5/2.0 EcoBoost; 2.5L; Hybrid/Energi.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 6F35 6-speed automatic (gas four-cylinders); 6F55 6-speed (Sport 2.7 AWD); eCVT planetary hybrid transaxle (HF35) on Hybrid/Energi. All SelectShift-capable on upper trims.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 2.5L 175 hp; 1.5 EB 181 hp; 2.0 EB 240 hp (2015–16) / 245 hp (2017–20); Sport 2.7 EB 325 hp; Hybrid 188 hp combined; Energi 188 hp combined.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 2.5L 175 lb-ft; 1.5 EB 185 lb-ft; 2.0 EB 270 lb-ft (2015–16) / 275 lb-ft (2017–20); Sport 2.7 EB 350 lb-ft; hybrid engine-side 129 lb-ft (system figure not published — Not verified — needs OEM source).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD standard; AWD optional on SE/Titanium 2.0L (2015–2018) and standard on Sport; Hybrid/Energi FWD only.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: Length 191.7 in, width 72.9 in (body; ~83.5 in w/ mirrors), height 58.1 in, wheelbase 112.2 in; curb 3,410–3,816 lb (gas; KBB 2019), up to ~4,166 lb for Energi.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: 2019–2020 EPA: 2.5 FWD 21/31/26 (2020; earlier years 21/32); 1.5 EB FWD 23/34/27; 2.0 EB FWD 21/31/25, AWD 20/29/23; Sport 2.7 AWD 17/26/20; Hybrid 43/41/42; Energi 103 MPGe combined / ~25–26 mi EV range / 43/41 gas-only.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: One of Ford's better sedans: the Hybrid (shared architecture with the C-Max/Toyota-patent-licensed eCVT) has an excellent durability record in taxi/fleet use. Black marks are concentrated in the EcoBoost gas engines (coolant intrusion) and the 6F35 automatic.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) Coolant intrusion/cracked head-block on 1.5L/1.6L/2.0L EcoBoost — class action Reed et al. v. Ford covering 2013–2019 Fusion; SSMs 47625/47849 detail coolant-consumption and white-smoke diagnostics on 2014–2019 Fusion 1.5L. (2) 6F35 valve-body/solenoid wear causing harsh/erratic shifts (large aftermarket for reman valve bodies). (3) Shift-cable bushing detachment (rollaway risk) — Ford recall covering ~2013–2016 Fusion; verify completion by VIN. (4) 1.6L (2013–2014) overheating/fire recalls — outside core range but present on early cars the shop will see.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: IOLM oil, max 1 yr / 10,000 mi (5W-20 on 2.5/1.5/2.0; 5W-30 Sport — verify by oil cap). Spark plugs 100,000 mi. Coolant 6 yr / 100,000 mi initial then 3 yr / 50,000 mi. 6F35/6F55 ATF 150,000 mi scheduled; 60k drain-and-fill is prudent given valve-body history. Hybrid eCVT fluid per schedule (150k). Brake fluid every 3 years. All engines timing-chain (the 2.5L and EcoBoost fours are chain-driven).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: Sport 2.7 AWD 0–60 ~5.3 s — genuinely quick for the class; 2.0 EB ~6.9 s; 2.5L ~8.5 s; Hybrid ~8.9 s. Sport adds adaptive dampers and quad-look exhaust.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Sport and 2.0 EcoBoost respond well to ECU tunes (Livernois, SCT); Garrett PowerMax drop-in turbo for 2013–2018 2.0L supports ~350 bhp; intake/exhaust, lowering springs, and sway bars from the Focus ST/Mondeo aftermarket cross-shop lists. Fusion Sport has a small but loyal tuning scene.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: CD391 platform (Ford's global CD4 architecture; kin to Mondeo MK5) with a sophisticated control-blade multi-link rear suspension and, on Sport, continuously controlled damping. 2017 facelift added the rotary shifter, SYNC 3, and the Sport's 2.7L compacted-graphite-iron-block EcoBoost V6. Hybrid uses a power-split eCVT with a 2.0L Atkinson engine — architecture shared with Ford's first-gen hybrid system.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Sport is a certified sleeper — 325 hp AWD sedan that looks like a rental car — and commands a loyal following and strong resale. Mainstream Fusions are appliance cars, but hybrid examples are prized by high-mileage drivers for Prius-level running costs with conventional driving manners.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota Camry and Honda Accord (reliability and resale leaders), Mazda6 (best steering), Hyundai Sonata/Kia Optima (value/warranty). Fusion counters with the best ride/handling balance of the pre-2018 class, available AWD, and the Sport's V6 — but its EcoBoost coolant-intrusion history makes Camry/Accord the safer reliability bet.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: S550 (2015–2023): 3.7L "Cyclone" NA V6 (2015–2017 base); 2.3L EcoBoost turbo I4; 5.0L "Coyote" NA V8 (Gen 2 2015–17, Gen 3 dual-injection 2018+); 5.2L "Voodoo" flat-plane-crank NA V8 (Shelby GT350/R, 2015–2020); 5.2L "Predator" supercharged V8 (Shelby GT500, 2020–2022). S650 (2024–2026): new-gen 2.3L EcoBoost I4 (twin-scroll turbo, port+DI); 5.0L Coyote Gen 4 (dual throttle bodies) in GT and Dark Horse tunes; 5.2L supercharged V8 (GTD, 2025+, track model).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: Getrag MT82-D4 6-speed manual (GT); Tremec TR-3160 6MT (GT350, Mach 1, Dark Horse); 6R80 6-speed automatic (2015–2017); 10R80 10-speed automatic (2018+, sole auto; only transmission on S650 EcoBoost); Tremec TR-9070 7-speed dual-clutch (GT500 only).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: S550: 3.7 V6 300 hp; 2.3 EB 310 hp (330 hp High-Performance Package, 2020–23); 5.0 GT 435 hp (2015–17) / 460 hp (2018–21) / 450 hp (2022–23); Bullitt 480 hp (2019–20); Mach 1 460–470 hp (2021–23); GT350 526 hp; GT500 760 hp. S650: EcoBoost 315 hp; GT 480 hp (486 hp w/ active exhaust); Dark Horse 500 hp; GTD 815 hp.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: S550: 3.7 280 lb-ft; 2.3 EB 320 lb-ft (2015–17) / 350 lb-ft (2018+); 5.0 GT 400 lb-ft (2015–17) / 420 lb-ft (2018–21) / 410 lb-ft (2022–23); GT350 429 lb-ft; GT500 625 lb-ft. S650: EcoBoost 350 lb-ft @ 3,000; GT 415 lb-ft (418 w/ active exhaust); Dark Horse 418 lb-ft; GTD 664 lb-ft.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Rear-wheel drive only, all years; Torsen limited-slip available/standard on performance trims; electronic line-lock and launch control from 2015 (auto) and 2018+ respectively.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: S550: length 188.3–188.5 in, width 75.4 in, height 54.1–54.3 in, wheelbase 107.1 in, curb ~3,530 (EcoBoost) to ~4,225 lb (GT500). S650: length ~189.4–190.2 in, width ~75.5 in (Dark Horse ~76.5 in), height ~54.3–55.0 in, wheelbase 107.0 in, curb ~3,741–4,140 lb — S650 per-trim figures: cross-check Ford media kit (minor variance by trim).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: S650 (fueleconomy.gov, 2024): EcoBoost 10R80 22/33/26; GT 5.0 10R80 15/24/18. S550 EPA: 2018 EcoBoost auto 21/32/25; 2018 GT auto 16/25/19; GT350 14/21/16; GT500 12/18/14 (fueleconomy.gov historical ratings).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Average for a performance car. The Coyote V8 is fundamentally robust (200k+ mile examples common) but 2018+ Gen-3 cars carry documented oil-consumption and valvetrain-noise TSBs; the MT82 manual is the car's weakest link (notchy shifts, clutch issues, class action over 2011–2019 MT82 defects). 10R80 shares the corporate harsh-shift complaints. S650 too new for a full reliability picture; early recall for brake-control-module software (2024).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) 2018+ 5.0L Gen-3 oil consumption — TSB 19-2365 (PCM strategy; short-block in severe cases). (2) "Typewriter tick" valvetrain/DI noise — SSM 49761; mostly characteristic, sometimes VCT solenoid/rocker failure. (3) MT82-D4 6MT: high-effort/notchy shifts, gear-lockout, clutch-drag defects — long-running owner complaints and litigation. (4) GT350 Voodoo: oil consumption and some engine replacements under warranty — verify service history. (5) 10R80 harsh/erratic shifts (corporate issue). (6) 2024 S650 recall: brake-control-module software (autoevolution/NHTSA).

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: IOLM oil, max 1 yr / 10,000 mi (5W-30 5.0; 5W-20 2.3; GT350/GT500 5W-50 full synthetic, track cars per supplement). Spark plugs 100,000 mi (sooner on tuned cars). Coolant 6 yr / 100,000 mi initial, then 3 yr / 50,000 mi. 10R80/6R80 ATF 150,000 mi scheduled; GT500 DCT and TR-3160/MT82 manual gear oil per severe-service schedule for track use. Brake fluid every 3 years — annual flushes for tracked cars. All engines timing-chain (Coyote uses chains; no belt).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: GT500 0–60 3.3 s, 1/4-mile 10.7 s @ 132 mph; GT350 0–60 ~4.0–4.3 s; Mach 1 ~4.2 s; S550 GT (2018 auto) ~3.9–4.2 s; S650 GT ~4.0–4.3 s, Dark Horse ~4.1 s; EcoBoost HPP ~5.1 s, base EB ~5.6 s; GTD sub-3s claim (track model).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: One of the deepest aftermarket ecosystems in the world: superchargers (Whipple, Roush, VMP — 700+ hp bolt-on kits for Coyote), ECU calibrations, long-tube headers, axle-back/cat-back exhausts, Steeda/Cortex suspension, drag and road-course wheel/tire packages; EcoBoost tunes add ~40–60 hp. Factory performance variants: GT350/R, GT500, Bullitt, Mach 1, Dark Horse, GTD.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: S550 (2015) was the first Mustang with independent rear suspension across the range (integral-link), transforming ride and put-down traction; 2018 brought Gen-3 Coyote dual injection and the 10R80. GT500's Predator uses a 2.65L TVS supercharger and a Tremec DCT — a first for a factory Mustang. S650 (2024) is a heavy S550 evolution: same hardpoints and 107-in wheelbase, Gen-4 Coyote with dual airboxes/dual throttle bodies, electronic drift brake, and a digital cockpit.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The default American tuner platform — the Coyote aftermarket rivals the LS in depth. Cult trims: GT350 (flat-plane wail, 8,250-rpm redline — likely future collectible), Bullitt, Mach 1 (last S550 manual hero), Dark Horse. EcoBoost respected as a budget track/drift base. Community hubs: Mustang6G, SVTPerformance, AmericanMuscle/Steeda ecosystems.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Chevrolet Camaro (sharper chassis, worse visibility — discontinued after 2024), Dodge Challenger (bigger, straight-line focus — discontinued after 2023), Toyota GR Supra, Nissan Z, BMW M240i. Mustang is the last V8+manual pony car standing; it matches the Europeans on pace per dollar and beats them on parts cost, but interior quality and the MT82/10R80 issues are the trade-offs.

gmc-sierra-1500.subtitle: Light-duty full-size pickup; 2015–2018 (K2XX, incl. 2019 "Sierra 1500 Limited" carryover), 2019–2026 (T1XX)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: K2XX (2015–2018): 4.3L LV3 EcoTec3 V6 (naturally aspirated, direct injection); 5.3L L83 EcoTec3 V8 (NA, DI, AFM cylinder deactivation); 6.2L L86 EcoTec3 V8 (NA, DI, AFM). T1XX (2019–2026): 2.7L L3B turbo I4 ("Turbo High-Output," renamed "TurboMax" for 2024); 4.3L LV3 V6 (2019–2021 only); 5.3L L84 EcoTec3 V8 (DFM); 6.2L L87 EcoTec3 V8 (DFM); 3.0L LM2 Duramax turbo-diesel I6 (2020–2022) and 3.0L LZ0 Duramax (2023+).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: K2XX: 6-speed 6L80 automatic (MYC) standard; 8-speed 8L90 automatic on 6.2L and select high-trim 5.3L (2015–2018). T1XX: 8-speed 8L90 (2.7L TurboMax and lower-trim 5.3L); 10-speed 10L80 automatic (6.2L, Duramax, and most 5.3L applications by 2023 — 2023 fueleconomy.gov listings show all 5.3L Sierras as 10-spd).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 4.3L: 305 lb-ft. 5.3L: 383 lb-ft @ 4,100 rpm. 6.2L: 460 lb-ft @ 4,100 rpm. 2.7L: 348 lb-ft (2019–2021), increased to 430 lb-ft @ 3,000 rpm (2022+ high-output version). LM2 diesel: 460 lb-ft; LZ0 diesel: 495 lb-ft.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Both generations: RWD (2WD) standard, part-time 4WD optional across most trims; AT4/AT4X and Denali 6.2L models are 4WD-only. Autotrac 2-speed transfer case on 4WD.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: K2XX: length 205.5–229.5 in (regular/double/crew cab), width 80.0 in, height ≈73.5–74.2 in, wheelbase 119.0–153.7 in; curb weight ≈4,500–5,400 lb (approx.). T1XX: length ≈211–242 in, width ≈81 in, height ≈75–79 in (cab/bed dependent), wheelbase 126.5–157.0 in; curb weight ≈4,400–5,600 lb (approx.).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Reputation is mixed-to-below-average for the segment, driven by transmission and valvetrain campaigns rather than structural issues. K2XX 6L80 is regarded as durable; the 8L90 was the subject of a major TCC-shudder service campaign (see Known issues). T1XX reliability is dominated by AFM/DFM lifter failures and the 2021–2024 L87 bottom-end recall (NHTSA 25V274, ~597,630 U.S. vehicles). Owner-complaint volume on NHTSA is materially higher than Toyota Tundra in like-for-like years.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) 8L90 torque-converter-clutch shudder ("rumble strip" vibration 40–80 mph) — moisture-degraded factory fluid; fix per TSB 18-NA-355 is full fluid exchange with Mobil 1 Synthetic LV ATF HP (GM 19417577); Customer Satisfaction Program 18302 extended coverage; multiple class actions filed. (2) AFM/DFM lifter collapse on L83/L84/L86/L87 V8s — stuck collapsed lifters wipe cam lobes; class actions ongoing; GM's published oil-consumption limit is 1 qt/2,000 mi. (3) L87 6.2L connecting-rod/crankshaft defect — recall 25V274 (2021–2024) plus stop-sale; inspection, possible engine replacement, switch to 0W-40. (4) Brake vacuum pump output loss — recall 19V645 (2014–2018 trucks/SUVs, 3,456,111 vehicles), software update remedy. (5) K2XX A/C condenser leaks and 8L90 harsh 1-2 shifts widely documented on owner forums.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per Oil Life Monitor with dexos1-approved oil (0W-20 on EcoTec3 V8s; dexosD 0W-20 on Duramax diesels; post-recall L87s switched to 0W-40). Transmission fluid: change at 45,000 mi under severe service (tow/haul, short trips) — community and dealer practice on 6L80/8L90/10L80; normal-service schedule is longer ("filled for life" language varies by manual — verify per VIN owner's manual). Spark plugs 97,500 mi. Engine coolant (Dex-Cool) 150,000 mi / 5 years. All engines are timing-chain — no belt service. Brake fluid: inspect; replace on condition/moisture (no fixed GM interval — many shops use 3–5 yr). Transfer case and axle fluids: severe-service intervals ~50,000 mi.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 6.2L Denali crew cab 0–60 mph in 5.4 sec (Car and Driver test); 5.3L crew cab ≈7.3 sec (Car and Driver). Factory performance/off-road trims: AT4, AT4X (Multimatic DSSV dampers, front/rear lockers), AT4X AEV Edition, Denali Ultimate. Max towing up to 13,300 lb (diesel, properly equipped).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: One of the most-modified platforms in the US: custom ECU/TCU tuning (HP Tuners, Black Bear Performance — typical +15–30 hp on V8s), AFM/DFM delete kits (cam + lifters + tune), supercharger kits (Whipple/Magnuson on 5.3/6.2), leveling and lift kits, 33–35 in tires, cat-back exhausts (Borla etc. offers 2.7L-specific systems). Duramax LZ0 tuning is an emerging niche.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: K2XX rides on GM's GMT-K2XX body-on-frame architecture with Gen V small-block V8s (direct injection, AFM). The 2019 T1XX brought a new fully boxed frame, aluminum doors/hood/tailgate for ~360 lb weight reduction, available CarbonPro carbon-fiber bed, MultiPro six-function tailgate, and the first half-ton GM diesel (LM2 I6). The 2022 refresh added a new interior with 13.4-in screen, Super Cruise availability, and the AT4X with DSSV dampers; 2023 brought the uprated LZ0 diesel (305 hp/495 lb-ft).

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Sierra benefits from the entire LS/LT aftermarket ecosystem — tuner support is effectively unlimited on V8s. Denali and AT4X have strong followings as "luxury truck" and factory overlander respectively; the 6.2L is the cult engine. The 2.7L TurboMax is gaining respect for tuning headroom but is not yet a cult item.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Mechanical twin of the Chevrolet Silverado 1500 with more upscale trim. Against Ford F-150 (broader powertrain spread, aluminum body), Ram 1500 (better ride, similar Hemi lifter concerns), and Toyota Tundra (better long-term reliability reputation but worse mpg pre-hybrid and less refinement). Sierra's value case is Denali/AT4 content per dollar; its weak points vs rivals are the documented 8L90 and AFM/DFM/L87 issues above.

gmc-terrain.subtitle: Compact 5-seat crossover; 2018–2024 (2nd gen, D2 platform), 2025–2026 (3rd gen)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2nd gen (2018–2024): 1.5L LYX turbo I4 (all years); 2.0L LTG turbo I4 (2018–2020 only); 1.6L LH7 turbo-diesel I4 (2018–2019 only). 3rd gen (2025–2026): 1.5L LSD turbo I4 (sole engine).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 2nd gen: 9-speed automatic (GM 9T45/9T50 family) on gas engines; 6-speed automatic on the 1.6L diesel (per fueleconomy.gov 2018 listings). 3rd gen: CVT on FWD models; 8-speed automatic on AWD models.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 1.5L LYX: 170 hp @ 5,600 rpm. 2.0L LTG: 252 hp. 1.6L LH7 diesel: 137 hp. 1.5L LSD (2025+): 175 hp @ 5,600 rpm.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 1.5L LYX: 203 lb-ft @ 2,000–4,000 rpm. 2.0L LTG: 260 lb-ft. 1.6L diesel: 240 lb-ft. 1.5L LSD: 184 lb-ft (FWD/CVT) or 203 lb-ft (AWD/8-spd) — drivetrain-dependent rating.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: 2nd gen: FWD standard, AWD optional on all trims. 3rd gen: FWD or AWD on Elevation; AWD standard on 2026 AT4 and Denali.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 2nd gen: length 182.3 in, width 72.4 in, height 65.4 in, wheelbase 107.3 in; curb weight 3,449–3,848 lb (FWD 2.0T base to AWD Denali 2.0T as tested). 3rd gen: length ≈181 in, wheelbase 107.5 in (MotorWeek); width/height/curb weight — Not verified — needs OEM source.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: EPA city/highway/combined (fueleconomy.gov): 2018 — 1.5T FWD 26/30/28, AWD 24/28/26; 2.0T FWD 22/28/24, AWD 21/26/23; 1.6 diesel FWD 28/39/32, AWD 28/38/32. 2021 — 1.5T FWD 25/30/27, AWD 25/28/26. 2025 — FWD 26/28/27, AWD 24/28/25 (dealer-published EPA figures; consistent across two dealer sources).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Middling for the class. The mechanically identical Chevrolet Equinox (2018–2025) shows ~1,689 NHTSA complaints and 23 recalls (The Weekly Driver compilation) — Terrain tracks the same powertrains. The 2.0L LTG has the strongest mechanical reputation of the gen-2 engines; the 1.5L carries the oil-consumption and charge-air-cooler complaints (below). Gen 3 is too new for meaningful data.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) 1.5L LYX: excessive oil consumption complaints (piston ring/PCV-related) and moisture/icing buildup in the charge-air cooler causing cold-weather misfires — both widely documented in owner communities and used-parts/service literature; turbocharger oil leaks reported around 60k mi (Haynes). (2) 9T45/9T50 9-speed: harsh/hesitant shift complaints; GM issued multiple software updates. (3) 2023 Acadia/Blazer/Traverse-family transmission recall (~7,000 vehicles) covered GM crossovers of this era — see Acadia entry; Terrain not listed in that campaign. (4) Emissions/EVAP and check-engine complaints are the most common NHTSA categories for the 1.5T.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per Oil Life Monitor, dexos1 Gen 2/Gen 3 (0W-20 on 1.5L/2.0L gas; dexos2/dexosD 5W-30 on 1.6L diesel — verify per owner's manual). Critical on the 1.5T: strict oil-change discipline due to oil-consumption and turbo-lubrication sensitivity (documented shop guidance). Transmission fluid: no consumer-friendly interval published; severe-service ~45,000–50,000 mi is common shop practice on the 9T50. Spark plugs ~97,500 mi (iridium). Coolant (Dex-Cool) 150,000 mi / 5 yr. All engines timing-chain (no belt). Brake fluid: inspect/replace on condition.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 2.0T AWD Denali 0–60 mph in 6.8 sec, quarter-mile 15.4 @ 89 mph (MotorTrend 2018 test, 3,848 lb as-tested). 1.5T models are ≈9 sec 0–60 (2025 FWD: 0–100 km/h 9.2 sec per YallaMotor data). No factory performance trim; AT4 (2022–2024, and 2026 gen-3) is the off-road-appearance variant; Denali is the luxury variant.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Light aftermarket. Trifecta Performance offers plug-and-play calibrations for the 2.0L (and supported 1.5L) — the most common power mod. Otherwise cosmetic: wheels, all-terrain tires, crossbars. No meaningful suspension/turbo upgrade market.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The 2018 second gen moved from the aging Theta platform to GM's D2 architecture (shared with Equinox), shedding roughly 400 lb and downsizing to true compact dimensions; it introduced GM's 9T50 transverse 9-speed and the short-lived 1.6L "whisper diesel." The 2025 third gen (Elevation trim only at launch; AT4/Denali added for 2026) rides an updated D2 derivative with a 15-in portrait infotainment screen, Google built-in, and a split drivetrain strategy — CVT for FWD, 8-speed automatic for AWD.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Essentially no enthusiast following — it is viewed as a nicer-trimmed Equinox. The 2.0T (2018–2020) has a small sleeper following because 252 hp in a 3,600-lb crossover is genuinely quick; that is the trim tuners and used buyers seek out.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota RAV4, Honda CR-V, Mazda CX-5/CX-50, Ford Escape, and its Chevy Equinox twin. The Terrain drives competently but unremarkably; reliability reputation trails the Japanese benchmarks (RAV4/CR-V), roughly matches Escape, and the 2.0T's performance was a genuine differentiator until it was dropped. Value case is weak vs the cheaper, mechanically identical Equinox.

gmc-acadia.subtitle: 3-row midsize crossover; 2017–2023 (2nd gen, downsized C1 "Chi" platform; note: 2017 also offered the old full-size model as "Acadia Limited"), 2024–2026 (3rd gen, returns to full-size 3-row)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2nd gen (2017–2023): 2.5L LCV naturally aspirated I4 (base, FWD only); 3.6L LGX naturally aspirated V6; 2.0L LSY turbo I4 (added 2020). 3rd gen (2024–2026): 2.5L LK0 turbo I4 (sole engine).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 2nd gen: 6-speed 6T70 automatic (2017–2019; RPO M7W FWD / M7U AWD); 9-speed 9T65 automatic (2020–2023). 3rd gen: 8-speed automatic.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 2.5L LCV: 188 lb-ft. 3.6L LGX: 271 lb-ft. 2.0L LSY: 258 lb-ft @ 1,500–4,000 rpm. 2.5L LK0: 326 lb-ft @ 3,500 rpm.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: 2nd gen: FWD standard (2.5L is FWD-only), AWD optional on V6/2.0T; AT4 (2020+) AWD-oriented with twin-clutch rear differential. 3rd gen: FWD standard, AWD optional ($2,000), standard on AT4.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 2nd gen: length 193.6 in, width 75.4 in, height 66.0–66.3 in, wheelbase 112.5 in; curb weight ≈3,950–4,400 lb (approx.). 3rd gen: length 204.0 in, width 79.6 in, height 71–72 in, wheelbase ≈120.9 in (MotorWeek reports "121-inch wheelbase"); curb weight — Not verified — needs OEM source.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: EPA city/highway/combined (fueleconomy.gov): 2022 — 2.0T FWD 22/29/25, AWD 22/27/24; 3.6L FWD 19/27/22, AWD 19/26/21. 2024 — 2.5T FWD 20/27/23, AWD 19/24/21. 2017–2019 six-speed figures (2.5L FWD and 3.6L, pre-9-speed) — Not verified in this pass — pull per-model-year from fueleconomy.gov before publishing.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: 2nd gen is mid-pack: the 6T70 six-speed is a known-quantity unit, and the 3.6L LGX is one of GM's more durable engines; 2020–2023 with the 9T65 improved on early-build shift complaints. 3rd gen (2024–2025): 98 NHTSA complaints compiled so far with brakes, safety systems, and engine as the top categories; NHTSA crash rating 5 stars overall (The Weekly Driver/NHTSA data). 2017–2019 model years carry the higher repair-incident reputation of the generation.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) "Shift to Park" warning/no-start — shifter microswitch/harness fault on 2017–2019-era GM crossovers incl. Acadia; class action and special-coverage repairs documented. (2) 2023-model transmission recall covering ~7,000 vehicles across 2023 Acadia/Blazer/Traverse and 2022–2023 Cadillac XT5/XT6 (lemon-law tracking; verify campaign number via NHTSA by VIN). (3) 9T65 harsh-shift/valve-body complaints on 2020–2023. (4) Early 3rd-gen (2024) 8-speed calibration/quality holds reported at launch. (5) 2.5L LCV is durable but underpowered; PCV/oil-consumption complaints at high mileage.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per Oil Life Monitor, dexos1 (0W-20 on LCV/LSY/LGX; gen-3 LK0 per owner's manual — likely dexos1 Gen 3 0W-20, verify). Transmission fluid: 6T70/9T65/8-spd — severe-service change ~45,000–50,000 mi is standard shop practice; normal schedule per owner's manual. Spark plugs ~97,500 mi (LGX/LSY/LK0). Coolant (Dex-Cool) 150,000 mi / 5 yr. All engines timing-chain (no belt; note the LGX chain system is far more durable than the older LLT 3.6L). Brake fluid: inspect/replace on condition.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 2017 Acadia Denali AWD (3.6L) 0–60 mph in 6.2 sec (zeroto60times aggregation of period tests); 2.0T models ≈7.3 sec (period review consensus); 2025 Acadia Denali AWD (2.5T) 0–60 in 7.8 sec per zeroto60times — treat gen-3 figure as single-source. AT4 is the off-road-appearance trim (gen-3 AT4: 1-in lift, all-terrain tires, skid plates, twin-clutch AWD). No performance trim.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Minimal enthusiast aftermarket. Trifecta offers calibrations for the 2.0L LSY; V6 models see occasional intake/exhaust bolt-ons. Most "mods" are AT4-style: all-terrain tires, leveling, roof racks, tow accessories (max tow 5,000 lb V6/2.5T with trailering package).

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The 2017 second gen abandoned the original Lambda full-size architecture for the smaller C1 ("Chi") platform — 7 in shorter and ~700 lb lighter — with the 3.6L LGX (DI, VVT, AFM on later calibrations) as the volume engine; the 2020 facelift added the AT4 trim, LSY turbo, and 9-speed. The 2024 third gen reversed course back to full-size 3-row dimensions (204 in, longer than the original 2007 Acadia) on an updated C1 variant shared with Traverse/Enclave, replacing the V6 with the high-output 2.5L LK0 turbo and adding a 15-in screen and available Super Cruise.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Little enthusiast cachet; community interest centers on the AT4 and on keeping high-mileage V6 models alive (the LGX is respected as a durable, tune-friendly GM engine in other applications). Denali trims have a loyal family-hauler following.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Ford Explorer, Toyota Highlander, Kia Telluride, Hyundai Palisade, Honda Pilot, and its Chevy Traverse twin. Gen-2 Acadia was quieter and more maneuverable than rivals but gave up third-row room; Telluride/Palisade and Highlander hold stronger reliability reputations. Gen-3's 2.5T out-powers most base rivals on torque, and value/equipment (screen, Super Cruise) is competitive, but early-launch quality data is still thin.

gmc-yukon.subtitle: Full-size body-on-frame SUV (incl. Yukon XL long-wheelbase); 2015–2020 (K2XX), 2021–2026 (T1XX)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: K2XX (2015–2020): 5.3L L83 EcoTec3 V8 (NA, DI, AFM); 6.2L L86 EcoTec3 V8 (Denali). T1XX (2021–2026): 5.3L L84 EcoTec3 V8 (DFM); 6.2L L87 EcoTec3 V8 (Denali, optional AT4); 3.0L LM2 Duramax turbo-diesel I6 (2021–2022), 3.0L LZ0 Duramax (2023+).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: K2XX: 6-speed 6L80 automatic (5.3L, 2015–2020); 8-speed 8L90 (6.2L Denali, 2015–2017); 10-speed 10L80 (6.2L, 2018–2020). T1XX: 10-speed 10L80 across all engines.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Both gens: RWD (2WD) standard, part-time 4WD optional; AT4 is 4WD-standard; Denali 6.2L predominantly 4WD (2WD Denali offered some years). XL variants mirror standard-wheelbase drivetrains.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: K2XX (std wheelbase): length 203.9 in, width 80.5 in, height 74.4 in, wheelbase 116.0 in; curb weight ≈5,300–5,700 lb (approx.). T1XX: length 210.0 in, width 81.0 in, height 76.5 in, wheelbase 120.9 in; curb weight ≈5,500–5,900 lb (approx.). XL adds ~15 in of wheelbase/length both gens.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Below the Toyota Sequoia benchmark, roughly par for domestic BOF SUVs. K2XX trouble clusters: 8L90 shudder (2015–2017 Denali), AFM lifters, brake vacuum pump (recall 19V645). T1XX: 2021 launch year has the highest recall density of the generation (seat-belt, fuel-system, brake, and air-bag campaigns per The Weekly Driver/NHTSA); 2021–2024 6.2L covered by engine recall 25V274; Duramax models run the cleanest complaint profile of the generation.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) 8L90 TCC shudder on 2015–2017 (and later 8-spd) — TSB 18-NA-355 fluid exchange with Mobil 1 LV ATF HP; Customer Satisfaction Program 18302 covers 2015–2020 Yukon 8L90 to 6 yr/100,000 mi. (2) AFM/DFM lifter collapse on 5.3/6.2 V8s — class actions; cam/lifter job is the classic high-mileage failure. (3) L87 6.2L recall 25V274 (2021–2024): rod/crank defect, engine replacement path. (4) Vacuum-pump brake-assist recall 19V645 (2015–2018 Yukon). (5) T1XX: 10L80 harsh-shift/valve-body complaints; electronic shifter (ETRS) and fuel-pump-module campaigns on 2021–2023; Magnetic Ride Control shock and air-suspension leaks/compressor failures at mileage on Denali/AT4.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Identical GM truck schedule to Sierra 1500: oil per Oil Life Monitor, dexos1 0W-20 V8s / dexosD 0W-20 diesel (post-recall L87: 0W-40); transmission fluid 45,000 mi severe service (tow use strongly advised at this interval on 6L80/8L90/10L80); spark plugs 97,500 mi; coolant 150,000 mi/5 yr; all engines timing-chain; brake fluid on condition; transfer-case and axle fluids ~50,000 mi severe service.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 mph: 2015 Yukon XL Denali 5.6 sec; 2017 XL Denali 4WD 5.8 sec; 2025 Yukon Denali 4WD 5.9 sec; 2022 Yukon AT4 5.3L 7.0 sec; 2025 AT4 Ultimate diesel ≈6.9 sec (zeroto60times aggregation). Performance-flavored trims: AT4/AT4 Ultimate (6.2L available), Denali/Denali Ultimate. No factory horsepower upgrade trims.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Strong aftermarket: supercharger kits are the signature Denali mod — Hennessey HPE650 for the 6.2L (665 hp, 0–60 in 4.5 sec on a 2015 XL Denali, documented by LSX Magazine); ECU/TCU tunes (HP Tuners ecosystem), AFM deletes, level kits and 33s on AT4, cat-back exhausts. Diesel LZ0 tuning emerging.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: K2XX shares its architecture with the Silverado/Sierra pickups — body-on-frame, solid rear axle, Gen V small-blocks. The 2021 T1XX was the first Yukon with an independent rear suspension, enabling a lower floor, a real third row, and +4.9 in of wheelbase; it added available Air Ride adaptive suspension, Magnetic Ride Control, the first factory diesel in Yukon history, and (2025) a 16.8-in portrait screen and Super Cruise expansion.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Denali is a genuine cult object in the full-size SUV world — the default answer to "fast luxury family hauler" — and the 6.2L's LS-ecosystem tuner support (cams, blowers, headers) is effectively unlimited. AT4 has a growing overland following; the Duramax is the road-trip/tow favorite for its 27-mpg highway rating.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Twin of the Chevrolet Tahoe, sibling of the Cadillac Escalade; rivals: Ford Expedition, Toyota Sequoia, Jeep Wagoneer, Nissan Armada. The 2021+ IRS gives Yukon the best ride/handling balance in the domestic class, and the diesel is a segment exclusive among rivals except none match its mpg. Reliability trails Sequoia (Toyota's V8/TTV6 record aside, GM's AFM/DFM and L87 issues are well documented); the GMC premium over a Tahoe is justified mainly by trim content.

honda-civic.subtitle: Sedan / coupe / hatchback — 2015 (9th gen, FB/FG), 2016–2021 (10th gen, FC/FK), 2022–2026 (11th gen, FE/FL)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015: 1.8L R18Z1 SOHC i-VTEC I4 (NA); Si: 2.4L K24Z7 DOHC i-VTEC I4. 2016–2021: 2.0L K20C2 DOHC I4 (NA); 1.5L L15B7 turbo I4; Si: L15B7 high-boost; Type R (2017–2021): 2.0L K20C1 turbo. 2022–2026: 2.0L K20C2; 1.5L L15B8 turbo; Si: L15B7 200-hp tune; Type R (FL5, 2023+): K20C1; 2025+ Sport/Sport Touring hybrid: 2.0L Atkinson LFC-M1 + two-motor e:HEV.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 2015: 5MT or CVT (1.8L), 6MT (Si). 2016–2021: CVT or 6MT (1.5T Sport/Si; Type R 6MT only). 2022–2026: revised CVT w/ Step-Shift (2.0L/1.5T), 6MT (Si, Type R), clutch-type e-CVT — no conventional gearbox on hybrid (engine-clutch drive at highway speed).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD only, all generations and trims including Si and Type R (helical LSD on Si/Type R).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: EPA 2015: CVT 29/37/33, HF 31/40/34, 5MT 28/35/31, Si 22/31/25, Hybrid 43/45/44. EPA 2021: 2.0L CVT sedan 30/38/33; 1.5T CVT sedan 32/42/36; 1.5T Sport sedan (AV-S7) 30/38/33; Si 29/37/32; Type R 22/28/25. EPA 2025: 2.0L sedan CVT 32/41/36; 2.0L hatch CVT 30/38/34; hybrid sedan 50/47/49; hybrid hatch 50/45/48; Si 27/37/31; Type R 22/28/24 (fueleconomy.gov).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: 9th gen — strong; simple R18/K24 powertrains, few systemic faults. 10th gen — good powertrain reliability but dragged by A/C condenser failures (Honda extended condenser warranty to 10 yr/unlimited miles, TSB 19-091 / NHTSA MC-10194916) and early 1.5T oil dilution in cold climates (Honda product update covering 2016–18 1.5T Civics in 21 cold-weather states, per WardsAuto). 11th gen — strong early record; recall exposure so far is fleet-wide items (Denso fuel pump, passenger seat weight sensor).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: 10th gen A/C condenser micro-leaks → loss of cooling (warranty extension + class action, sources below); 1.5T fuel/oil dilution on 2016–2018 builds (product update w/ ECU & HVAC recalibration); 10th gen A/C compressor failures also documented in aftermarket channels; Denso low-pressure fuel-pump impeller recall spans 2018–2020 builds. 11th gen: no pattern failures documented as of research date — "no systemic issues yet documented."

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Maintenance Minder (MM) system: 0W-20 oil, MM code A/B (~7,500–10,000 mi or 12 mo typical); CVT fluid HCF-2 (MM sub-code 3, ~30k severe service); MT fluid Honda MTF; coolant Honda Long Life Type 2 (blue) — first change 120k/10 yr, then 60k/5 yr; iridium plugs ~105k–120k (MM code 4); brake fluid every 3 yr regardless of MM. ALL Civic engines in scope are timing CHAIN (R18Z1, K24Z7, K20C2, L15B7/L15B8, K20C1, LFC-M1) — no belt service.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 10th gen 1.5T sedan ≈7.2 s, Si ≈6.3 s, Type R (FK8) ≈5.0 s (publication instrumented tests — not re-verified this session, confirm before print). 11th gen verified: hybrid sedan 6.9 s (Motor Matchup sim), 2024 Si 7.4 s (Motor Matchup). Type R FL5: FWD Nürburgring/Suzuka FWD lap records; 0–60 ≈4.9–5.0 s per publication tests (verify before print).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Huge ecosystem: Hondata FlashPro & KTuner reflashes (Si/1.5T gains of 10–20%), JB4 piggybacks (240–280 hp packages on L15B7), PRL intakes (dyno-verified ~6% on 11th gen 2.0L), downpipes/cat-backs (Milltek etc. for FK8/FL5), coilovers, sway bars. Factory performance variants: Si (coupe 2015; sedan/hatch 2017+), Type R FK8 (2017–2021) & FL5 (2023+).

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The 10th gen moved Civic to a new global compact architecture with a ~25% stiffer body and introduced Honda's first US-market mass-volume turbo (L15B7). The 11th gen (FE/FL) is an evolution with a longer wheelbase, further body-rigidity gains, and a cleaner interior (honeycomb-mesh dash); the 2025 hybrid adds the two-motor e:HEV system (engine mostly acts as generator, direct-drive clutch at cruise). All engines are direct-injected except the port-injected R18Z1; all use timing chains and VTEC (valve-timing) systems that techs must account for on diagnosis.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: One of the deepest tuner bases in the industry (CivicX.com, CivicXI.com); Si is the value sweet spot (LSD, 6MT), Type R is a cult object and tracks-day staple. Even base 1.5T cars respond well to bolt-ons + tune, which drives steady shop business for clutches, intercooler piping, and CVT-aware calibrations.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota Corolla, Mazda3, Hyundai Elantra, VW Jetta. Civic typically leads on powertrain refinement and resale; Mazda3 feels more premium inside; Elantra N/GR Corolla out-muscle the Si but the Type R remains the FWD performance benchmark. Reliability is top-tier aside from the documented 10th-gen A/C and 1.5T dilution issues.

honda-accord.subtitle: Midsize sedan (coupe through 2017) — 2015–2017 (9th gen, CR), 2018–2022 (10th gen, CV), 2023–2026 (11th gen, CY)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2017: 2.4L K24W1 DI I4 (185 hp; Sport 189 hp); 3.5L J35Y1/Y2 SOHC V6 w/ VCM (278 hp); Hybrid (2014–15, 2017): 2.0L Atkinson + i-MMD two-motor, 212 hp combined. 2018–2022: 1.5L L15B7/BE turbo (192 hp); 2.0L K20C4 turbo (252 hp, detuned K20C1-family); Hybrid: 2.0L Atkinson i-MMD (212 hp combined). 2023–2026: 1.5L L15CG turbo (192 hp); Hybrid: 2.0L Atkinson LFC-M1 two-motor e:HEV (204 hp).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 2015–2017: CVT (I4), 6AT (V6), 6MT (I4 & V6 coupe/Sport); hybrid: clutch-type e-CVT. 2018–2022: CVT (1.5T), Honda 10-speed automatic (2.0T — first 10AT in a FWD production car), 6MT offered on 1.5T/2.0T Sport through 2020; hybrid e-CVT. 2023–2026: revised CVT w/ Step-Shift (1.5T only); hybrid e-CVT. No manual or V6 after 2017; no 2.0T after 2022.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 181 lb-ft @ 3900 (K24W; Sport 182); 252 lb-ft @ 4900 (J35Y); 192 lb-ft @ 1500–5000 (1.5T); 273 lb-ft @ 1500–4000 (K20C4); hybrid 247 lb-ft traction-motor torque (11th gen, Honda 2026 release).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD only, all generations (no AWD Accord ever offered in the US).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: EPA 2017: I4 CVT 27/36/30; Sport CVT 26/34/29; I4 6MT 23/32/26; V6 6AT 21/33/25; V6 Sport sedan 21/32/24; V6 coupe 6MT 18/28/21; Hybrid 49/47/48. EPA 2021: 1.5T 30/38/33; Sport/Touring 1.5T 29/35/32; 2.0T 10AT 22/32/26; Hybrid 48/48/48; Hybrid Sport/Touring 44/41/43. EPA 2024: 1.5T 29/37/32; Hybrid EX-L 51/44/48; Hybrid Sport/Touring 46/41/44 (fueleconomy.gov; 2026 EX-L hybrid rated 51 city/48 combined per Honda).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: 9th gen — among the most durable midsize sedans of its era; K24W and J35Y are proven units. 10th gen — good overall but 1.5T shares the L15-family oil-dilution exposure and early builds had battery-drain/infotainment complaints. 11th gen — strong early record; hybrid-heavy mix (no V6, no 2.0T) reduces mechanical risk. IIHS Top Safety Pick+ across most of the range (verify per model year before publishing).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: 9th gen: V6 VCM-related plug fouling/oil consumption (documented J-series VCM pattern; class-action powertrain warranty extensions covered earlier J35 variants — confirm applicability by VIN), starter-motor failures, Bluetooth-module battery drain. 10th gen: 1.5T fuel/oil dilution in cold-climate short-trip use (same Honda product-update family as CR-V/Civic); Denso fuel-pump recall (2018–2020); weak 12V batteries/parasitic drain; 2018 infotainment glitches. 11th gen: no pattern failures documented as of research date.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Maintenance Minder; 0W-20 all engines (5.7 qt V6, 3.7–4.4 qt I4s — verify by VIN); 6AT fluid DW-1; 10AT fluid Honda ATF Type 3.1; CVT fluid HCF-2; coolant Type 2 (120k/10 yr first, then 60k/5 yr); iridium plugs ~105k–120k; brake fluid 3 yr. Timing: K24W, L15, K20C4 = CHAIN; J35Y V6 = BELT — 105k mi/7 yr with water pump (major service, budget accordingly).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 9th-gen V6 6MT/AT ≈5.6 s (Car and Driver instrumented test, quoted via DriveAccord); 10th-gen 2.0T 10AT ≈5.5–5.7 s (publication tests — verify before print); 11th-gen hybrid 0–100 km/h 6.5 s (Driving.ca). Sport trims (Sport, Sport 2.0T, Sport-L Hybrid) are appearance/chassis packages; no factory "Type R" Accord.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Hondata FlashPro for 2018–2022 1.5T/2.0T (10–20% gains stage 1/2, verified product), KTuner, downpipes/intakes, 4-bar MAP sensor for big-boost 1.5T/2.0T builds (28+ psi), coilovers/sways. 9th-gen V6 coupe 6MT and 10th-gen 2.0T 6MT are the cult collector trims; HFP (Honda Factory Performance) accessories were offered on 9th gen.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The 10th gen ditched the V6 for a pair of VTEC Turbo fours on a stiffer, lighter platform and debuted Honda's FWD 10-speed automatic (K20C4) plus the two-motor i-MMD hybrid. The 11th gen (2023) is a refinement: same 111.4-in wheelbase, retuned CVT with Step-Shift, and a revised e:HEV hybrid with side-by-side motors and direct injection on the 2.0L Atkinson engine. All I4s are chain-driven; the 9th-gen V6 keeps the belt + VCM hardware techs know well.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: DriveAccord.net is the hub. The 9th-gen V6 coupe 6MT ("last V6 manual midsizer") and the 10th-gen 2.0T 6MT (≈1% take rate) have genuine cult status and strong resale. Tuning support is real but modest compared with Civic; most modded Accords are 2.0T FlashPro builds.
