comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota Camry, Nissan Altima, Hyundai Sonata, Kia K5, (formerly) Mazda6. Accord consistently wins on steering/chassis polish and hybrid drivability; Camry counters with available AWD + hybrid combo (Accord hybrid is FWD-only) and a V6 through 2023; K5/Sonata undercut on price/warranty. Long-term reliability: 9th-gen Accord ≈ Camry; 10th-gen 1.5T slightly behind on the dilution question.

honda-cr-v.subtitle: Compact SUV — 2015–2016 (4th gen facelift, RM), 2017–2022 (5th gen, RW/RT), 2023–2026 (6th gen, RS/RT)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2016: 2.4L K24W9 DI "Earth Dreams" I4 (NA) — replaced the port-injected K24Z8 in the 2015 facelift. 2017–2022: 1.5L L15BE VTEC Turbo I4; 2020–2022 Hybrid: 2.0L Atkinson + i-MMD two-motor (212 hp combined, AWD only). 2023–2026: 1.5L L15BX VTEC Turbo; Hybrid: 2.0L Atkinson LFB-M1 two-motor e:HEV (204 hp).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 2015–2016: CVT (replaced the 5AT with the facelift). 2017–2022: CVT (1.5T); hybrid: clutch-type e-CVT. 2023–2026: retuned CVT w/ Step-Shift (1.5T); hybrid e-CVT.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 181 lb-ft @ 3900 (K24W9); 179 lb-ft @ 2000–5000 (L15BE/L15BX — oil-spec sheet cross-check: autofiles lists 190 hp/179 lb-ft, 3.7 qt 0W-20); hybrid 247 lb-ft (traction motor, 2023+).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD standard, Real Time AWD optional all gens (1.5T). 2020–2022 Hybrid: AWD standard (only config). 2023+ Hybrid: FWD standard on Sport, AWD optional/standard on Sport-L/Sport Touring/TrailSport.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: 4th gen (2015–16): very strong — no pattern powertrain recalls; main complaint category is idle vibration (below). 5th gen: mechanically durable but the L15BE oil-dilution problem is one of Honda's most documented modern issues — class action filed, warranty extended, product-update TSBs issued for 21 cold-weather states (WardsAuto). 6th gen: strong early record; IIHS Top Safety Pick. Recall exposure on 2020–2021 builds: seat weight sensor (airbag) and Denso fuel pump.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: 2015–2016: engine vibration/rough idle at stop in Drive (first-year CVT+DI calibration) — Honda product update/TSB addressed motor mounts, radiator mount, and PCM calibration; CarComplaints shows "vibration" as the dominant 2015 category. 2017–2019 (5th gen) 1.5T: fuel dilution of engine oil (rising dipstick level, fuel smell, misfires in extreme cases) — Honda product update (ECU + HVAC recalibration, faster warm-up) plus warranty extension and class action; advise cold-climate/short-trip customers on 3,000–5,000 mi oil intervals. Also: Denso low-pressure fuel-pump recall (2019–2020 builds); A/C failures reported at lower rates than Civic. 6th gen: no pattern failures documented as of research date.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Maintenance Minder; 0W-20 (3.7 qt w/ filter on L15BE — verified via oil-spec database); CVT fluid HCF-2 (MM sub-code 3); coolant Type 2 (6.6 qt, first change 120k/10 yr); iridium plugs ~105k; brake fluid 3 yr. ALL engines in scope are timing CHAIN — no belt service. Rear diff fluid (AWD models): Honda DPSF-II every ~30k (severe) per MM sub-code 6.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 1.5T ≈7.5–7.8 s; 2023+ hybrid ≈7.5–8.0 s (publication estimates — not re-verified this session; confirm before print). Sport Touring Hybrid is the quickest variant (highest output). No factory performance trim; 2026 TrailSport Hybrid is an appearance/light-off-road package.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Light compared with Civic: Hondata/KTuner support for the 1.5T (intake/downpipe/tune ~220+ hp), all-terrain tires, mild lift/level kits, roof systems. Hybrid models see essentially no tuning. Most shop work is maintenance + the dilution-related services above.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The 2015 facelift swapped in the direct-injected K24W9 + CVT — and its first-year calibration caused the vibration issue. The 5th gen (2017) moved to the 1.5T/CVT on the Civic-based platform with an available i-MMD hybrid from 2020. The 6th gen (2023) shares architecture with the 11th-gen Civic: longer wheelbase (+1.6 in), wider track, stiffer body, and a redesigned Real Time AWD system with more rear torque capacity; hybrid becomes the volume powertrain.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Not an enthusiast vehicle, but CR-V Owners Club forums are highly active on the oil-dilution topic — owners arrive educated, so techs should know the TSB/product-update history cold. 1st/2nd-gen CR-Vs have a mild cult following; modern ones are valued for practicality and resale.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota RAV4 (incl. hybrid/prime), Mazda CX-5/CX-50, Subaru Forester, Nissan Rogue, Hyundai Tucson. CR-V matches RAV4 at the top of the segment on space, resale, and hybrid efficiency; CX-5/CX-50 drive better; RAV4 offers more hybrid powertrain choices. On reliability, avoid painting with a broad brush: 4th gen excellent, early 5th gen 1.5T is the documented weak point.

honda-pilot.subtitle: Midsize 3-row SUV — 2015 (2nd gen, YF), 2016–2022 (3rd gen, YF5/YF6), 2023–2026 (4th gen)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015 (2nd gen, final year): 3.5L J35Z4 SOHC V6, 250 hp — older figures; verify against OEM spec sheet before print. 2016–2022: 3.5L J35Y6 SOHC DI V6 w/ VCM, 280 hp. 2023–2026: all-new 3.5L J35Y8 DOHC DI V6, 285 hp.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 2015: 5-speed automatic. 2016–2022: Honda 6-speed automatic (LX/EX/EX-L); ZF 9HP 9-speed automatic (Touring/Elite 2016+, spreading to more trims 2019+, standard range-wide by 2021 per fueleconomy S9 listing). 2023–2026: Honda 10-speed automatic, all trims.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 280 hp @ 6000 (J35Y6); 285 hp @ 6100 (J35Y8 — Honda Canada press + spec aggregators). 2015 J35Z4: 250 hp (needs OEM confirmation).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD standard; i-VTM4 torque-vectoring AWD optional (standard on Elite/Black Edition; 2022+ TrailSport AWD-only; 4th-gen TrailSport/Elite AWD standard).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: EPA 2021 (9AT): FWD 20/27/23; AWD 19/26/22. EPA 2023 (10AT): FWD 19/27/22; AWD 19/25/21; TrailSport 18/23/20. 2015 (2nd gen): 17/24/20 est. — needs OEM source (fueleconomy.gov page not retrievable this session).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: 3rd gen: 21 NHTSA recall campaigns across 2016–2022 (The Weekly Driver) — high count, but mostly non-powertrain (fuel pump, brake booster fastener, seat weight sensor, hood latch). IIHS top marks per Driving.ca used-car guide. The J35Y6 engine itself is durable; the ZF 9HP is the weak link (below). 4th gen rated "best overall" generation by aggregators; the switch to Honda's own 10AT removed the 9-speed complaint stream.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: 3rd gen ZF 9HP: harsh/erratic shifting, hesitation, premature wear — widely documented ("troublesome 9-speed," "clunky 9-speed" in ranked guides); check for software updates and fluid service history. Also documented: 2019+ hood-latch recall (hood can open at speed); Denso fuel-pump recall; brake-booster tie-rod fastener recall (2021–2022); infotainment/rear-camera glitches; VCM-related plug fouling at high miles. Timing belt service is a known big-ticket maintenance item (belt-driven J35, see below). 4th gen: no pattern failures documented as of research date.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Maintenance Minder; 0W-20; 6AT fluid DW-1; 9AT/10AT: Honda ATF Type 3.1 (verify by VIN — ZF unit has specific fill procedure); coolant Type 2; iridium plugs ~105k; brake fluid 3 yr. **J35 V6 = timing BELT: 105k mi/7 yr with water pump, tensioner, idlers** (1A Auto service documentation for 2016–2022). i-VTM4 rear diff fluid every ~15k–30k (MM sub-code 6) — critical on AWD.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 2022 (3rd gen) ≈7.0 s (Edmunds, via MotorBiscuit); 2023 (4th gen) 6.9 s (TopSpeed). Tow rating: 3,500 lb FWD / 5,000 lb AWD (Honda specs via TopSpeed/GuideAuto). TrailSport (2022+, expanded 2023+) adds off-road suspension, all-terrain tires, steel skid plates, and 1-in lift (4th gen).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Modest scene: Stage-1 ECU tunes exist for the J35Y6 (AMT Tuning etc.), plus A/T tires, leveling kits, and overland-style accessories on TrailSport. No meaningful forced-induction market. Factory performance variant: none — TrailSport is the halo.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The 3rd gen dropped ~300 lb vs the 2nd gen and introduced direct injection on the J35Y6 plus the ZF-sourced 9HP on upper trims — a cost/efficiency move that became the generation's reliability sore spot. The 4th gen (2023) moves to a new platform shared with the Acura MDX: longer wheelbase (+2.8 in), a brand-new DOHC J35Y8 (first DOHC J-series in a US Honda SUV), Honda's own 10AT, and second-gen i-VTM4 with 40% more rear torque capacity and faster response. Removable second-row middle seat (8↔7 seats) is a packaging first for the segment.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Not an enthusiast machine; Piloteers.org is the knowledge hub, dominated by 9HP shifting threads and timing-belt DIYs. TrailSport has created a small but growing light-off-road community. Owners are maintenance-loyal — good long-term service customers.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Kia Telluride, Hyundai Palisade, Toyota Highlander/Grand Highlander, Ford Explorer, Subaru Ascent. Pilot's naturally aspirated V6 + proven Honda 10AT (2023+) is a durability argument against turbo rivals; Telluride/Palisade win on interior flash/value; Grand Highlander offers hybrid + Turbo MAX powertrains the Pilot can't match. On used lots, 2019–2022 9-speed cars should be priced/sold with the transmission history in mind.

honda-odyssey.subtitle: Minivan — 2015–2017 (4th gen, RL5), 2018–2025 (5th gen, RL6); 2026 status not confirmed at research date

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2017: 3.5L J35Z8 SOHC V6 w/ VCM (port + updated i-VTEC), 248 hp. 2018–2025: 3.5L J35Y7 SOHC DI V6 w/ VCM, 280 hp. No hybrid or alternative powertrain offered in the US.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 2015–2017: Honda 6-speed automatic, all trims. 2018–2019: ZF 9HP 9-speed (LX/EX/EX-L) or Honda 10-speed automatic (Touring/Elite); 2020–2025: Honda 10AT standard range-wide (EPA lists S10 only from 2020).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD only, all years — no AWD or 4WD ever offered (key differentiator vs Toyota Sienna).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: EPA 2018: 19/28/22 for BOTH 9AT and 10AT. EPA 2023: 19/28/22 (10AT). EPA 2015–2017: 19/28/22 per secondary compilations — fueleconomy.gov model page not retrievable this session; flag for re-verification.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: 4th gen: solid — J35Z8/6AT is a known quantity; main caveats are VCM-related wear items (below). 5th gen: the heaviest complaint load of any current mainstream minivan per The Weekly Driver's NHTSA analysis — 2,486 complaints (2018–2025), 24 recall campaigns, complaints-per-year ~5× segment average; note the complaints concentrate in transmission behavior, doors, and electronics, NOT the J35Y7 block itself.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: 5th gen: ZF 9HP harsh/erratic shifting (same family as Pilot; software updates + fluid service); 10AT harsh/hesitant 1–2 and 2–3 shifts (software updates available); power sliding-door failures (cables/latches/motors — a signature RL6 complaint); infotainment/Display Audio glitches; second-row seat-latch recall (2018–2020 — verify by VIN); connecting-rod bearing campaign noted in The Weekly Driver's reliability file (get specifics from NHTSA by VIN); Denso fuel-pump recall. 4th gen: VCM plug fouling/oil consumption at high mileage (J-series VCM pattern; Soto v. Honda class-action powertrain warranty extension covered 2011–2012 — later years' applicability varies, verify by VIN); torque-converter judder product update on earlier 4th-gen builds; sliding-door and motor-mount wear.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Maintenance Minder; 0W-20 (J35, ~5.7 qt w/ filter — verify by VIN); 6AT fluid DW-1; 9AT/10AT Honda ATF Type 3.1 — **transmission fluid changes are the single most valuable preventive service on 2018+ Odysseys given the shift-quality history**; coolant Type 2; iridium plugs ~105k; brake fluid 3 yr. **J35 V6 = timing BELT: 105k mi/7 yr with water pump** — quote this proactively; it is the major service on every Odyssey.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 ≈6.6–7.0 s for the 5th gen 10AT (publication instrumented tests — not re-verified this session, confirm before print); 4th gen ≈7.9–8.2 s est. Tow rating 3,000–3,500 lb depending on year/equipment (verify by VIN). No performance trims.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Minimal tuning scene (J35 ECU tunes exist but rare); typical "mods" are practical: hitches, roof systems, all-season/all-weather tires, cabin tech. No factory performance variant — Elite/Black Edition are luxury trims.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The 5th gen (2018) moved to a new global light-truck platform shared with Pilot/Ridgeline: direct-injected J35Y7, Honda's first in-house 10AT (upper trims), Magic Slide second-row seats, and CabinWatch/CabinTalk tech. Structure is stiffer and quieter than RL5; the drivetrain strategy (one V6, FWD only) kept weight and complexity down but left Honda without an answer to the Sienna hybrid/Pacifica PHEV on efficiency.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Not an enthusiast vehicle; Odyclub.com is the community hub and is dominated by transmission and sliding-door threads — owners arrive informed and frustrated, so documented TSB familiarity builds trust fast. The 4th gen is becoming a respected high-mileage used buy.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota Sienna (hybrid-only from 2021, available AWD), Chrysler Pacifica (PHEV option), Kia Carnival. Odyssey is the driver's choice of the segment (best steering/ride balance) with a proven V6, but it has no hybrid/AWD answer and carries the segment's heaviest documented complaint load (5th gen transmission/doors). Sienna wins on efficiency and resale; Pacifica on features-per-dollar.

hyundai-elantra.subtitle: Compact sedan, 2017–2020 (AD, 6th gen) and 2021–2026 (CN7, 7th gen; facelift for 2024). Elantra GT hatchback (2018–2020) is a related but separate body style.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: AD (2017–2020): 2.0L Nu MPI I4 (G4NH), 1.4L Kappa T-GDI I4 (G4LD, Eco), 1.6L Gamma T-GDI I4 (G4FJ, Sport). CN7 (2021–2026): 2.0L Smartstream G2.0 MPI I4; Hybrid 1.6L Smartstream GDI Atkinson I4 + electric motor; N Line 1.6L Gamma II T-GDI I4 (G4FP); Elantra N 2.0L Theta II T-GDI I4 (G4KH).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: AD: 6-speed manual (SE, Sport), 6-speed conventional automatic (internal code not published — needs OEM source), 7-speed EcoShift DCT D7UF1 (Eco, Sport DCT). CN7: IVT chain-type CVT (2.0), 6-speed EcoShift DCT (Hybrid), 7-speed DCT D7UF1 (N Line; 6MT offered 2021–2022), N: 6-speed manual or 8-speed wet-clutch DCT (D8LF1/D8F48W family) with N Grin Shift overboost.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: AD: 2.0 Nu 147 hp @ 6,200 rpm; 1.4T Eco 128 hp @ 5,500 rpm; 1.6T Sport 201 hp @ 6,000 rpm. CN7: 2.0 147 hp @ 6,200 rpm; Hybrid 139 hp combined; N Line 201 hp; N 276 hp (286 hp on NGS overboost, DCT only).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: AD: 2.0 132 lb-ft @ 4,500 rpm; 1.4T 156 lb-ft; 1.6T 195 lb-ft. CN7: 2.0 132 lb-ft @ 4,500 rpm; Hybrid 195 lb-ft combined system torque (approx. — verify per MY); N Line 195 lb-ft; N 289 lb-ft.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Front-wheel drive only, all generations and trims, including Elantra N. No AWD offered.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: AD (EPA): 2.0 auto 28/37/32 (Limited) to 29/38/33 (SE); 1.6T Sport 26/33/29; 1.4T Eco 32/40/35; 2020 2.0 IVT 31/41/35. CN7 (EPA): 2.0 IVT 33/43/37 (SE), 31/41/35 (SEL/Limited); Hybrid Blue 53/56/54, Hybrid Limited 49/52/51; N Line 28/36/31; N ≈21/31/25 (verify per MY/gearbox at fueleconomy.gov).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: AD: average-to-better reputation; IIHS Top Safety Pick on equipped trims; ~8 NHTSA recalls across 2017–2020, most notably the 2.0L Nu piston oil-ring recall. CN7: improved standing — 2024 Elantra named IIHS Top Safety Pick+; NHTSA recall count lower than AD; Consumer Reports rates recent Elantras around average for the class.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: AD: NHTSA recall 20V746000 — 2019–2020 Elantra (and Kona/Veloster) 2.0L Nu MPI piston oil rings not properly heat-treated, engine damage/stall risk (engine inspection/replacement). 2017 steering recall (damaged steering components). 7-speed D7UF1 DCT low-speed hesitation/judder complaints on Eco/Sport. Steering coupler (MDPS rubber coupler) clunk is a well-documented Hyundai wear item. Nu-family engines were also covered by an oil-consumption class settlement (2014–2020 Nu 2.0 GDI applications; G4NH MPI cars less affected). CN7: 2022 Elantra N included in the 8-speed DCT transmission oil-pump circuit-board recall (fail-safe limp mode; NHTSA 22V-000 series, ~120K vehicles Hyundai/Kia combined). Few other systemic CN7 engine issues documented to date.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 7,500 mi or 12 mo normal, 3,750 mi or 6 mo severe (Hyundai US schedule). All engines use timing chains — no belt service. Iridium spark plugs ~100,000 mi on NA engines (turbo GDI plug interval varies by MY — confirm in owner's manual). Long-life coolant: first replacement ~10 yr/120,000 mi. Automatic transmission fluid: no normal-service replacement; inspect/replace under severe usage (~60,000 mi for DCT/ATF per severe schedule). Brake fluid: inspect at each service; replace ~every 3 years recommended (confirm per MY manual).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 (publication tests, via zeroto60times aggregation): AD 2.0 Limited 8.6 s; AD Sport 1.6T 6.9 s; CN7 2.0 Limited 8.2 s; CN7 Hybrid 8.4 s; Elantra N 5.1 s DCT / 5.2 s MT (2022), 4.8 s DCT (2024). Performance trims: AD Sport (2017–2020), CN7 N Line (2021+), Elantra N (2022+) with eLSD (N Corner Carving Differential), variable exhaust, launch control.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Elantra N has strong tuner support: JB4 and flash tunes (Lap3, SXTH Element, Velossa Tech), downpipes, intakes, intercooler upgrades, oil catch cans (GDI valve coking prevention); common chassis mods are lowering springs, rear sway bars, and 18/19-in wheel/tire packages. Factory N Performance parts catalog exists in some markets. AD Sport/GT Sport share Gamma 1.6T tuning parts with Veloster/Kia Forte community.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: AD moved to a "Superstructure" body with ~53% advanced high-strength steel and structural adhesive for rigidity. CN7 rides on the third-gen K3 compact platform — lower, wider, longer wheelbase — and introduced the first Elantra Hybrid and the chain-driven IVT replacing the conventional 6AT on volume trims. Elantra N adds reinforced strut mounts, stiffer sway bars, integrated drive shaft/WTA-type knuckle geometry and multi-link rear suspension unique to N.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Elantra N is regarded by the community as one of the best front-drive sport sedans ever sold in the US — praised for rotation on corner entry, real mechanical handbrake and available 6MT; it is the cult trim. N Line is seen as a strong value "Si-fighter." Tuner ecosystem (SXTH, Lap3, N75, aFe) is mature.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda Civic, Toyota Corolla, Mazda3, VW Jetta. Versus Civic, Elantra counters with more feature content per dollar and longer warranty (5 yr/60K basic, 10 yr/100K powertrain), while Civic holds a resale/reliability edge; Elantra N undercuts Civic Type R by ~$10K with nearly comparable pace. Driving feel favors Mazda3/Civic slightly on base trims; N flips that verdict.

hyundai-sonata.subtitle: Midsize sedan, 2015–2019 (LF, 7th gen; facelift 2018) and 2020–2025/26 (DN8, 8th gen; facelift 2024). US lineup thinned for 2025–2026 (SEL/SEL Plus/N Line; hybrid continues).

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: LF (2015–2019): 2.4L Theta II GDI I4 (G4KJ); 2.0L Theta II T-GDI I4 (G4KH, Sport/Limited 2.0T); 1.6L Gamma T-GDI I4 (G4FJ, Eco, 2015–2019); Hybrid/PHEV 2.0L Nu GDI I4 + motor. DN8 (2020–2026): 2.5L Smartstream G2.5 GDI I4 (G4KN); 1.6L Smartstream T-GDI I4 (G4FP); N Line 2.5L Smartstream T-GDI I4 (G4KP); Hybrid 2.0L Smartstream GDI + motor.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: LF: 6-speed automatic (2.4, 2.0T 2015–2017; internal code not published — needs OEM source), 7-speed EcoShift DCT D7UF1 (Eco), 6-speed automatic (Hybrid); 8-speed automatic introduced on 2.0T for 2018. DN8: 8-speed conventional automatic (2.5, 1.6T), 8-speed wet-clutch DCT D8F48W family (N Line), 6-speed automatic with multi-plate clutch pack replacing the torque converter (Hybrid).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: LF: 2.4 185 hp @ 6,000 rpm; 2.0T 245 hp @ 6,000 rpm; 1.6T Eco 178 hp @ 5,500 rpm; Hybrid 193 hp combined. DN8: 2.5 191 hp @ 6,100 rpm; 1.6T 180 hp @ 5,500 rpm; N Line 290 hp @ 5,800 rpm; Hybrid 192 hp combined (150-hp engine + 51-hp motor).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Front-wheel drive only on LF and on DN8 through 2023; DN8 added optional HTRAC AWD on 2.5 SEL/SEL Plus trims for 2025 (US). No RWD/4WD.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: LF: L 191.1 in / W 73.4 in / H 58.1 in / WB 110.4 in; curb ≈3,192–3,600 lbs. DN8: L 192.9 in / W 73.2 in / H 56.9 in / WB 111.8 in; curb 3,120–3,336 lbs (gas, 2020 MY), Hybrid ≈3,325 lbs.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: LF (EPA): 2.4 25/37/29; 2.0T 22/31/26 (23/32 on some early MY — verify per year); 1.6T Eco 28/38/32; Hybrid 36/40/38 (2016), PHEV 38 combined gas / 93 MPGe. DN8 (EPA): 2.5 27/37/31 (SE 28/38/32); 1.6T 27/37/31; N Line 23/33/27; Hybrid Blue 50/54/52, Hybrid SEL/Limited 45/51/47.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: LF is the weak point of this nameplate's reliability history: it sits inside the Theta II GDI engine-defect era (class settlement covering 2011–2019 Sonata; $1.3B US settlement approved 2021 covering ~3.9M Hyundai/Kia owners). DN8 is materially better; 2024 Sonata earned IIHS Top Safety Pick (though IIHS rated the updated moderate-overlap rear-occupant test Poor). Consumer Reports scores DN8 around average.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: LF: Theta II GDI bearing/connecting-rod wear from crankshaft machining debris — engine knock, seizure, stall, and non-collision fires; recalls 15V568000 (2011–2012 Sonata), 17V226000 (2013–2014), plus the Theta II class-action settlement with 10-yr/120K-mi short-block warranty extension and KSDS knock-sensor software campaign. Nu GDI oil-consumption settlement also covers 2016–2019 Sonata Hybrid/PHEV (Nu 2.0 GDI). ABS/HECU internal brake-fluid leak → electrical short/fire risk recall covers 2011–2015 Sonata Hybrid (NHTSA 23V651000 family). D7UF1 DCT hesitation on Eco. Panoramic sunroof shattering complaints (NHTSA-investigated across 2011–2016 Hyundai/Kia). DN8: 2021–2022 Sonata (N Line) included in 8-speed DCT oil-pump circuit-board recall (fail-safe shutdown); Smartstream G2.5 piston-ring oil consumption documented on sibling applications (Tucson 2022–early 2023) — monitor oil level.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 7,500 mi / 12 mo normal, 3,750 mi / 6 mo severe — shops should steer Theta II GDI and turbo cars to the severe interval. All engines timing-chain (no belt). Iridium plugs ~100,000 mi NA; GDI turbo interval shorter on some MYs — confirm per manual. Coolant first change ~10 yr/120,000 mi. ATF: no normal-service interval; replace under severe usage (~60,000 mi); DCT gear/clutch fluid service per severe schedule. Brake fluid inspect; ~3-yr replacement recommended.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 (publication tests): LF 2.4 7.4–8.1 s; LF 2.0T 6.9–7.2 s; LF Eco 6.9 s; DN8 2.5 Limited 7.3 s; DN8 1.6T 7.1–7.8 s; DN8 N Line 5.1–5.2 s, quarter mile 13.3–13.7 s. Performance trim: N Line (2021+) is the de facto sport model — 290 hp was the most powerful front-drive midsize sedan in class at launch.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: N Line gets the same Smartstream 2.5T tuner ecosystem as K5 GT: JB4 piggybacks, flash tunes, downpipes, intakes, catch cans; typical chassis mods are lowering springs, strut bars, and 19-in wheel packages. LF 2.0T (G4KH) shares tuning parts with Veloster N/Kia Optima SX community (intakes, intercoolers, tunes to ~280–300 hp). No factory performance sub-brand beyond N Line.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: LF introduced a stiffer unibody (~51% AHSS) and replaced the old 3-link rear with a new 4-link multilink suspension; the 2018 facelift added the 8-speed auto to 2.0T. DN8 moved to the N3 platform with a lower, wider stance, "Sensuous Sportiness" design, hidden daytime-running-light strips, digital key, and (hybrid) a solar-roof charging option. The hybrid's 6AT replaces the torque converter with an oil-bath multi-plate clutch and adds Active Shift Control for faster, smoother shifts.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The enthusiast community largely ignores LF except as a discount used buy with a warranty-extended engine. N Line has a modest but real following as a "sleeper" family sedan; tuner support is shared with the K5 GT and is growing, though far smaller than the Elantra N scene.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota Camry, Honda Accord, Nissan Altima, Kia K5. Camry/Accord beat Sonata on long-term reliability reputation and resale; Sonata counters with warranty length, feature content, and (N Line) class-leading power for the money. Identical-mechanicals K5 is the cross-shop; Altima matches on value but trails on powertrain polish (CVT).

hyundai-tucson.subtitle: Compact SUV, 2016–2021 (TL, 3rd gen; facelift 2019) and 2022–2026 (NX4, 4th gen; facelift 2025).

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: TL (2016–2021): 2.0L Nu GDI I4 (G4NC) base; 1.6L Gamma T-GDI I4 (G4FJ) 2016–2018 (Eco/Sport/Limited); 2.4L Theta II GDI I4 (G4KJ) replaced the 1.6T on SEL/Sport/Limited for 2019–2021. NX4 (2022–2026): 2.5L Smartstream G2.5 GDI I4 (G4KN); Hybrid 1.6L T-GDI + 44.2-kW motor; Plug-in Hybrid 1.6L T-GDI + 66.9-kW motor, 13.8-kWh battery.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: TL: 6-speed automatic (2.0, 2.4); 7-speed EcoShift DCT D7UF1 (1.6T). NX4: 8-speed automatic (2.5); 6-speed automatic with clutch-in-lieu-of-converter (Hybrid/PHEV). No manual in US.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: TL: 2.0 164 hp @ 6,200 rpm; 1.6T 175 hp @ 5,500 rpm; 2.4 181 hp. NX4: 2.5 187 hp @ 6,100 rpm; Hybrid 226 hp combined (231 hp from 2025 facelift); PHEV 261 hp combined (268 hp from 2025).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: TL: 2.0 151 lb-ft @ 4,000 rpm; 1.6T 195 lb-ft @ 1,500–4,500 rpm; 2.4 175 lb-ft. NX4: 2.5 178 lb-ft @ 4,000 rpm; Hybrid/PHEV 258 lb-ft combined.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: TL: FWD standard, AWD optional on all trims (1.6T largely AWD-paired on top trims). NX4: FWD standard on gas (AWD optional, ~$1,500); HTRAC AWD standard on Hybrid and PHEV.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: TL: L 176.2 in / W 72.8 in / H 64.8 in / WB 105.1 in; curb ≈3,300–3,800 lbs. NX4: L 182.3 in (182.7 in 2025+) / W 73.4 in / H 65.6 in / WB 108.5 in; curb ≈3,307–3,906 lbs (Hybrid Limited ≈3,906 lbs).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: TL (EPA, fueleconomy.gov 2016): 2.0 FWD 23/30/26, AWD 21/26/23; 1.6T FWD 25/30/27, AWD 24/28/25; 1.6T Eco FWD 26/32/28; 2.4 (2019+) ≈22/28/25 FWD (verify per MY). NX4 (EPA): 2.5 FWD 26/33/29 (2025: 25/33/28), AWD 24/29/26; Hybrid Blue 38/38/38; Hybrid SEL/Limited ≈37/36/37 (2025 Limited 37/36); PHEV ≈35 combined gas, ~80 MPGe, ~33 mi EV range.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: TL is a mixed bag: 2015–2019 cluster shows 3 NHTSA recalls and 1,000+ complaints logged (NHTSA data via MECH AI aggregation), driven mainly by Nu GDI oil consumption and DCT issues; 2020–2024 cluster shows 3 recalls / fewer complaints. NX4 improved: 2024 Tucson named IIHS Top Safety Pick+; Consumer Reports rates it around average, hybrids slightly better regarded than base gas.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: TL: Nu 2.0 GDI oil consumption/piston-ring wear — included in the Nu/Gamma GDI class settlement (2014–2021 Tucson Nu 2.0 GDI) with lifetime engine warranty for qualifying failures; D7UF1 7-speed DCT low-speed shudder/hesitation and clutch TSBs; ABS/HECU module electrical-short fire-risk recalls (2019–2021 Tucson recalled for ABS module, 2021; earlier TLs in broader HECU campaigns — verify by VIN at nhtsa.gov/recalls). NX4: excessive oil consumption on 2.5 GDI built 2022–early 2023 (faulty piston rings; Hyundai revised ring design mid-2023 without a formal recall — monitor oil); 8-speed transmission ISG oil-pump circuit-board fire-risk recall (2023); Mobis tow-hitch wiring-harness water-intrusion recall (2022–2024, NHTSA 25V893000); unintended AEB/FCA activation complaints.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 7,500 mi / 12 mo normal, 3,750 mi / 6 mo severe — use severe interval on all Nu/Theta/Smartstream GDI and turbo engines. All engines timing-chain. Iridium plugs ~100,000 mi NA (turbo per MY manual). Coolant first change ~10 yr/120,000 mi. ATF no normal-service interval; severe usage ~60,000 mi (important on D7UF1 clutch fluid). AWD rear differential/transfer case fluid inspect/replace under severe schedule. Brake fluid ~3-yr replacement recommended.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 (publication tests): TL 1.6T ≈7.9–8.3 s; TL 2.0 ≈9 s (verify); NX4 2.5 gas ≈8.8–9.6 s; NX4 Hybrid Limited 7.1 s (Car and Driver, quickest tested Tucson); PHEV ≈7.2 s. No true factory performance trim; N Line (2022+) is appearance/suspension tuning only, not more power.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Light enthusiast activity: NX4 owners fit lowering springs, larger wheels (up to 20 in), roof racks/AT tires for soft-roading; the 1.6T hybrid responds to intakes and piggyback tunes but hybrid tuning is niche. TL 1.6T shares Gamma tuning parts with Veloster/Forte GT (intakes, tunes, intercooler). No meaningful factory performance variant exists.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: TL introduced the 7-speed EcoShift DCT and torque-vectoring "Active Cornering Control" AWD to the nameplate; the 2019 facelift swapped the 1.6T for the 2.4 Theta II on upper trims. NX4 rides on the N3 platform with a long-wheelbase US spec, "Parametric Dynamics" styling with grille-integrated LED DRLs, and Hyundai's first compact-SUV hybrid/PHEV powertrains; hybrids use a 6AT with clutch pack instead of a torque converter plus Active Shift Control.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The community views the Tucson as a value/practicality play rather than an enthusiast car; the NX4's styling and hybrid drivability get genuine praise. TL 1.6T is the sleeper pick. Tuner support exists only via shared Gamma/Smartstream ecosystems — no dedicated Tucson scene.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota RAV4, Honda CR-V, Mazda CX-5/CX-50, Kia Sportage. RAV4/CR-V hold the reliability-resale advantage; Tucson Hybrid counters the RAV4 Hybrid on power and refinement at a sharper price, and beats both on warranty and cabin tech. Sportage is the same hardware in different sheet metal; CX-5 out-drives the gas Tucson but lacks a hybrid option.

hyundai-santa-fe.subtitle: Midsize SUV, 2015–2018 (DM/NC, 3rd gen: Santa Fe Sport 5-seat + Santa Fe 3-row; 3-row carried over as 2019 Santa Fe XL), 2019–2023 (TM, 4th gen; facelift 2021), 2024–2026 (MX5, 5th gen).

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: DM Sport (2015–2018): 2.4L Theta II GDI I4 (G4KJ); 2.0L Theta II T-GDI I4 (G4KH). DM 3-row (2015–2019 XL): 3.3L Lambda II GDI V6 (G6DH). TM (2019–2020): 2.4L G4KJ and 2.0T G4KH; TM facelift (2021–2023): 2.5L Smartstream G2.5 (G4KN), 2.5L Smartstream T-GDI (G4KP), Hybrid/PHEV 1.6L T-GDI + motor. MX5 (2024–2026): 2.5L Theta III/Smartstream T-GDI I4 (G4KP family); Hybrid 1.6L Gamma III T-GDI + motor.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: DM: 6-speed automatic (all engines). TM: 8-speed automatic (2.4, 2.0T, 2.5); 8-speed wet-clutch DCT D8F48W family (2.5T, 2021–2023); 6-speed automatic (Hybrid/PHEV). MX5: 8-speed wet-clutch DCT D8F48W family (2.5T); 6-speed automatic (Hybrid).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: DM: Sport 2.4 190 hp @ 6,300 rpm; Sport 2.0T 264 hp @ 6,000 rpm (2015–2016), 240 hp @ 6,000 rpm (2017–2018); 3-row 3.3 V6 290 hp @ 6,400 rpm. TM: 2.4 185 hp @ 6,000 rpm; 2.0T 235 hp @ 6,000 rpm; 2.5 191 hp; 2.5T 277 hp @ 5,800 rpm; Hybrid 226 hp combined; PHEV 261 hp. MX5: 2.5T 277 hp @ 5,800 rpm; Hybrid 231 hp combined.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: All generations: FWD standard, AWD optional (HTRAC branding from TM onward). MX5 XRT adds 1.5 in lift/AT tires but remains on-demand AWD — no low-range 4WD on any Santa Fe.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: DM Sport: L 184.6 in / W 74.0 in / WB 106.3 in; curb ≈3,500–3,900 lbs. DM 3-row: L 193.1 in / W 74.2 in / WB 110.2 in; curb ≈3,900–4,300 lbs. TM: L 187.8 in / W 74.4 in / H 66.1–67.1 in / WB 108.9 in; curb ≈3,591–4,300 lbs. MX5: L 190.2 in / W 74.8 in / H 69.7 in / WB 110.8 in; curb 4,098–4,575 lbs (Hybrid SEL 4,431 lbs; Hybrid Limited 4,575 lbs).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: DM (EPA): Sport 2.4 FWD 20/27/23; Sport 2.0T FWD 19/27/22; 3-row V6 FWD 18/25/21. TM (EPA): 2.4 FWD 22/29/25; 2.0T 20/25/22; 2.5 FWD 25/28/26; 2.5T 22/28/25; Hybrid Blue 36/31/34; PHEV ~76 MPGe, ~30 mi EV. MX5 (EPA, fueleconomy.gov 2024): 2.5T FWD 20/29/24, AWD 20/28/23, AWD XRT 19/26/22; Hybrid FWD 36/35/36, Hybrid AWD 35/34/34.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: DM/TM carry the Theta II stigma: the US/Canada class settlements explicitly cover 2013–2019 Santa Fe Sport 2.4/2.0T; the 3.3L Lambda V6 is the robust pick of the DM era. 2013–2015 Santa Fe (and 2013 Santa Fe Sport) are in the ABS-module internal-leak fire-risk recall (NHTSA 23V651000 family). MX5: 2024 Santa Fe earned IIHS Top Safety Pick; too new for meaningful long-term data — no systemic engine pattern documented yet.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: DM/TM (Theta II cars): connecting-rod bearing wear → knock/seizure/fire (class settlement, 10-yr/120K short-block warranty extension, KSDS software); ABS/HECU fire-risk recalls; steering MDPS coupler clunk; AWD rear coupler failures on higher-mileage units. TM facelift: 2021–2022 Santa Fe in the 8-speed DCT oil-pump circuit-board recall; Smartstream 2.5 GDI oil-consumption reports (piston rings, 2022–early 2023 sibling applications); tow-hitch harness water-intrusion recall. MX5: early-build infotainment/software glitches; no systemic powertrain defect documented as of mid-2026 — monitor.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 7,500 mi / 12 mo normal, 3,750 mi / 6 mo severe — severe interval strongly advised on all Theta II/Smartstream GDI and turbo engines. All engines timing-chain (incl. 3.3 V6). Iridium plugs ~100,000 mi NA; turbo GDI per MY manual. Coolant first change ~10 yr/120,000 mi. ATF/DCT fluid: no normal-service interval, replace under severe usage (~60,000 mi). AWD transfer case/rear differential: inspect, replace severe. Brake fluid ~3-yr replacement recommended.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 (publication tests): DM 3-row V6 7.2–7.4 s; DM Sport 2.0T 7.9–8.8 s; TM 2.0T 7.6 s; TM 2.4 8.7 s; TM 2.5T Calligraphy AWD 6.1 s (2021); TM 2.5 XRT 9.6 s; MX5 2.5T Calligraphy AWD 6.2 s; MX5 Hybrid ≈9.8 s in one published aggregation test (verify — needs a second source). No factory performance trim; XRT is an appearance/lift package.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Minimal enthusiast modification scene — typical owner mods are cosmetic/utility: wheels and AT tires (XRT-style), roof platforms, towing accessories (MX5 tow rating up to 4,500 lbs with package on XRT; 3,500 lbs braked on TM). The 2.5T (G4KP) responds to the same JB4/flash-tune ecosystem as Sonata N Line/K5 GT for those inclined. No cult performance variant.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The 2019 TM replaced both the DM Sport and the 3-row, moving to a new platform with HTRAC AWD and an 8-speed automatic; the 2021 facelift was effectively a re-engineering — new N3-derived platform, Smartstream G2.5/G2.5T, first Santa Fe Hybrid/PHEV. MX5 (2024) is a boxy, Land Rover-tinged redesign on a stretched N3 architecture (WB +1.9 in), standard 3-row seating in the US, full-width tailgate opening, and dual 12.3-in curved display; HTRAC adds terrain modes.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The community sees the Santa Fe as a family appliance; respect goes to the 2.5T's stout midrange and the MX5's packaging. No cult trims or tuner culture — the 3.3 V6 DM is the "mechanic's choice" used buy because it dodges the Theta II problem.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota Highlander, Honda Passport/Pilot, Ford Edge, Kia Sorento (corporate twin). Sorento is the same hardware with different styling and typically a price edge; Highlander/Pilot beat Santa Fe on reliability reputation and resale, while Santa Fe counters on warranty, feature content, and hybrid fuel economy. Driving feel is competent-but-isolated across all generations; Mazda CX-9 out-drives it.

jeep-wrangler.subtitle: Body-on-frame 4x4 SUV, 2-door and 4-door (Unlimited): 2015–2018 (JK, third gen), 2018–2026 (JL, fourth gen, incl. 2024 refresh; 4xe PHEV 2021–2025; EcoDiesel 2020–2023; Rubicon 392 2021–2025)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: JK (2015–2018): 3.6L Pentastar (ERB) DOHC V6, naturally aspirated. JL (2018–2026): 3.6L Pentastar (ERB) V6 NA (optional eTorque 48V mild-hybrid on 2019–2020 automatics); 2.0L GME-T4 "Hurricane" turbo I4 (2018+); 3.0L EcoDiesel VM Motori L630 DOHC turbo-diesel V6 (2020–2023); 4xe PHEV — 2.0L GME-T4 turbo + 100 kW transmission-integrated motor + 33 kW e-motor, 17 kWh pack (2021–2025, discontinued in US after 2025); 6.4L (392) HEMI OHV V8 NA in Rubicon 392 (2021–2024, 2025 Final Edition).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: JK: 5-speed W5A580 (Mercedes NAG1-family) automatic or 6-speed NSG370 manual. JL: 6-speed Aisin AL6 manual (3.6 only); 8-speed TorqueFlite 850RE automatic (3.6 / 2.0T / 4xe); 8-speed ZF 8HP75 automatic (EcoDiesel and Rubicon 392).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 3.6 V6: 285 hp @ 6,400 rpm (JK and JL). 2.0T: 270 hp @ 5,250 rpm. EcoDiesel: 260 hp. 4xe: 375 hp combined. Rubicon 392: 470 hp @ 6,000 rpm.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 3.6 V6: 260 lb-ft @ 4,800 rpm. 2.0T: 295 lb-ft @ 3,000 rpm. EcoDiesel: 442–443 lb-ft. 4xe: 470 lb-ft combined. Rubicon 392: 470 lb-ft @ 4,300 rpm.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: 4WD standard across 2015–2026 US range. JK/JL Sport & Sahara: Command-Trac part-time (2.72:1 low); Selec-Trac full-time available on Sahara; Rubicon: Rock-Trac (4:1 low) with Tru-Lok electronic lockers front/rear and electronic sway-bar disconnect.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: JK 2-dr: WB 95.4 in, L 164.3 in, W 73.7 in, H 70.9 in (72.3 Rubicon); JK 4-dr: WB 116 in, L 184.9 in, W 73.9 in. JL 2-dr: WB 96.8 in, L 166.8 in; JL 4-dr: WB 118.4 in, L 188.4 in; W 73.9 in, H ~70.9 in. Curb weight JL: 3,948–5,103 lb. JK curb: ≈3,900–4,400 lb (period spec-sheet figures vary by trim/top — verify per VIN for quotes).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: EPA (fueleconomy.gov) — JK 2015 3.6: 2-dr 17/21/18 (auto and manual); 4-dr 16/21/18 manual, 16/20/18 auto. JL 3.6 auto (2021): 2-dr 18–20/23–24/20–21, 4-dr 19/24/21; JL 3.6 manual (2025): 2-dr 17/23/19, 4-dr 16/22/19. JL 2.0T (2021): 2-dr 22/24/23, 4-dr 21/24/22 (2025: 2-dr 20/23/21, 4-dr 20/22/21). EcoDiesel 4-dr (2021): 22/29/25 (Rubicon 21/26/23). 4xe: 49 MPGe combined, 20 MPG gas-only, 21–22 mi EV range. Rubicon 392: 13/17/14 (C&D 75-mph highway test returned 13 mpg).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Below-average predicted reliability per Consumer Reports historically; JK considered the more durable of the two late generations (2012–2018 3.6 cars recommended by used-car guides). JL improved after 2020; 2018–2019 early-build cars carried weld-defect recalls (frame, front track-bar bracket, steering shaft). IIHS: JL rated Marginal in driver-side small overlap after the test vehicle rolled onto its side (2020 and again in 2022 retest); moderate overlap Good (original test). High NHTSA complaint volumes on JL electrical, steering, and engine categories.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: JK: "death wobble" front-end shimmy (worn track bar/ball joints/steering), TIPM electrical failures (fuel pump, wipers, locks), 3.6 oil filter housing/cooler adapter leaks (plastic housing; aluminum upgrades common), early Pentastar cylinder-head/valvetrain tick (mainly 2012–2013). JL: 2018–2019 frame-weld and track-bar bracket weld recalls; steering wander/death wobble TSBs (steering gear, stabilizer); 3.6 oil filter housing leak persists; ESS dual-battery/electrical gremlins; Uconnect failures; 4xe high-voltage battery fire risk — three NHTSA recalls (24V720: 154,032 units; 25V741: 320,065 units, Oct 2025, following 19 Samsung-cell fires) with park-outside/do-not-charge guidance; 4xe also reports of sudden power loss. EcoDiesel: CP4 high-pressure fuel pump failure risk (see Grand Cherokee recall note; same Bosch pump family).

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per oil-life monitor, up to 10,000 mi/12 mo (severe duty 3,500–5,000 mi); 0W-20 (3.6/2.0T), 0W-40 (392), 5W-40 (EcoDiesel). Spark plugs 3.6: 100,000 mi (then every 50k); 2.0T plugs: shorter interval — verify against owner's manual. Coolant: OAT, 10 yr/150,000 mi. Transmission: "fill for life" under normal duty; severe/off-road/towing service fluid & filter ~60k (ZF and shop practice). All engines timing chain (no belt). Brake fluid every 2–3 years. Axle/transfer-case fluids inspect every 20k, change under severe duty. 4xe adds battery coolant and charging-system checks.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: JL 2.0T ≈6.1–7.0 sec (Road Test Reviews instrumented 6.1 on a 2018 Rubicon 2-dr; TFL ~7.0); JL 3.6 ≈6.8–7.5; 4xe ≈5.5–6.0; Rubicon 392: 4.0 sec (Car and Driver instrumented, 2021; Jeep claims 4.5) with ~12s quarter-mile. JK 3.6: Not verified — needs OEM source (period tests ~8 sec). Rubicon 392 EPA 13/17/14.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: The largest aftermarket of any US SUV: lift kits (2.5–4.5"), 33–37" tires, regear packages (4.56/4.88/5.13), Dana 44/60 axle upgrades, bumpers/armor, winches, LED lighting, snorkels. Engine mods: cold-air intakes, cat-back exhausts, DiabloSport/JET tuners (JK 3.6), Sprintex/Edelbrock supercharger kits for JK/JL 3.6 (~300+ whp), 392 exhaust/tune work. Factory halo variants: Rubicon, Rubicon X, Willys, Moab, High Tide, Rubicon 392 (incl. 20th Anniversary and Final Edition).

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: JK (2007–2018) kept classic body-on-frame/solid-axle layout and added the first factory 4-door; from 2012 the 3.6 Pentastar replaced the 3.8 pushrod V6 with 285 hp and a 5-speed automatic. JL (2018+) uses a revised lighter frame with aluminum doors/hood/fenders (~200 lb lighter), electrically assisted recirculating-ball steering, and an 8-speed automatic; it added turbo, diesel, PHEV, and V8 powertrains for the first time in Wrangler history. 2024 refresh brought the 12.3" Uconnect 5, full-float Dana 44 rear (Rubicon), 35" tire availability and factory support for 40s. All engines chain-driven, coil-on-plug; 2.0T uses a twin-scroll turbo and water-cooled intercooler.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Wrangler is the center of the Jeep universe — JKs are now the value/cult buy ("2016–2018 Rubicons offer 90% of new capability at ~50% cost"), while JL Rubicons and the 392 are the modern halo. Tuner support is unmatched: Mopar/JPP, AEV, MetalCloak, Rock Krawler, Teraflex all build complete programs. The 4xe is divisive off-road (great low-end electric torque) but its battery recalls have hurt its standing.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Direct rival is the Ford Bronco (2021+), with the Toyota 4Runner as the durability benchmark. Wrangler beats both off-road (solid axles, lockers, 4:1 case, removable top/doors) and holds best-in-class resale (J.D. Power/ALG awards), but trails the 4Runner badly on long-term reliability and the Bronco on on-road steering refinement. Owns a unique niche — nothing else is a convertible solid-axle 4x4.

jeep-grand-cherokee.subtitle: Mid-size unibody SUV: 2015–2021 (WK2, fourth gen; carried over as "Grand Cherokee WK" through 2022), 2021–2026 (WL, fifth gen — Grand Cherokee L 3-row from 2021, 2-row from 2022; 4xe 2022–2025)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: WK2: 3.6L Pentastar (ERB) V6 NA; 5.7L HEMI (EZH) OHV V8 with MDS cylinder deactivation; 6.4L (392) HEMI V8 (SRT); 6.2L supercharged HEMI "Hellcat" V8 (Trackhawk, 2018–2021); 3.0L EcoDiesel VM Motori A630 DOHC turbo-diesel V6 (2014–2019). WL: 3.6L Pentastar V6 (eTorque-equipped applications); 5.7L HEMI V8 (Grand Cherokee L only, 2021–2023); 4xe PHEV — 2.0L GME-T4 turbo + 100 kW transmission motor, 17.3 kWh pack (2022–2025); 2.0L Hurricane 4 EVO turbo added for 2026 (details limited — verify per VIN).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: WK2: 8-speed TorqueFlite 845RE (early V6), 850RE (V6 from ~2018); 8-speed ZF 8HP70 "TorqueFlite 8" (5.7, EcoDiesel, SRT, Trackhawk). WL: 8-speed TorqueFlite 850RE across the range.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 3.6: 290 hp (2011–2015) → 295 hp @ 6,400 rpm (2016+, first application of the upgraded Pentastar per WardsAuto); WL 3.6: 293 hp. 5.7: 360 hp (WK2) / 357 hp (WL). 6.4 SRT: 470 hp (2012–2014) → 475 hp (2015+). Trackhawk: 707 hp @ 6,000 rpm. EcoDiesel: 240 hp. WL 4xe: 375 hp combined.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 3.6: 260 lb-ft @ 4,000–4,800 rpm. 5.7: 390 lb-ft. 6.4 SRT: 470 lb-ft @ 4,300 rpm. Trackhawk: 645 lb-ft @ 4,800 rpm. EcoDiesel: 420 lb-ft. WL 4xe: 470 lb-ft combined.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: WK2: RWD standard on V6; 4WD — Quadra-Trac I (single-speed), Quadra-Trac II (2-speed, 2.72:1 low), Quadra-Drive II (adds rear ELSD); SRT/Trackhawk use full-time performance AWD transfer cases. WL: RWD standard (V6); Quadra-Trac I, Quadra-Trac II, Quadra-Drive II with ELSD and available Quadra-Lift air suspension; 4xe is 4WD-only.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: WK2: WB 114.8 in, L 189.8 in, W 76.3 in, H 69.3 in; curb 4,533 lb (V6 RWD) to ~5,264 lb (V8 4WD), Trackhawk ~5,356 lb. WL 2-row: WB 116.7 in, L 193.5 in; WL L 3-row: WB 121.7 in, L 204.9 in; W 77.9 in, H ~71.5 in; curb 4,513–5,558 lb.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Mixed generation-level record. WK2: mid-pack; the 3.6/8-speed drivetrain is well understood and durable with the oil-housing and valvetrain caveats below; SRT/Trackhawk robust but consumable-heavy. WK2 earned high owner-satisfaction; Trackhawk holds value strongly. WL: improved build quality; IIHS crash scores Good on the current structure. Both 4xe models carry the Samsung-cell HV battery recalls (see below). NHTSA complaint data clusters on WK2 electrical/powertrain and on WL/4xe electrical.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: WK2: monostable electronic shifter rollaway recall (2014–2015, part of a 1.1M-vehicle recall with 41 injuries reported; software fix) — a documented fatality driver; 3.6 oil filter housing/cooler leaks and rocker-arm/lifter tick; Quadra-Lift air suspension compressor and air-spring failures; transfer-case actuator and parking-pawl recalls; 2014–2015 included in the 1.4M-vehicle Uconnect remote-hack recall (Miller/Valasek Cherokee hack). EcoDiesel: EPA/DOJ emissions-cheating consent decree (~$300M penalties, approved emissions modification recall), EGR cooler/soot and oil-cooler failures, bottom-end bearing failures, crankshaft position sensor recall, and Bosch CP4 high-pressure fuel pump self-destruction — 2022 recall of 138,645 (2014–2020 Grand Cherokee, 2014–2019 Ram 1500). WL/4xe: HV battery fire recalls 24V720 (154,032 units) and 25V741 (320,065 units combined Wrangler/GC 4xe, Oct 2025, park-outside order); early Uconnect 5/software glitches.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per oil-life monitor, up to 10,000 mi/12 mo; 0W-20 (3.6), 5W-20 (5.7), 0W-40 (6.4/6.2 SC), 5W-40 (EcoDiesel, plus DEF service and diesel fuel filter intervals). Spark plugs 3.6/5.7: 100,000 mi (then every 50k); 392/Trackhawk ~60k — verify per manual. Coolant: OAT 10 yr/150,000 mi. ZF 8HP70/TorqueFlite: "fill for life" normal duty; ZF and shop practice fluid + pan/filter ~60–80k, sooner for towing/track use. All engines timing chain. Brake fluid every 2–3 years; SRT/Trackhawk need frequent fluid and pad/rotor service. Transfer case and axle fluids under severe-duty schedule. EcoDiesel adds EGR/DPF monitoring.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 3.6 ≈7.5–8.2 (period tests; not freshly verified — treat as approximate); 5.7 ≈6.5–7.0 (approximate); SRT 4.4–4.8 (KBB cites 4.8 for 2018); Trackhawk 3.5 sec with 11.6-sec quarter-mile (multiple instrumented tests; fastest SUV of its era). WL 3.6: Not verified — needs OEM source. WL 4xe: Not verified — needs OEM source (press estimates mid-5s). WK2 towing: up to 6,200 lb (3.6), 7,200 lb (5.7), 7,200 lb (Trackhawk).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: WK2 has deep Mopar/aftermarket support: DiabloSport inTune/Trinity tuners and PCM swaps (5.7/6.4), cold-air intakes (aFe etc.), long-tube headers and cat-backs for HEMIs, Trackhawk pulley/tune packages pushing 800+ hp, lowering springs and 22" wheels for SRT/Trackhawk; overland-style lifts (~1.5–2"), skid plates and A/T tires on Trailhawk trims (Eibach Pro-Truck kits now exist for WL). Factory performance variants: SRT (2012–2021), Trackhawk (2018–2021), plus Trailhawk off-road trim (2017–2021 WK2; WL Trailhawk 4xe 2022+).

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: WK2 (2011–2022) rides on a Chrysler-developed unibody platform later adopted by the Mercedes-Benz W166 ML/GLE — independent suspension all around, optional Quadra-Lift air springs, and the first Grand Cherokee diesel and 707-hp supercharged variants; 2014 brought the 8-speed and EcoDiesel, 2016 the upgraded 295-hp Pentastar, 2017–2018 the Trailhawk and Trackhawk. WL (2021+) moves to the Giorgio-derived architecture shared with Alfa Romeo Stelvio/Maserati Grecale, adds a 3-row L body, Uconnect 5 with passenger screen, available McIntosh audio, and the 4xe PHEV; the V8 was phased out of the 2-row after 2023.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Trackhawk is a modern cult classic — "the Hellcat you can daily and tow with" — and clean examples command strong money. SRT WK2s are the budget super-SUV. Off-roaders like the WK2/WL Trailhawks for Quadra-Drive II, but hard-core builders stay with Wranglers; tuning culture centers on HEMI power adders rather than rock crawling.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Ford Explorer, Toyota 4Runner/Highlander, Dodge Durango (corporate cousin), BMW X5, Land Rover Defender. Grand Cherokee undercuts the Germans on price with comparable powertrains and beats Explorer/Highlander on capability and V8 availability, but its reliability record (especially EcoDiesel and air suspension) trails Toyota, and WL interior quality, while improved, still meets mixed reviews against the X5/Defender.

jeep-cherokee.subtitle: Compact/mid-size unibody crossover: 2015–2018 (KL, pre-facelift), 2019–2023 (KL facelift; US production ended Feb 28, 2023). Nameplate returns as the 2026 Cherokee (KM) hybrid — all-new model, not covered here; verify separately.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2.4L Tigershark (ED6) MultiAir2 SOHC I4 NA (2014–2021; 2023 Altitude Lux); 3.2L Pentastar (EHB) DOHC V6 NA (2015–2022; standard 2022); 2.0L GME-T4 "Hurricane" turbo I4 (2019+; 2023 Trailhawk). All transverse-mounted; all timing chain.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 9-speed ZF 948TE (Chrysler-built ZF 9HP48) automatic on every US-model Cherokee; no manual offered in the US. FWD or 4WD.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 2.4 Tigershark: 184 hp (171 lb-ft) — 180 hp quoted on some 2019+ materials; 3.2 V6: 271 hp; 2.0T: 270 hp @ 5,200 rpm.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD standard; 4WD via three systems — Active Drive I (single-speed PTU with rear-axle disconnect), Active Drive II (two-speed PTU, 2.92:1 low range), Active Drive Lock (Trailhawk; adds mechanical locking rear differential). Selec-Terrain modes on 4WD models.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: WB 106.3 in, L 182.0 in, W 73.2 in (74.9 in Trailhawk), H 66.2–67.8 in; curb 3,655–4,046 lb.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: One of FCA's weakest reliability performers in early years: 2014–2015 models drew very high complaint counts (carcomplaints/safecar), almost all transmission-related; Consumer Reports' poor dependability review of the Cherokee and siblings was widely reported (FCA's chief quality officer departed shortly after). Improved markedly after 2016–2017 software and hardware revisions; 2019+ facelift cars are materially better. IIHS 2022: Good in all crash tests; headlights Acceptable/Marginal by trim. Named sources: Consumer Reports, NHTSA complaints via safecar.gov, Car and Driver's 9-speed coverage.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) ZF 948TE 9-speed — the model's defining defect: harsh/erratic shifts, hesitation, limp mode, unexpected shift-to-neutral; NHTSA recall 16V-529/S55 (2014–2015, TCM/harness crimps), ZF-side recall of ~505,000 units for a control sensor that could drop the trans into neutral, plus a long TSB chain (18-018-17, 21-008-17, multiple TCM flashes) and "snap ring" durability repairs; subject of class-action litigation. (2) 2.4L Tigershark oil consumption — Wood et al. v. FCA class action (final settlement approval Dec 2022; ~1.6M vehicles incl. 2014–2019 Cherokee built before July 2018); defective pistons/rings, oil-control PCV strategy; can stall engines. (3) PTU/rear-drive-module failures on Active Drive II/4WD cars ("service 4x4" warnings; PTU, rear diff, RDM replacement documented). (4) 2014–2015 Uconnect remote-hack recall (1.4M vehicles, Miller/Valasek demonstration). (5) 2019 recall (~86k) for software that could cause stalls.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per monitor, up to 10,000 mi/12 mo — on 2.4 Tigershark cars, shops should advise checking oil level monthly and shorter intervals (class-action cars burned a quart per ~1,000 mi in severe cases); 0W-20 (2.4/3.2), 5W-30 (2.0T). Spark plugs 100,000 mi (then every 50k). Coolant: OAT 10 yr/150,000 mi. 948TE fluid: "fill for life" normal duty, but fluid changes ~60k are cheap insurance given the unit's history; requires the ZF quick-learn procedure after service with a capable scan tool. PTU/RDM gear oil on 4WD models per severe-duty schedule. Brake fluid every 2–3 years. All engines timing chain.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 2.0T ≈7.5 sec (MotorTrend instrumented data via FastestLaps); 3.2 V6 ≈7.2–7.5 (period tests; approximate — not freshly verified); 2.4 ≈8.5–9.5 (approximate — not freshly verified). Best-in-class V6 tow rating at launch: 4,500 lb (2,000 lb on 2.4). No factory performance trim; Trailhawk is the capability flagship.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Modest scene: Trailhawk lift kits (~1.5–2", Rocky Road/MFC), 245–265 A/T tires, skid plates, roof racks, light bars. No meaningful engine tuning for the 948TE driveline; the 2.0T responds to intake/exhaust but the transmission limits hard use. Factory capability variants: Trailhawk (2014–2022), Trailhawk L/Elite, plus Overland luxury trim.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: First Jeep on the FCA Compact US Wide (CUSW) platform shared with the Chrysler 200 — transverse engines, unibody, and the industry's first 9-speed automatic in an SUV, launched weeks late because of transmission software. Segment-first rear-axle disconnect for 4WD fuel economy. The 2019 facelift restyled the polarizing "squint" front end, added the 2.0T, and cut ~150 lb via an aluminum hood/liftgate and composite tailgate. Production moved Toledo → Belvidere in 2017; ended February 2023 when Stellantis idled Belvidere.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Enthusiast respect is limited — XJ loyalists rejected the KL's crossover layout, and the 9-speed's reputation stuck. The Trailhawk has a genuine niche following as a capable, compact overlander with a real low range and locker. Tuner support is thin; most community effort goes into making Trailhawks trail-worthy, not fast.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota RAV4, Honda CR-V, Ford Escape, Subaru Forester/Outback. The Cherokee counters with real 4WD hardware (low range, locker, 4,500-lb tow) that none of them offer, but the RAV4/CR-V/Forester all beat it on reliability, resale, and interior packaging; the 2.4/9-speed powertrain also trails on refinement and fuel economy in real-world use.

jeep-compass.subtitle: Compact unibody crossover: 2015–2016 (MK49, first gen; final MKs sold into early 2017), 2017–2026 (MP/552, second gen; 2022 interior/tech facelift; 2.0T powertrain from 2023). Third gen (J4U) unveiled May 2025 but North American production doesn't start until late 2027 (2028 MY) — the MP remains the US-market Compass through at least 2026.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: MK (2015–2016): 2.0L GEMA "World" DOHC I4 NA (FWD Sport only); 2.4L GEMA "World" DOHC I4 NA. MP (2017–2026): 2.4L Tigershark (ED6) MultiAir2 SOHC I4 NA (2017–2022; 177 hp for 2022); 2.0L GME-T4 turbo I4 (2023+). All timing chain.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: MK: 5-speed Magna T355 manual; 6-speed Hyundai 6F24 (PowerTech) torque-converter automatic (from 2014); Jatco JF011E CVT2 — mandatory with Freedom Drive II (19:1 simulated low range). MP: 6-speed manual (early Sport), 6-speed Aisin TF-60SN automatic (FWD), 9-speed ZF 948TE automatic (4WD, 2017–2022); 8-speed Hyundai 8F30 automatic (2023+, standard 4WD).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: MK 2.0: 158 hp; MK 2.4: 172 hp. MP 2.4 Tigershark: 180 hp (177 hp for 2022); MP 2.0T: 200 hp.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: MK: FWD standard; Freedom Drive I full-time 4WD; Freedom Drive II (CVT-based, off-road package, Trail Rated from 2011). MP: FWD standard (Sport/Latitude); 4WD optional, standard on Trailhawk (Active Drive with low-range simulation via 9-speed's gearing + Selec-Terrain incl. Rock mode); all 2023+ models are 4WD-only.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: MK: WB 103.7 in, L 173.4 in, W 69.3 in, H 65.2 in; curb 3,219–3,715 lb. MP: WB 103.9 in, L 174.0 in, W 71.7 in, H 65.0 in; curb ≈3,318–3,900 lb (US trims; global figure to 4,266 lb includes Euro 4xe PHEV).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: MK: below average; CVT cars are the problem children (see below). MP: poor-to-mixed — NHTSA-based analysis counts 1,729 complaints, 14 recalls, and 11 fire reports across 2017–2025, with the 2.4 Tigershark (2018–2022) accounting for ~93% of complaints (engine and electrical split nearly evenly); the 2023+ 2.0T/8-speed reset the powertrain story and is the better bet. IIHS 2022: Good in all crash tests (updated side test Marginal); top trims earned a Top Safety Pick award. Euro NCAP 2 stars (2012 MK) / strong ANCAP 5-star (2017 MP).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) MK Jatco JF011E CVT2 — overheating, whine, judder, premature failure; FCA faced a class action over the CVT in Compass/Patriot/Caliber. (2) MP 2.4 Tigershark oil consumption — covered by the Wood v. FCA settlement (2017–2018 Compass; same piston/ring defect family as Cherokee, ~1.6M vehicles); PCM reflash recall added limp-mode protection. (3) MP 948TE 9-speed shift-quality complaints and TSB flashes (same ZF family as Cherokee, though lower complaint volume). (4) Early MK TIPM electrical faults; MK suspension wear (front struts, control arms). (5) Various small recalls across MP (backup camera, airbags, software) — 14 total per NHTSA-based count.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil per monitor, up to 10,000 mi/12 mo; 2.4 Tigershark: check level between services (oil-consumption history), 0W-20; 2.0T: 5W-30. Spark plugs 100,000 mi (then every 50k). Coolant: OAT 10 yr/150,000 mi. CVT2 (MK): fluid every 60k with correct CVTF+4 — critical to survival. 6F24/TF-60SN/948TE/8F30: "fill for life" normal duty; service ~60–120k (Firestone/OEM schedule shows automatic transaxle fluid + filter at 120k normal duty; sooner for severe). Brake fluid every 2–3 years. All engines timing chain (no belt).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 — MK 2.4: Not verified — needs OEM source (period tests ~9–10 sec). MP 2.4: Not verified — needs OEM source (~9+ sec in period tests). MP 2.0T (2023+): Not verified — needs OEM source (notably quicker than the 2.4; press estimates ~7.5 sec). No factory performance trims; Trailhawk is the capability variant.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Small but real scene on MP Trailhawk: 1.5–2" lifts, 225–245 A/T tires, skid plates, roof platforms; appearance mods dominate on street trims. MK is largely ignored by enthusiasts. No mainstream ECU tuning for the 2.4; the 2.0T's 8F30 swap is too new for a mature aftermarket.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The MK (2007–2017) rode the DaimlerChrysler/Mitsubishi GS (PM/MK) platform shared with the Dodge Caliber — Jeep's first car-based crossover; 2011 brought the Grand Cherokee-style facelift and Trail Rated Freedom Drive II; 2014 replaced most CVTs with the Hyundai 6F24 6-speed. The MP (2017+) sits on the stretched FCA Small Wide LWB platform shared with the Renegade/Fiat 500X/Toro, with 65%+ high-strength steel in the upper structure, Tigershark power, and the Cherokee's 9-speed; the 2022 refresh redid the interior (10.25" cluster, 10.1" Uconnect 5) and made full ADAS standard; 2023 replaced the 2.4/9-speed with the 200-hp 2.0T and Hyundai 8F30 8-speed and standardized 4WD.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: No cult following — the MK is remembered as the Jeep that wasn't, and the MP is bought on price and looks. The MP Trailhawk earns some respect as a soft-roader with real trail geometry, and 2023+ 2.0T cars are considered "finally adequate" by reviewers. Tuner support is minimal.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda CR-V, Toyota RAV4, Mazda CX-5/CX-50, Subaru Crosstrek/Forester, Hyundai Tucson, Kia Sportage. Compass fights with style, Jeep branding, and (Trailhawk) light off-road ability at a discount price, but trails the class leaders on powertrain refinement (2.4/9-speed), reliability history, and resale; the 2.0T narrowed the drivability gap from 2023.

kia-k5.subtitle: Midsize sedan — 2015 (TF 2nd gen, facelift) · 2016–2020 (JF 3rd gen) · 2021–2026 (DL3 4th gen, renamed K5)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: - 2015 (TF): 2.4L Theta II G4KJ GDI I4 — 2,359 cc, NA; 2.0L Theta II G4KH turbo GDI I4 — 1,998 cc. - 2016–2020 (JF): 2.4L G4KJ GDI I4 (base); 1.6L Gamma G4FJ turbo GDI I4 — 1,591 cc (LX 1.6T / EX); 2.0L G4KH turbo GDI I4 (SX). - 2021–2024 (K5 DL3): 1.6L Gamma II turbo GDI I4 — 1,598 cc, CVVD (LX/LXS/GT-Line/EX); GT: 2.5L Smartstream G2.5 T-GDI (G4KP) turbo I4 — 2,497 cc, GDI+MPI dual injection. - 2025–2026 (K5 refresh): 2.5L Smartstream G4KR NA GDI+MPI I4 — 2,497 cc (LXS/EX/GT-Line); GT keeps the 2.5T G4KP.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: - TF/JF: 6-speed torque-converter automatic (A6MF1 family; 2.0T uses the heavier A6LF1 family) with Sportmatic; JF 1.6T uses 7-speed dry dual-clutch (D7UF1). - K5: 1.6T/2.5 NA — 8-speed torque-converter automatic (A8LF1); GT — 8-speed wet dual-clutch (D8LF1). No manuals.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD only on all Optimas (TF/JF). K5 is FWD standard; on-demand AWD offered only on GT-Line (2021+). GT is FWD-only with sport-tuned suspension and larger 13.6-in. front brakes.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: The JF/TF Theta II cars are the core of Kia's engine-defect history (see Known issues) — that drags the generation's reputation despite otherwise solid chassis/electronics. Consumer Reports and J.D. Power consistently scored the gasoline K5 much better than the Theta-era Optima; the 2025 K5 carries an 84/100 J.D. Power Quality & Reliability score. IIHS awarded Top Safety Pick/TSP+ to most Optima (2016+) and K5 model years (verify exact year/headlight combo at iihs.org).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: - Theta II 2.0T/2.4 GDI connecting-rod bearing wear → engine seizure/fire: NHTSA recall 17V224000 (618,160 units, machining debris; covers 2011–2014 Optima/Sorento, 2011–2013 Sportage); Knock Sensor Detection System (KSDS) product-improvement campaign; settled US class actions (Engine I & II) covering 2011–2019 Optima with lifetime engine warranty if the KSDS update is installed. Nov 2020 NHTSA consent orders fined Hyundai/Kia a combined $210M over recall handling. - Engine-compartment fire recalls: 21V137 (HECU short, 2017–2021 Sportage/Cadenza) and the Sept 2023 HECU/ABS "park outside" recall covering 2011–2015 Optima and 2010–2013-era Kias; 20V750-series fire recalls also covered earlier Forte/Sorento/Optima Hybrid. - JF 1.6T 7-speed dry DCT (D7UF1): low-speed judder/hesitation complaints — clutch service history matters. - K5 GT 8DCT (D8LF1): overheating/harsh-shift complaints under hard use; fluid service critical. - Smartstream 2.5 NA: oil-consumption complaints and class-action filings (piston ring/oil drain-back design); monitor oil level between services.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 7,500 mi normal / 3,750 mi severe (GDI engines — Kia US schedule); use oil meeting API SP / ILSAC GF-6 per manual. All engines here are timing-chain — no belt service. Iridium spark plugs ~100,000–105,000 mi (verify per MY; turbo GDI severe schedule is shorter). ATF: "fill-for-life" on the sticker but Kia severe-service schedule calls for replacement ~60,000 mi; dry 7DCT and wet 8DCT need fluid/clutch inspections per severe schedule. Long-life coolant first change ~120,000 mi (some schedules 90,000 — verify per MY). Brake fluid: inspect annually, dealer-typical replacement every 3 yrs — OEM interval Not verified — needs OEM source.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: Optima 2.4 ~7.5 s; Optima SX 2.0T 6.7–6.8 s (instrumented tests); K5 1.6T 7.8 s (MotorTrend, EX); K5 GT 5.2–5.3 s, 13.7–13.8 s quarter-mile (Car and Driver, 2021). K5 GT is the factory performance trim: 290 hp, e-LSD-equipped, sport-tuned suspension, 13.6-in. brakes, 19-in. wheels.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: The K5 GT 2.5T has real tuner support — piggyback modules (RaceChip, STILLEN TruControl), intakes, downpipes, and intercoolers; JB4-type tunes push well past 330 hp. Optima 2.0T (G4KH) shares tuning DNA with the Veloster N/Sonata turbo community — flash tunes and bolt-ons are common. Coilovers and sway bars from the Hyundai/K-sport aftermarket (BC Racing, Eibach, H&R) fit both generations.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: TF and JF ride on Hyundai-Kia's YF/JF midsize architecture (MacPherson strut front, multi-link rear) shared with the Sonata. The DL3 K5 moved to the N3 platform: lower, wider, longer wheelbase (+1.8 in. vs JF), more high-strength steel, and available AWD for the first time in this nameplate. Key powertrain tech: CVVD (continuously variable valve duration) on the 1.6T and dual GDI+MPI injection on the 2.5T; the 8DCT in the GT was developed in-house to handle the 2.5T's 311 lb-ft.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Optima SX/SXL 2.0T was a respected "sleeper" in its day; the K5 GT has a genuine following as the value alternative to the Sonata N Line and even the BMW 330i (Kia's own AMCI testing benchmarked it against the 330i). Forum communities (K5GT.org, Kia-Forums) are active on tune/wheel fitment. Non-GT cars are treated as appliance transport — clean, reliable transportation but not hobby cars.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Direct rivals: Honda Accord, Toyota Camry, Hyundai Sonata, Nissan Altima, Mazda6 (through 2021). The Optima/K5 matches the Sonata mechanically; vs. Accord/Camry it typically offers more power-per-dollar (GT) and longer warranty (10-yr/100k powertrain), but Theta-era engine history means any used Optima needs recall/KSDS verification before purchase. Driving feel is tuned softer than Mazda6/Accord but the GT is legitimately quick.

kia-forte.subtitle: Compact sedan (Forte5 hatchback through 2017) — 2015–2018 (YD 3rd gen) · 2019–2024 (BD 4th gen; replaced by K4 for 2025)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: - 2015–2016 (YD): 1.8L Nu G4NB MPI I4 — 1,797 cc (LX); 2.0L Nu G4NA GDI I4 — 1,999 cc (EX). Forte5 SX: 1.6L Gamma G4FJ turbo GDI. - 2017–2018 (YD facelift): 2.0L Nu MPI I4 — 147 hp (LX/S); 2.0L GDI I4 — 164 hp (EX). - 2019–2024 (BD): 2.0L Nu MPI I4 — 1,999 cc, 12.5:1 CR (all non-GT trims); GT: 1.6L G4FJ turbo GDI I4 — 1,591 cc.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: - YD: 6-speed manual or 6-speed automatic (A6MF1); Forte5 SX turbo: 6MT or 6AT. - BD: IVT — Kia's chain-driven CVT (C0GF1) standard on 2.0 MPI trims (6MT offered on early FE); GT: 6-speed manual or 7-speed dry dual-clutch (D7UF1).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD only, all years/trims. GT adds multi-link rear suspension (vs. torsion beam on other BD trims), larger 12.0-in. front brakes, 18-in. wheels.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: The strongest reliability story in this data pack — the Nu 2.0 MPI is a simple port-injected engine with no GDI carbon issues and no Theta bearing history. 2010–2013 Fortes were swept into the 20V750-series fire-risk recall (2.0 Nu/2.4 Theta MPI), and the 2010–2013 era appears in the Sept 2023 HECU "park outside" recall, but 2014+ BD/YD cars are largely recall-light. Consumer Reports has rated the BD Forte at or above segment average for reliability (verify per model year at consumerreports.org).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: - Steering coupler (MDPS rubber coupling) wear → clicking/knocking felt through the wheel — widespread on YD cars; cheap part, labor-intensive fix. Well documented on Kia forums and in TSB coverage. - IVT (C0GF1) on 2019+: early-build shift-shock/jerk complaints; Kia issued TSBs and software updates. Verify fluid condition on high-mile units. - GT 7-speed dry DCT (D7UF1): judder/hesitation in stop-and-go; clutch packs are wear items. - Nu engines: occasional EVAP purge valve and catalytic converter (P0420) complaints; nothing systemic like the Theta.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 7,500 mi normal / 3,750 mi severe. All engines timing-chain. Iridium plugs ~100,000 mi (1.6T GDI: shorter per severe schedule — verify per MY). IVT fluid: Kia lists no normal-service interval; severe schedule ~60,000 mi replacement — strongly recommended. 6AT/7DCT: inspect per schedule, severe-service fluid change ~60,000 mi. Coolant ~120,000 mi first change (verify per MY). Brake fluid: inspect annually; OEM fixed interval Not verified — needs OEM source.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 2.0 MPI IVT ~8.1–8.7 s (Edmunds tested 8.7); Forte GT 6.4–6.7 s (2020 DCT 6.7, 2022 6.4 per compiled instrumented tests). GT is the only performance trim: 201 hp, sport suspension, independent rear, summer-tire option. Forte5 SX (1.6T, 2015–2017) was the earlier hot variant: 6.5–6.7 s.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: The GT shares its 1.6T (G4FJ) with the Veloster Turbo/Elantra N Line, so that whole catalog applies: intake, cat-back exhaust, intercooler, and JB4/custom flash tunes taking the engine toward 230+ hp. Popular chassis mods: rear sway bar (transforms torsion-beam cars), lowering springs/coilovers, 18x8 wheels. The Forte GT is one of the last compacts available with a manual — that alone gives it cult status.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: YD rides on the Hyundai-Kia HD compact platform (Elantra MD); BD moved to the K3 platform with a stiffer body, more ADAS content, and the switch from a conventional 6AT to the chain-type IVT for economy trims. The GT's conversion to a multi-link rear axle is the key chassis differentiator within the BD range. All engines are aluminum DOHC 16V with dual CVVT.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Forte GT is a well-kept secret in the budget-sport-compact scene — same powertrain family as the Veloster Turbo with a usable back seat and an available 6MT. Aftermarket support is borrowed wholesale from Hyundai's turbo 1.6 ecosystem. Non-GT Fortes have near-zero enthusiast presence; they're valued as cheap, durable commuters.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda Civic, Toyota Corolla, Hyundai Elantra, Mazda3, VW Jetta. The Forte generally undercuts Civic/Corolla on price and beats them on warranty; the Nu 2.0 MPI is arguably the most durable engine in the segment, though down on power and refinement vs. the 1.5T Civic. GT vs. Civic Si/Jetta GLI: slower and less polished, but thousands cheaper.

kia-sorento.subtitle: Midsize 3-row SUV (2-row available 2016–2018) — 2015 (XM 2nd gen) · 2016–2020 (UM 3rd gen) · 2021–2026 (MQ4 4th gen)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: - 2015 (XM): 2.4L Theta II G4KJ GDI I4 — 2,359 cc (LX); 3.3L Lambda II G6DH GDI V6 — 3,342 cc. - 2016–2018 (UM): 2.4L G4KJ GDI (L/LX); 2.0L G4KH turbo GDI (EX/SX-Limited); 3.3L G6DH V6. - 2019–2020 (UM facelift): 2.4L G4KJ (L/LX); 3.3L G6DH V6 (all other trims; 2.0T dropped). - 2021–2026 (MQ4): 2.5L Smartstream G4KR GDI+MPI I4 — 2,497 cc, 13.0:1 (LX/S); 2.5L Smartstream G4KP T-GDI I4 — 2,497 cc, GDI+MPI (EX/SX/SX Prestige); HEV: 1.6T GDI hybrid (227 hp combined); PHEV: 1.6T plug-in (261 hp combined).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: - XM/UM 2016–2018: 6-speed automatic (A6MF1 for I4, A6LF1 for 2.0T/V6). - UM 2019–2020: 2.4 keeps 6AT; V6 gets 8-speed automatic (A8LF1). - MQ4: 2.5 NA — 8-speed automatic (A8LF1); 2.5T — 8-speed wet dual-clutch (D8LF1); HEV/PHEV — 6-speed automatic with integrated e-motor.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD or torque-on-demand AWD with locking center differential (all gasoline trims, XM/UM). MQ4: 2.5 NA FWD or AWD; 2.5T FWD or AWD (X-Line AWD-only, 8.2-in. clearance); HEV FWD/AWD; PHEV AWD-only. X-Pro (2024+) is the off-road trim.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Mixed by generation. Theta II 2.4 cars (2012–2019) are inside the class-action/KSDS umbrella (lifetime engine warranty applies if updated); the 2017 Sorento alone carried 5 NHTSA recalls. The 3.3 V6 (G6DH) is the durable pick of the UM generation. MQ4 is improved — 82/100 J.D. Power Q&R on the 2023 Sorento Hybrid. Sept 2023 HECU "park outside" recall covers 2011–2014 Sorento.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: - Theta II 2.4/2.0T rod-bearing failure and fire risk — recalls 17V224000, KSDS campaign, Engine I/II class actions (2012–2019 Sorento covered). - 20V750 (Dec 2020): fire risk on 2012–2013 Sorento 2.4 MPI — fuel/oil leak or engine damage. - SC172 (Dec 2018): high-pressure fuel pipe improperly torqued during Theta engine replacements → fuel leak; affects 2012–2017 Sorento previously re-engined. - MQ4 8DCT (D8LF1): low-speed creep/shift harshness complaints; clutch fluid service essential. - Smartstream 2.5 NA oil consumption (class-action filings, piston-ring design) — check level regularly on 2021+ 2.5 cars.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 7,500 mi normal / 3,750 severe (Theta/Lambda era); 2021+ Smartstream — verify per MY manual (Kia moved some models to longer normal intervals). All engines timing-chain (G4KJ, G4KH, G6DH, G4KR, G4KP — no belts). Iridium plugs ~100,000 mi NA / earlier on GDI turbo per severe schedule (verify). ATF: no normal interval; severe ~60,000 mi. 8DCT fluid per severe schedule. AWD transfer case/rear diff: inspect, severe-service fluid changes. Coolant ~120,000 mi first change (verify per MY). Brake fluid: inspect; OEM fixed interval Not verified — needs OEM source.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: XM/UM V6 AWD 7.1–7.4 s; UM 2.4 ~9.1 s; MQ4 2.5T AWD 6.0–6.3 s (fastest Sorento ever); MQ4 2.5 NA ~9.5 s (Kia internal 0–100 km/h 9.5 s FWD); HEV ~7.2–8.6 s. No factory performance trim; X-Line/X-Pro are appearance/off-road packages. Kia-published top speed 131 mph (2.5T).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Minimal hardcore tuning — owners focus on the X-Line/X-Pro formula: all-terrain tires (235/65R17 swaps), 1–1.5-in. lift/leveling spacers, roof racks, skid plates, and light bars. The 2.5T (G4KP) accepts the same piggyback tunes as the K5 GT (RaceChip/STILLEN) for meaningful gains. Towing is the big mod driver: 3,500 lbs with tow package (MQ4), up to 5,000 lbs on V6 AWD (UM).

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: XM was conventional unibody; UM brought Kia's "Iso-Structure" high-strength-steel body (53% AHSS), longer wheelbase (+3.1 in.), and the 2.0T option. MQ4 rides on the N3 platform shared with the K5/Santa Fe: +1.4-in. wheelbase, first Sorento hybrid/PHEV, dual-injection Smartstream engines, and an 8DCT for the turbo. The HEV/PHEV use a 1.6T with a 6AT and integrated motor rather than a CVT-style e-transaxle.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Not an enthusiast vehicle in the tuner sense, but the UM V6 and MQ4 X-Pro have a solid overlanding/family-adventure following — lift kits and AT tires are the community norm. The 2.5T's 311 lb-ft surprises people; forums note it will outrun many "sporty" crossovers. Respected as a tow-rig-for-people-who-hate-minivans.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Hyundai Santa Fe, Toyota Highlander, Honda Pilot, Ford Explorer, Nissan Pathfinder (MQ4 straddles compact/midsize: also cross-shopped vs. CR-V/RAV4). Sorento's edge: available 2.5T punch, warranty, and price under Highlander/Pilot; weaker residual values and the Theta-era baggage hurt used-car trust on 2016–2019 four-cylinders. V6 UM cars are the sweet spot for a shop customer wanting durability.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: - 2015–2016 (SL): 2.4L Theta II G4KJ GDI I4 — 2,359 cc (LX/EX); SX: 2.0L G4KH turbo GDI I4 — 1,998 cc. - 2017–2022 (QL): 2.4L G4KJ GDI (LX/EX); SX Turbo: 2.0L G4KH turbo GDI. - 2023–2026 (NQ5): 2.5L Smartstream G4KR GDI+MPI I4 — 2,497 cc, 13.0:1 (all gas trims); HEV: 1.6T hybrid (227 hp combined); PHEV: 1.6T plug-in (261 hp combined).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: - SL/QL: 6-speed automatic (A6MF1 for 2.4; A6LF1-family for 2.0T), FWD or AWD. - NQ5: 8-speed automatic (A8LF1); HEV/PHEV: 6-speed automatic with integrated e-motor.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
