horsepower.text: - SL: 2.4 — 182 hp @ 6,000 (LX FWD; 180 hp EX AWD); 2.0T — 260 hp @ 6,000 (2015 SX listed 260; 2016 SX 260 ULEV). - QL: 2.4 — 181 hp @ 6,000; 2.0T — 240 hp @ 6,000 FWD / 237 hp AWD. - NQ5: 2.5 NA — 187 hp @ 6,100; HEV — 227 hp combined; PHEV — 261 hp combined.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD standard, on-demand AWD optional on virtually all trims (all gens). NQ5 X-Line/X-Pro/X-Pro Prestige are AWD-only with 8.3-in. ground clearance and all-terrain tires (X-Pro).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: QL/SL four-cylinders carry the Theta II story (2011–2019 Sportage in the class actions; lifetime engine warranty with KSDS installed). NHTSA data shows 819 owner complaints filed across Sportage generations, with engine dominating (556). The 2017–2021 Sportage was subject to recall 21V137 — a park-outside HECU fire-risk recall covering 379,931 Sportage/Cadenza vehicles. NQ5 is materially better: 84/100 J.D. Power Q&R for the Sportage Hybrid. NQ5 earned IIHS Top Safety Pick recognition in multiple years (verify per MY at iihs.org).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: - Theta II 2.4/2.0T rod-bearing failure/seizure — recalls 17V224000 (2011–2013 Sportage), KSDS campaign, class actions through 2019; same symptoms: knock, oil light, stall. - 21V137 (March 2021): HECU electrical short → engine-compartment fire, 2017–2021 Sportage without Smart Cruise Control; "park outside" consumer alert issued by NHTSA. - SC172 (2018): fuel-pipe leak check after Theta engine replacements (2011–2018 Sportage). - QL AWD couplings and 6AT torque-converter shudder complaints at high mileage (forum-documented; service fluid early). - NQ5 2.5 NA oil consumption complaints/class-action filings (Smartstream piston-ring issue) — monitor level.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 7,500 mi normal / 3,750 severe (Theta era); 2023+ Smartstream — verify per MY manual. All timing-chain — no belt service. Iridium plugs ~100,000 mi (2.0T shorter per severe schedule — verify). ATF: no normal interval, severe ~60,000 mi. AWD rear coupling/diff fluid: inspect, severe-service replacement. HEV/PHEV: same engine schedule plus hybrid-system coolant checks. Coolant ~120,000 mi first change (verify per MY). Brake fluid: inspect; OEM fixed interval Not verified — needs OEM source.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: SL/QL 2.4 ~8.8–9.3 s; QL SX Turbo 6.7 s FWD / 7.0 s AWD; NQ5 2.5 AWD ~8.5 s (independent tests — exact instrumented figure Not verified — needs OEM source); NQ5 HEV ~7.5–8 s (approximate). No factory performance trim; X-Pro is the off-road flagship with 17-in. AT tires and multi-terrain modes. Towing: 2,000 lbs (SL/QL), 2,500 lbs (NQ5 gas).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Light-tuner presence: SX Turbo (G4KH) accepts the usual Theta turbo bolt-ons and piggyback tunes. NQ5 X-Pro owners do mild overlanding builds — AT tires, roof baskets, 1-in. spacers. Wheels/tires and cosmetic mods dominate; serious performance builds are rare because the aftermarket points people to the K5 GT/Stinger instead.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: SL shared its platform with the Hyundai Tucson (ix35); QL added a stiffer structure and the 240-hp SX Turbo. NQ5 is built on the N3 platform (with K5/Sorento/Tucson) and the US gets the long-wheelbase body — +7.1 in. overall and +3.4 in. wheelbase vs. QL, yielding class-leading rear legroom and cargo space (39.6 cu ft behind row 2). Smartstream 2.5 uses dual GDI+MPI injection and a high 13.0:1 compression ratio; hybrids pair the 1.6T with a conventional 6AT.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The Sportage has never been a tuner darling — its following is pragmatic. The SX Turbo has a small cult for being a genuinely quick compact SUV (quicker than a base Stinger 2.0T to 60 in some tests), and the NQ5 X-Pro is popular in the soft-roading community. Forum sentiment: buy the NQ5 or a V6-free post-2020 QL and check recall status first.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda CR-V, Toyota RAV4, Hyundai Tucson, Mazda CX-5/CX-50, Ford Escape, Chevy Equinox. NQ5 wins on space, tech-per-dollar, warranty, and hybrid MPG (43 combined FWD); it trails RAV4/CR-V on resale and on the 2.5's refinement. For a used QL, the SX Turbo is the driver's pick but carries Theta risk — documented KSDS/recall completion is mandatory diligence.

lexus-rx.subtitle: Midsize luxury SUV/crossover, 5-seat (2-row) and RX L (3-row, 2018–2022); generations: 2016–2022 (4th gen, AL20), 2023–2026 (5th gen, ALA10/ALH10)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2016–2022: RX 350 = 3.5L 2GR-FKS naturally aspirated V6 (D-4S port+direct injection, VVT-iW); RX 450h = 3.5L 2GR-FXS Atkinson-cycle V6 + front/rear motor-generators (THS II). 2023–2026: RX 350 = 2.4L T24A-FTS turbocharged I4; RX 350h = 2.5L A25A-FXS Atkinson I4 hybrid (THS II); RX 450h+ = 2.5L A25A-FXS plug-in hybrid (18.1 kWh Li-ion); RX 500h = 2.4L T24A-FTS turbo hybrid (single-motor "Performance Hybrid" system).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 2016–2022: 8-speed automatic (UA80E/UA80F "Direct Shift-8") on RX 350; e-CVT planetary hybrid transaxle on RX 450h. 2023–2026: 8-speed automatic on RX 350; e-CVT on RX 350h and RX 450h+; 6-speed automatic on RX 500h.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 2016–2022: RX 350 = 295 hp @ 6,300 rpm; RX 450h = 308 hp system total. 2023–2026: RX 350 = 275 hp; RX 350h = 246 hp system; RX 450h+ = 304 hp system; RX 500h = 366 hp system (Lexus Canada rounds to 367).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 2016–2022: RX 350 = 267 lb-ft @ 4,700 rpm; RX 450h = 247 lb-ft system. 2023–2026: RX 350 = 317 lb-ft; RX 350h = 233 lb-ft @ 4,300 rpm (engine); RX 500h = 406 lb-ft system; RX 450h+ combined torque not published by Lexus.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: 2016–2022: RX 350 FWD or AWD (mechanical, Dynamic Torque Control); RX 450h FWD or AWD — AWD hybrid uses a rear-axle motor (E-Four), no driveshaft; RX 350L/450hL (2018–2022) FWD/AWD. 2023–2026: RX 350 FWD or AWD; RX 350h AWD standard (E-Four); RX 450h+ AWD (E-Four); RX 500h DIRECT4 AWD only.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 2016–2022: 192.5 in L × 74.6 in W × 67.7 in H, wheelbase 109.8 in; curb ≈ 4,222–4,674 lbs. RX L: 196.9 in L (same 109.8 in wheelbase); curb ≈ 4,464–4,905 lbs. 2023–2026: 192.5 in L × 75.6 in W × 67.3 in H, wheelbase 112.2 in; curb ≈ 4,155 lbs (RX 350 FWD) to ≈ 4,750 lbs (RX 450h+); RX 350h measured 4,365–4,431 lbs in press tests.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: 2016–2022 (EPA city/hwy/combined): RX 350 FWD 20/27–28/23, AWD 19/26/22; RX 450h FWD 31/30/30, AWD 31/28/30. 2023–2026: RX 350 FWD 22/29/25, AWD 21/28/24; RX 350h 37/34/36; RX 500h 27/28/27; RX 450h+ ≈35 combined on gas, 83 MPGe, 37 mi EPA electric range.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: 4th gen is one of the most dependable luxury SUVs sold: U.S. News gave the 2016 RX 350 a 5/5 reliability score (J.D. Power-based); Lexus ranked #1 overall in the 2024 J.D. Power Vehicle Dependability Study (135 PP100) with the RX taking a segment model award, repeating its 2023 win. NHTSA recorded only ~3 recalls on the 2016 model. 5th gen: too new for long-term scores but no systemic defects documented as of mid-2026.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: 2016 recalls: driver's knee airbag inflator gas leak, brake-fluid-pressure/ brake actuator concern, and an airbag sensor issue (per NHTSA via U.S. News). 2018–2019 RX models are covered by the industry-wide Denso low-pressure fuel pump recall (impeller swelling → stall risk; NHTSA 20V-012 and expansions). Owner-forum-documented (not recall-level): infotainment screen delamination/failure, auto-dimming mirror discoloration, water-pump weep on 2GR-FKS, and transfer-case/rear-diff seal seepage on high-mileage AWD units. 5th gen: e-Latch door and Lexus Interface software updates handled via TSBs; no pattern failures verified.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil: 0W-20 full synthetic every 10,000 mi/12 mo (5,000 mi severe-service) on 2016–2022; 2023+ T24A-FTS uses 0W-16 (API SP/GF-6B), 5.8 qt with filter — do not substitute the old spec. Spark plugs: iridium, 120,000 mi. Engine coolant: Toyota Super Long Life (pink), first change 100,000 mi then every 50,000 mi; hybrid inverter coolant same interval. ATF: WS "lifetime" per guide (inspect only); common shop practice is a drain-and-fill at 60,000 mi. Brake fluid: inspect per schedule; Lexus dealers commonly recommend replacement every 30,000 mi/3 yr. All engines are timing-chain; no belt service. AWD: transfer case and rear diff fluid inspect/replace per severe-service schedule.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 mph: 2016–2022 RX 350 ≈7.3 s (C&D, AWD F Sport), RX 450h ≈6.9 s; 2023+ RX 350 7.5 s FWD / 7.2 s AWD, RX 350h 7.4 s, RX 500h 5.9 s, RX 450h+ ≈6.2 s (all Lexus estimates). Performance trims: F Sport (adaptive variable suspension, sport tuning) and RX 500h F SPORT Performance (DIRECT4, 6-piston front brakes, 21-in wheels). Tow rating 3,500 lbs (2016–2022, AWD hybrids; 2,000 lbs FWD).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Light mod scene: 20–22-in wheel/tire packages, lowering springs, roof systems; no meaningful ECU tuning market for the V6 or hybrid systems. F SPORT accessories (aero, exhaust tips) are dealer-installed. The RX 500h is the fastest factory variant; nothing beyond cosmetics is commonly done.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: 4th gen (2016) moved to Toyota's K-platform (MC) architecture with a stiffer body, laser-screw welding and structural adhesives, and swapped the old 6-speed for the UA80 8-speed. 5th gen (2023) rides on GA-K (TNGA): ~198 lb weight reduction, 2.4-in-longer wheelbase with equal overall length, and the V6 replaced entirely by turbo-four and electrified powertrains — including Lexus's first plug-in RX (450h+) and the single-motor performance hybrid (500h) with DIRECT4 rear-axle torque control.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The community views the RX as the definitive "appliance done right" — not a driver's SUV, but the default answer for reliability-first luxury. Resale values are class-leading, so clean used examples move fast. The RX 450h+ PHEV gets respect as a genuine 37-mile EV commuter; the 500h divides opinion (quick, but the 6-speed + turbo hybrid is less refined than the V6 it replaced).

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Acura MDX, BMW X5, Mercedes GLE, Genesis GV80, Cadillac XT5. The RX beats all of them on long-term reliability, running cost and resale; the X5/GLE/GV80 drive better and tow more. For a repair shop's customers: it is the lowest-risk recommendation in the segment, with the 450h hybrids proven past 200k miles.

lexus-es.subtitle: Midsize luxury sedan, FWD-based; generations: 2015–2018 (6th gen, XV60; facelift from 2016), 2019–2025 (7th gen, XV70), 2026 (8th gen, electrified)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2018: ES 350 = 3.5L 2GR-FE naturally aspirated V6 (port injection, Dual VVT-i); ES 300h = 2.5L 2AR-FXE Atkinson I4 hybrid (THS II). 2019–2025: ES 350 = 3.5L 2GR-FKS V6 (D-4S, VVT-iW); ES 300h = 2.5L A25A-FXS Atkinson I4 hybrid (new THS II, NiMH battery); ES 250 (2021–2023) = 2.5L A25A-FKS Dynamic Force I4, AWD only. 2026: ES 350h = 2.5L I4 with 6th-generation Toyota hybrid system; ES 350e/ES 500e = battery-electric (single-motor FWD / dual-motor AWD).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 2015–2018: 6-speed automatic (U660E) on ES 350; e-CVT on ES 300h. 2019–2025: 8-speed "Direct Shift" automatic (UA80 family) on ES 350/ES 250; e-CVT on ES 300h. 2026: e-CVT (hybrid); single-speed reduction drive (EV).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 2015–2018: ES 350 = 268 hp @ 6,200 rpm; ES 300h = 200 hp system total. 2019–2025: ES 350 = 302 hp @ 6,600 rpm; ES 300h = 215 hp system; ES 250 = 203 hp. 2026: ES 350h = 244 hp system; EV outputs not yet published by Lexus (targets: 300-mile EPA range for ES 350e, 250 miles for ES 500e).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 2015–2018: ES 350 = 248 lb-ft @ 4,700 rpm; ES 300h engine 156 lb-ft (system figure not published). 2019–2025: ES 350 = 267 lb-ft @ 4,700 rpm; ES 300h engine 163 lb-ft @ 3,600–5,200 rpm; ES 250 = 184 lb-ft. 2026 hybrid torque — Not verified — needs OEM source.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD only for all ES 350 and ES 300h 2015–2025 (ES is resolutely front-drive; no AWD provision). ES 250 (2021–2023) = AWD only. 2026: hybrid FWD or AWD; EV FWD (350e) or AWD (500e).

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 2015–2018: 193.3 in L × 71.7 in W × 57.1 in H, wheelbase 111.0 in; curb 3,571 lbs (350) to 3,682 lbs (300h). 2019–2025: 195.9 in L × 73.4 in W × 56.9 in H, wheelbase 113.0 in; curb 3,649 lbs (350) to 3,704–3,730 lbs (300h). 2026 dimensions — Not verified — needs OEM source (new, larger body on updated GA-K).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: 2015–2018 (EPA city/hwy/combined): ES 350 21/31/24; ES 300h 40/39/40. 2019–2025: ES 350 22/32–33/26; ES 300h 43/44/44 (2019: 43/45/44); ES 250 AWD 25/34/28. 2026: ES 350h EPA 48/44/46 (Lexus-estimated 46 combined); EV range targets above, final EPA figures pending.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Exceptional across both generations. The ES won its segment in the 2024 J.D. Power Vehicle Dependability Study (Lexus #1 brand overall, 135 PP100). Consumer Reports has rated both XV60 and XV70 ES above average; iSeeCars scores the ES 300h 8.9/10 with an average lifespan estimate of ~180,000 miles. 2016 ES 350 carries a 5/5 U.S. News/J.D. Power reliability rating heritage; NHTSA recall counts are low (Takata airbag campaigns on 2015-era cars aside).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: XV60 (2015–2018): covered by Takata passenger airbag inflator recalls (industry-wide); Remote Touch "mouse" infotainment widely criticized (ergonomics, not failure); scattered mirror discoloration and minor interior trim complaints. XV70 (2019–2025): 2019 ES 350 included in Denso low-pressure fuel pump recall expansions; early cars had infotainment/screen glare complaints and some brake-feel (regen blending) criticism on the 300h. No engine, transmission or hybrid-system pattern failures documented on either generation.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil: 0W-20 full synthetic, 10,000 mi/12 mo (all engines; 5,000 mi severe). Spark plugs: 120,000 mi (iridium). Coolant: Toyota SLLC pink, 100,000 mi then 50,000 mi; hybrid inverter coolant same. Transmission: WS ATF "lifetime" per guide — drain-and-fill at 60,000 mi is standard shop practice on U660E and UA80 units. Brake fluid: inspect; dealers recommend ~30,000 mi/3 yr. All timing-chain; no belt. ES 300h hybrid battery (NiMH) is maintenance-free; 10-yr/150k-mi hybrid battery warranty applies to 2020+.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 mph: 2015–2018 ES 350 ≈7.1 s, ES 300h ≈7.8–8.1 s; 2019–2025 ES 350 6.6 s (Lexus est.), ES 300h ≈8.1 s. F Sport trim introduced for the first time on the ES in 2019 (adaptive dampers on F Sport Handling, 2021+), but there is no factory high-performance powertrain — the V6 is the top engine.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Essentially no tuner market. F Sport and F Sport Handling are cosmetic/chassis packages; owners occasionally fit 19-in wheels and springs. Nobody tunes the 2GR-FKS in ES trim, and hybrid powertrains are untouched by the aftermarket.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: XV60 shared Toyota's K platform with the Avalon. The 2019 XV70 moved to GA-K (TNGA): stiffer structure, multi-link rear suspension replacing struts, rack-mounted electric power steering, and a lower seating position/center of gravity — the first ES engineered for any dynamic ambition. The 2026 8th gen is a "multi-pathway" car on an evolved GA-K: hybrid (350h) and full BEV (350e/500e) variants with a NACS (Tesla-style) charge port from the factory.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Viewed as the sensible-shoes Lexus — the enthusiast press treats it as a Camry/Avalon in a good suit, but owners are fiercely loyal and the 300h's 44 mpg in a near-full-size sedan earns real respect. No cult trims; the 2025 ES 350 F Sport Handling is prized as the last V6 ES before electrification.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Acura TLX, Genesis G80 (2.5T), Cadillac CT5, Volvo S90, plus its Toyota Avalon sibling. The ES wins on reliability, ride isolation and cost of ownership; the Germans and the G80 are more engaging to drive. Value retention and the cheapest-to-keep hybrid in the class are its shop-floor selling points.

lexus-nx.subtitle: Compact luxury SUV/crossover; generations: 2015–2021 (1st gen, AZ10), 2022–2026 (2nd gen, AZ20)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2021: NX 200t (2015–2017) / NX 300 (2018–2021, rename only) = 2.0L 8AR-FTS twin-scroll turbo I4 (D-4ST port+direct injection, VVT-iW Otto/Atkinson switchable); NX 300h = 2.5L 2AR-FXE Atkinson I4 hybrid (THS II). 2022–2026: NX 250 = 2.5L A25A-FKS Dynamic Force NA I4; NX 350 = 2.4L T24A-FTS turbo I4; NX 350h = 2.5L A25A-FXS hybrid; NX 450h+ = 2.5L A25A-FXS plug-in hybrid (18.1 kWh Li-ion).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 2015–2021: 6-speed conventional automatic (Super ECT) on 200t/300; e-CVT on 300h. 2022–2026: 8-speed "Direct Shift" automatic on NX 250/350; e-CVT on NX 350h/450h+.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 2015–2021: NX 200t/300 = 258 lb-ft @ 1,650–4,000 rpm; NX 300h system torque not published. 2022–2026: NX 250 = 184 lb-ft; NX 350 = 317 lb-ft; NX 350h engine 167 lb-ft @ 3,200–3,700 rpm; NX 450h+ combined not published (front motor 199 lb-ft, engine 167 lb-ft).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: 2015–2021: NX 200t/300 FWD or AWD (Dynamic Torque Control AWD, mechanical); NX 300h AWD only (E-Four rear motor). 2022–2026: NX 250 FWD or AWD; NX 350 AWD only; NX 350h and NX 450h+ AWD only (E-Four).

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: 2015–2021: 182.3 in L × 73.6 in W × 64.8 in H, wheelbase 104.7 in, ground clearance 6.9 in; curb 3,940–4,050 lbs (200t), ≈4,185 lbs (300h). 2022–2026: 183.5 in L × 73.4 in W × 65.4 in H, wheelbase 105.9 in; curb ≈3,660 lbs (250 FWD) to ≈4,475 lbs (450h+).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: 2015–2021 (EPA city/hwy/combined): NX 200t FWD 22/28/25, AWD 22/27/24 (premium fuel required); NX 300h 33/30/31. 2022–2026: NX 250 FWD 26/33/28, AWD 25/32/28; NX 350 AWD 22/29/25 (2022–23), 21/28/24 (2024+); NX 350h 41/37/39; NX 450h+ 84 MPGe with 37 mi EPA electric range, 36 combined on gas.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Strong on both generations. The NX took a segment award in the 2024 J.D. Power Vehicle Dependability Study (Lexus #1 brand). The 300h's 2AR-FXE/THS II drivetrain is one of the most durable hybrid systems built (270,000–450,000 km engine-life estimates). CarComplaints shows low complaint volume; the 8AR-FTS turbo cars generate most of the (modest) TSB traffic.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: 8AR-FTS-specific TSBs: L-SB-0041-16 (MIL on, DTC P019023 fuel-rail-pressure sensor stuck low — ECM reflash); L-SB-0007-21 (8AR-FTS engine concern affecting NX 200t/300 and other 8AR models); L-TT-0215-16 (highway-speed vibration diagnosis, 2015–2017). Remote Touch touchpad infotainment widely panned (usability). Turbo engine requires premium fuel and has occasional wastegate rattle reports on forums. Gen 2 (2022+): Lexus Interface infotainment glitches/software updates; e-Latch electronic door-handle learning curve; no mechanical pattern failures verified as of mid-2026.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil: 0W-20 full synthetic, 10,000 mi/12 mo (5,000 mi severe; turbo cars benefit from shorter intervals — 8AR-FTS capacity ≈4.9 qt w/filter). Premium unleaded required on 8AR-FTS and T24A-FTS. Spark plugs: 120,000 mi iridium. Coolant: SLLC pink, 100,000 mi then 50,000 mi (engine and hybrid inverter loops). ATF: WS "lifetime" per guide; 60,000-mi drain-and-fill recommended shop practice. Brake fluid: inspect; ~30,000 mi/3 yr dealer norm. All engines timing-chain. AWD models: inspect transfer case/rear diff fluid; hybrid transaxles are sealed e-CVT units.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 mph: NX 200t 7.0 s (manufacturer; KBB lists 7.2 s F Sport), NX 300h 9.1 s; 2022+: NX 250 8.2 s, NX 350 6.6 s, NX 350h 7.2 s, NX 450h+ 6.0 s (fastest NX). F Sport trim (both gens) adds sport suspension/AVS and appearance; gen 2 adds F Sport Handling with adaptive dampers. Towing: 2,000 lbs (gen 1).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Small aftermarket: wheel/tire packages, lowering springs, roof carriers; some bolt-on support borrowed from RAV4-platform cousins. No credible ECU tune market for the 8AR-FTS in NX trim (a handful of piggyback boxes exist). F Sport / F Sport Handling are the ceiling from the factory.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: Gen 1 rode on Toyota's New MC platform (RAV4 XA4-related) with Lexus-first manufacturing tricks: laser-screw welding, body adhesives, and hot-stamped reinforcements; it introduced Lexus's first turbocharged engine (8AR-FTS). Gen 2 (2022) moved to GA-K (TNGA-K), cutting over 200 lbs (350h vs. 300h) while adding the Lexus Interface touchscreen system, e-Latch doors, and Lexus's first-ever plug-in hybrid (450h+, shared with the RAV4 Prime powertrain).

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Seen as the entry point to the brand — bought for reliability and resale, not driving thrills. The 450h+ has a genuine following (quick, 37-mile EV range, holds value extremely well — iSeeCars ranks NX near the top of the class for retained value). The 200t/300 turbo cars are the least-loved used pick due to premium-fuel appetite.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Acura RDX, BMW X3, Mercedes GLC, Audi Q5, Volvo XC60. The NX 350 matches or beats the Germans on torque and undercuts on price; the 350h/450h+ crush them on fuel economy. It gives up rear-seat room and ultimate handling polish to the X3, but wins the reliability and cost-of-ownership argument decisively.

lexus-is.subtitle: Compact luxury sport sedan, RWD/AWD; single generation in scope: 3rd gen XE30 — 2015–2020 (incl. 2017 facelift) and 2021–2026 (major 2021 re-facelift, same platform)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2016: IS 250 = 2.5L 4GR-FSE NA V6 (204 hp) before discontinuation; IS 350 = 3.5L 2GR-FSE NA V6 (D-4S). 2016–2020: IS 200t (RWD, 2016–2017) = 2.0L 8AR-FTS twin-scroll turbo I4; IS 300 AWD = 2GR-FSE detuned (255 hp); IS 300 RWD (2018–2020) = 8AR-FTS; IS 350 = 2GR-FSE (306 hp). 2021–2026: IS 300 RWD = 8AR-FTS (241 hp); IS 300 AWD = 3.5L 2GR-FKS V6 (260 hp); IS 350 = 2GR-FSE (311 hp); IS 500 F Sport Performance (2022–2025+) = 5.0L 2UR-GSE NA V8.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 2015–2016 IS 250: 6-speed automatic. RWD models 2014+: 8-speed Sport Direct Shift automatic (AA81E family, paddle-shifted). All AWD models (300/350): 6-speed automatic. IS 500: 8-speed automatic, RWD only.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: RWD standard; AWD optional on IS 300 (mandatory on 300 AWD trim) and IS 350 throughout. IS 200t/300 RWD (turbo-4) = RWD only. IS 500 = RWD only. No manual offered in the US this era.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: 2015–2020 (EPA city/hwy/combined): IS 200t 22/33/26; IS 300 AWD 19/26/21; IS 350 RWD 19/28/22, AWD 19/26/21 (premium fuel recommended/required on turbo). 2021–2026: IS 300 RWD 21/31/25; IS 300 AWD 19/26/22; IS 350 RWD 20/28/23, AWD 19/26/22; IS 500 17/25/20.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Among the most dependable sport sedans built. The IS won its segment in the 2024 J.D. Power Vehicle Dependability Study (Lexus #1 brand, 135 PP100). The 2GR-FSE V6 is a proven high-mileage unit, and D-4S dual injection largely avoids the carbon-buildup problems of direct-injection-only rivals. NHTSA recall counts per model year are low; Takata airbag campaigns touch early (2014–2015) cars.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: Documented items: 8-speed automatic low-speed hesitation/shift-quality complaints addressed by ECM/TCM reflash TSBs (2014–2017 cars); auto-dimming mirror discoloration; clicking/popping from rear suspension (TSB-documented on 2020-era cars); occasional water-pump weep on 2GR-FSE at high mileage; check-engine P0751-type shift-solenoid codes reported on higher-mileage 8-speeds. IS 200t (8AR-FTS): covered by the same fuel-rail-pressure DTC/ECM TSB family as the NX. IS 500: no pattern failures verified — isolated rear-diff seal seepage reports on forums only.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil: 0W-20 full synthetic, 10,000 mi/12 mo (5,000 mi severe; IS 500 same interval, ~6.8 qt). Premium fuel: required on 8AR-FTS and IS 500, recommended on V6s. Spark plugs: 120,000 mi iridium. Coolant: SLLC pink, 100,000 mi then 50,000 mi. ATF: WS "lifetime" per guide — 60,000-mi drain-and-fill recommended on both the 8-speed and AWD 6-speed. Brake fluid: inspect; ~30,000 mi/3 yr dealer norm. All engines timing-chain (including 4GR/2GR/8AR/2UR). AWD: front transfer case and rear diff fluid service per severe schedule.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60 mph: IS 200t ≈6.9 s; IS 300 AWD ≈6.1 s; IS 350 RWD 5.5–5.6 s (C&D 5.5 s on early cars; Lexus 5.6 s for 2021+); IS 500 4.4 s (C&D tested 4.3 s, 12.8-s quarter @ 112 mph). Top speed 131–143 mph (IS 350), 149 mph (IS 500). F Sport package (sport suspension/AVS, LFA-style cluster) across the line; IS 500 F Sport Performance adds Torsen LSD, Yamaha rear performance dampers, 14.0-in two-piece front brakes, stacked-quad exhaust.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: One of the strongest Lexus aftermarket scenes: RR Racing tunes and their 2GR-FSE supercharger kit, PPE/ Sikky headers and exhaust, JB4 piggybacks for the 8AR-FTS, BC/Tein/Öhlins coilovers, 18–19-in flow-formed wheels, and the popular IS 500 big-brake retro-fit onto IS 350s. Factory F Sport accessories (springs, sway bars, aero) are dealer-installed. The 2021+ cars take well to mild tune + intake/exhaust on the 2UR-GSE.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The XE30 sits on Toyota's New N platform (double-wishbone front, multi-link rear) shared with the GS/RC. The 2017 facelift was cosmetic; the 2021 update was far deeper — new body panels front to rear, added spot welds and structural adhesive for rigidity, revised suspension with lighter components, Lexus Safety System+ 2.5, and a longer, wider stance on the unchanged 110.2-in wheelbase. The IS 500 is essentially an IS F drivetrain (2UR-GSE V8) re-engineered into the refreshed shell — the last naturally aspirated V8 compact sport sedan on sale.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The IS is the enthusiast's Lexus: the XE30 is respected as the reliable Japanese answer to the 3 Series, with the IS F's legacy keeping tuner interest high. The IS 500 became an instant cult car — NA V8, hydraulic-feel steering, no hybrid — and low-mileage examples already trade at or above MSRP. ClubLexus and the drift/track communities keep parts support deep.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: BMW 3 Series, Mercedes C-Class, Audi A4, Genesis G70, Acura TLX. The IS trails the Germans on cabin tech and outright chassis sophistication (especially pre-2021), but beats them decisively on reliability, maintenance cost and resale — and the IS 500 has no direct NA V8 rival left in the class. For customers prioritizing long-term cost, it's the class pick.

mercedes-c-class.subtitle: Compact executive sedan (coupe/cabriolet through 2023), 2015–2021 (W205) / 2022–2026 (W206). Coupe and convertible moved to the CLE nameplate for 2024+.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: - 2015–2018 (W205 pre-facelift): C300 = 2.0L M274 DE20 LA turbo I4 (241 hp); C400 = 3.0L M276 DE30 LA biturbo V6 (329 hp); C450 AMG (2016) = M276 uprated to 362 hp; AMG C63/C63 S = 4.0L M177 DE40 LA biturbo V8 (469/503 hp); C350e (2016–2018) = M274 + 87-hp motor PHEV. - 2019–2021 (W205 facelift; coupe/cabrio to 2023): C300 = 2.0L M264 E20 DEH LA turbo I4 (255 hp); AMG C43 = M276 3.0L biturbo V6 (385 hp); C63/C63 S unchanged (M177). - 2022–2026 (W206): C300 = 2.0L M254 turbo I4 + 48V ISG mild hybrid (255 hp); AMG C43 (2023+) = 2.0L M139 turbo I4 + 48V, electric exhaust-gas turbo (402 hp US rating); AMG C63 S E Performance (2024+) = 2.0L M139L turbo I4 (469 hp engine alone) + 201-hp rear-axle motor PHEV (671 hp combined).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: - 2015–2018: 7-speed 7G-Tronic Plus (722.9) on C300/C400/C450; 9-speed 9G-Tronic (725.0) phased in 2017–2018; C350e used 7G-Tronic hybrid variant; AMG C63 = AMG SPEEDSHIFT MCT 7-speed. - 2019–2021: 9G-Tronic standard; AMG C43 = AMG SPEEDSHIFT TCT 9-speed; C63 = AMG SPEEDSHIFT MCT 9-speed. - 2022–2026: 9G-Tronic on C300; AMG SPEEDSHIFT MCT 9-speed on C43/C63 S.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: - W205: RWD or 4MATIC AWD on C300 (4MATIC standard on early US launch cars; RWD available from ~2015+); C400/C450/C43 = 4MATIC only; C63/C63 S = RWD only. - W206: US C300 = 4MATIC; C43 = 4MATIC (31/69 rear-biased permanent AWD); C63 S E Performance = AMG Performance 4MATIC+ (variable 0–50% front axle, RWD-biased).

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: - W205 sedan: L 184.5 in / W 71.3 in / H 56.8 in / WB 111.8 in; curb ≈3,605 lb (2019 C300 4MATIC sedan spec sheet); AMG C63 ≈3,900+ lb (exact per-trim weights — Not verified — needs OEM source). - W206 sedan: L 187.0 in / W 71.7 in / H 56.6 in / WB 112.8 in; curb ≈3,880 lb (2023 C300, Edmunds).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: - 2018 C300 RWD: 24/33/27; C300 4MATIC: 23/32/26; C350e PHEV: 51 MPGe / 30 MPG gas-only; AMG C43 sedan: 20/27/23; AMG C63: 18/24/20 (fueleconomy.gov). - 2022 C300: 25/35/29; C300 4MATIC: 23/33/27; AMG C43 (carryover coupe/cabrio): 19/28/22 (fueleconomy.gov). - 2019–2021 M264 figures and 2023+ C43/C63 S E Performance EPA ratings — Not verified — needs OEM source.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Consumer Reports expects the 2025 C-Class at "about average" reliability (brand score-based). Mercedes-Benz brand scored 243 PP100 in the 2025 J.D. Power US Vehicle Dependability Study (industry average 202). W205 earned "Good" in all IIHS crash categories (2020 model); W206 rated "Good" across IIHS tests (2024 model). NHTSA lists 34 unique recall campaigns touching the 2015–2025 C300 line (spanning both generations; includes Takata-era and multi-model campaigns).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: - M274 2.0T piston cracking — documented in class-action lawsuits and MB TSB LI03.10-P-060916 ("unfavorable tolerance of the piston wrist pin"); affects 2015–2018 C300 and other M274/M270 cars; a 2015 C300 M274 case also appears in a NHTSA defect petition file (govinfo.gov). - 9G-Tronic (725.0) fully-integrated transmission control unit (Y3/8) faults — limp mode, harsh engagement; TSB-documented (TSB 11636 coverage). - 7G-Tronic (722.9) valve-body/conductor-plate failures (limp mode, delayed reverse) — long-documented on pre-2018 cars. - M276 (C400/C43) camshaft-adjuster and timing-chain-tensioner cold-start rattle (oil drains from adjuster passages). - Recalls: EPS software shutdown (15V845000, 16V438000, 2015–2016); panoramic roof panel bonding (21V197000 / 23V854000, 2014–2020); 2022 W206 trunk moisture into SAM (22V678000); 2022–2023 transmission wiring harness wrong length (23V462000).

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: ASSYST flexible service — Service A and Service B alternate every ~10,000 miles/1 year (oil + filter, MB 229.5/229.51-approved synthetic). Brake fluid every 2 years (universal MB spec). All engines above are timing-chain — no belt service. Coolant is long-life MB 325.x (MB data sheets list very long intervals, ~15 years/250k km class). Spark plugs and 9G-Tronic fluid intervals — Not verified — needs OEM source (dealer practice: plugs ~50–60k miles; 9G-Tronic fluid/filter ~60k miles; MBUSA markets the gearbox as fill-for-life — confirm against the model-year maintenance booklet).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: C300 (M264) ≈5.9 s 0–100 km/h manufacturer claim; C43 (2016) 4.9 s; C63 S 3.9 s 0–100 km/h; W206 C43 4.6 s; C63 S E Performance 3.4 s 0–100 km/h (manufacturer claims). Performance trims: C43, C63, C63 S; 2017–2018 AMG Performance Exhaust option (muted from 2019 due to noise regs).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Very active tuning scene: BMS JB4 piggyback for M274 C300 (+50 whp claimed) and M276 C43 (+70 whp claimed); ECU flashes (AMR and others) for M274/M264/M177; downpipes and valved exhausts are the most common hardware mods; W205-to-AMG bumper conversions and VLAND-style lighting are popular cosmetics. M177 C63 responds well to Stage 1/2 tunes and downpipes.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: W205 was the first car on Mercedes' MRA (Modular Rear Architecture) platform, cutting ~100 kg vs W204 via extensive aluminum; four-link front / five-link rear suspension. W206 moves to MRA II (shared with W223 S-Class), goes all-four-cylinder with a 48V integrated starter-generator standard, and offers rear-axle steering (2.5°). The W206 C63's M139L makes 469 hp from 2.0L — the highest-output production four-cylinder.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The W205 C63/C63 S is the cult car — the last V8 C-Class, with strong aftermarket support and a loyal following that resents the W206 C63's four-cylinder PHEV swap (heavy but fast). C43s are popular daily-tuner platforms; the base C300 is viewed as a solid but unremarkable tune candidate.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: BMW 3 Series (F30/G20), Audi A4 (B9), Lexus IS. The C-Class is usually judged the most luxurious-riding of the German trio but trails the 3 Series in steering feel; reliability is mid-pack — the Lexus IS is the durability pick, while the C300's M274 piston issue and 9G-Tronic quirks drag its record below the BMW B46/B58 cars.

mercedes-e-class.subtitle: Executive sedan/wagon, 2015–2016 (W212 facelift) / 2017–2023 (W213) / 2024–2026 (W214). Coupe/cabriolet (C238/A238, 2018–2023) moved to CLE for 2024+.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: - 2015–2016 (W212): E250 BlueTEC = 2.1L OM651 DE22 LA twin-turbo diesel I4 (195 hp); E350 = 3.5L M276 DE35 naturally aspirated V6 (302 hp); E400 = 3.0L M276 biturbo V6 (329 hp); E550 = 4.7L M278 DE46 AL biturbo V8 (402 hp); E63 AMG = 5.5L M157 DE55 LA biturbo V8 (550 hp; S-Model 577 hp). - 2017–2023 (W213): E300 = 2.0L M274 turbo I4 (241 hp, 2017–2019); E350 = 2.0L M264 turbo I4 (255 hp, 2020–2023); E400 = 3.0L M276 biturbo V6 (329 hp, 2017–2018); E450 = M276 (362 hp, 2019–2020) then 3.0L M256 E30 DEH LA G turbo I6 + 48V EQ Boost (362 hp, 2021–2023); AMG E43 = M276 (396 hp, 2017–2018); AMG E53 = M256 I6 (429 hp, 2019–2023); AMG E63 S = 4.0L M177 biturbo V8 (603 hp, 2018–2023); E350e PHEV = M274 + 87-hp motor. - 2024–2026 (W214): E350 4MATIC = 2.0L M254 turbo I4 + 48V ISG (255 hp); E450 4MATIC = 3.0L M256 turbo I6 + 48V ISG; AMG E53 HYBRID 4MATIC+ (2025+) = M256 I6 + 161-hp motor PHEV (577 hp combined; 604 hp with RACE START).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: - W212: 7-speed 7G-Tronic Plus (722.9); E63 = AMG SPEEDSHIFT MCT 7-speed. - W213: 9-speed 9G-Tronic (725.0) standard; AMG E53 = AMG SPEEDSHIFT TCT 9-speed; E63 = AMG SPEEDSHIFT MCT 9-speed. - W214: 9G-Tronic on all.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: - W212: RWD standard; 4MATIC optional on E350/E400/E550, standard on E63 S (US); E250 BlueTEC RWD or 4MATIC. - W213: E300/E350 RWD or 4MATIC; E400/E450 = 4MATIC; E53/E63 S = 4MATIC+ (E63 S has drift mode decoupling the front axle). - W214: US E350/E450 = 4MATIC; E53 = 4MATIC+.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: - 2021 E350 RWD: 22/31/25; E350 4MATIC: 22/30/25; E450 4MATIC: 23/30/26; AMG E53: 22/29/25; AMG E63 S: 16/23/18 (fueleconomy.gov). - 2024 E350 4MATIC: 24/33/27; E450 4MATIC: 22/31/25 (fueleconomy.gov). - W212 (2015–2016 E250 BlueTEC/E350/E400/E550) and 2017–2019 E300/E400 figures — Not verified — needs OEM source (check fueleconomy.gov by model year).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: W213 won MotorTrend 2021 Car of the Year and was a Car and Driver 10Best pick (2019); facelift W212 earned IIHS Top Safety Pick+ (2014). W214 scored 92% adult-occupant / 5-star Euro NCAP (2024). Brand-level: Mercedes 243 PP100 in the 2025 J.D. Power US VDS (below the 202 average). NHTSA unique campaigns: E350 14 (2015–2025), E300 17 (2017–2019), E450 16 (2019–2025).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: - OM651 2.1 diesel (E250 BlueTEC): Delphi piezo injector leaks on early builds (MB service campaign replaced them with solenoid injectors); hydraulic timing-chain tensioner fails well before the chain itself (labor-intensive repair — drive is at the gearbox end); cracked plastic intake manifold; oil dilution from a clogged heat exchanger or failed variable-displacement oil pump can spin bearings; switchable water pump/thermostat faults set warm-up CELs. - M276 cam-adjuster/timing-chain-tensioner cold-start rattle (same as C-Class). - 9G-Tronic Y3/8 control-unit faults (limp mode, harsh shifts). - 48V ground cable recall 24V100000 (2021–2023 E450/E53 — overheating risk); 2024 W214 transmission wiring harness routed too close to driveshaft (24V224000); W213 Airmatic strut/compressor wear on air-suspension cars (age/mileage item, documented across MB air-suspension platforms).

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Service A/B alternating every ~10,000 miles/1 year (ASSYST). Brake fluid every 2 years. All listed engines are timing-chain (OM651 uses chain + gears at the transmission end). E250 diesel: also plan for AdBlue/DEF refills and DPF/SCR upkeep; use MB 229.51/229.52 low-SAPS oil. Coolant long-life MB 325.x. Spark plug and 9G-Tronic fluid intervals — Not verified — needs OEM source (dealer practice: plugs ~50–60k miles on DI gas engines; 9G-Tronic fluid ~60k miles).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: E300 ≈6.2 s 0–100 km/h (manufacturer); E400 4.9–5.3 s; E450 (M256) ≈5.1 s; E53 ≈4.5 s; E63 S (W213) ≈3.4 s; W212 E63 S 3.5–3.6 s; W214 E53 HYBRID 3.7 s with RACE START (manufacturer claims). Performance trims: E43 → E53 → E63 S; W213 E63 S drift mode; W212 E63 S-Model was the first US 4MATIC E63.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: E63/E63 S M177 tuning is the big scene (Stage 1 flashes, downpipes); M256 E53s take well to ECU tunes; W212 E63 M157 cars have a strong used-tuner following. Cosmetic AMG-line conversions and lowering links for Airmatic cars are common. Base E300/E350 tuning is limited mostly to piggybacks.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: W212 closed out the pre-MRA era with a €1B facelift that fused the classic four-lamp face into single housings and added stereo-camera driver assists. W213 moved to MRA with a 0.23 Cd body, dual 12.3-in widescreen cockpit, and the brand's switch from V6 to the M256 inline-6 with 48V EQ Boost. W214 adds MBUX Superscreen, optional 4.5° rear-axle steering, and standard mild-hybridization across the range.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The W212 E63 (M157) and W213 E63 S wagon are the enthusiast picks — the wagon especially has cult status as a 603-hp family hauler. The E350/E450 are respected as durable highway cars, with the M256 widely regarded as one of Mercedes' best modern engines.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: BMW 5 Series (F10/G30), Audi A6 (C7/C8), Genesis G80, Lexus GS/ES. The E-Class typically leads on cabin tech and ride isolation; the 5 Series is the driver's pick. On reliability the E-Class trails the Lexus and roughly matches the A6; the OM651 diesel and early 9G-Tronic/48V electrical issues are its main demerits.

mercedes-glc.subtitle: Compact luxury SUV (GLK successor; SUV + Coupe SUV), 2016–2022 (X253/C253) / 2023–2026 (X254/C254). No 2015 GLC in the US — the 2015 slot was the GLK.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: - 2016–2019 (X253 pre-facelift): GLC300 = 2.0L M274 turbo I4 (241 hp); GLC350e (2018–2019) = M274 + 87-hp motor PHEV; AMG GLC43 (2017+) = 3.0L M276 biturbo V6 (362 hp); AMG GLC63 (2018+) = 4.0L M177 biturbo V8 (469 hp); GLC63 S = M177 (503 hp). - 2020–2022 (X253 facelift): GLC300 = 2.0L M264 turbo I4 (255 hp); GLC43 = M276 (385 hp); GLC63/63 S unchanged. - 2023–2026 (X254): GLC300 4MATIC = 2.0L M254 turbo I4 + 48V ISG (255 hp); GLC350e (2025+) = M254 PHEV (313 hp combined); AMG GLC43 (2024+) = 2.0L M139 turbo I4 + 48V e-turbo (416 hp); AMG GLC63 S E Performance (2024+) = M139L + PHEV (671 hp combined).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: 9-speed 9G-Tronic (725.0; W9A 500/700 internals) on all gasoline X253/X254 models; GLC350e (X253) used a 7G-Tronic hybrid variant; AMG GLC43/63 (X254) = AMG SPEEDSHIFT MCT 9-speed.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: GLC300 (M274): 241 hp @ 5,550. GLC300 (M264): 255 hp @ 5,800. GLC300 (M254): 255 hp @ 5,800 (+23-hp ISG). GLC350e (X253): 315–320 hp combined (manufacturer; exact US rating — Not verified — needs OEM source). GLC43: 362 hp @ 5,550 (2017–2019) / 385 hp @ 6,100 (2020–2022) / 416 hp @ 6,750 (X254). GLC63: 469 hp; GLC63 S: 503 hp @ 5,500. GLC350e (X254): 313 hp combined. GLC63 S E Performance: 671 hp combined.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: X253 US: GLC300 RWD or 4MATIC; GLC350e/GLC43/GLC63 = 4MATIC/4MATIC+. X254 US: GLC300 = 4MATIC standard; GLC43 = rear-biased 4MATIC (31/69 split); GLC63 S = 4MATIC+.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: - X253 SUV: L 183.3 in / W 74.4 in / H 64.5 in / WB 113.1 in; curb ≈3,947 lb. - X254 SUV: L 185.7 in / W 74.4 in / H 64.6 in / WB 113.7 in; US curb weight — Not verified — needs OEM source.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: - 2021 GLC300 RWD: 22/29/25; GLC300 4MATIC: 21/28/23; GLC43: 18/24/20; GLC63/63 S: 15/22/17 (fueleconomy.gov). - 2024 GLC300 4MATIC: 24/32/27; AMG GLC43: 19/25/21 (fueleconomy.gov). - 2016–2019 X253 figures and GLC350e/GLC63 S E Performance ratings — Not verified — needs OEM source.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: The X253 GLC won its segment (Compact Premium SUV) in the 2025 J.D. Power US Vehicle Dependability Study (2022 model year) — one of the strongest Mercedes results on record; UK trade press rates the X253 as one of the most dependable used Mercedes SUVs. Brand overall: 243 PP100 in the same 2025 VDS. X253 earned 5-star Euro NCAP (2015, 95% adult). NHTSA: 21 unique campaigns touching GLC300 (2016–2025).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: - Front-tire "crabbing"/judder at full lock in cold, wet conditions (Ackermann geometry + OE tire compound) — widely documented on X253; Mercedes' fix in several markets was revised steering knuckles plus all-season tires (owner-club documentation, UK/AU). - M274 piston cracking on 2016–2019 GLC300 (same TSB LI03.10-P-060916 family issue as the C300). - M276 GLC43 cam-adjuster/chain-tensioner cold-start rattle. - Recalls: 2021 headlight connector not watertight (22V261000); 2023 front tow-eye threading (23V629000); 2024 window bonding/airbag support (23V741000); M254 high-pressure fuel pump screw sealing (23V878000, 2023); panoramic roof bonding campaign (23V854000) also covers X253. - 9G-Tronic Y3/8 control-unit faults (shared with C/E-Class).

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Service A/B alternating every ~10,000 miles/1 year. Brake fluid every 2 years. All engines timing-chain. Note for the shop: staggered OE tire setups and the crabbing complaint make tire brand/model matching per axle important — mismatched tread depths aggravate the transfer case. Spark plug / 9G-Tronic fluid intervals — Not verified — needs OEM source (dealer practice ~50–60k miles each).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: GLC300 ≈6.2–6.6 s 0–100 km/h; GLC43 4.9 s (X253) / 4.8 s (X254); GLC63 S 3.8 s; GLC63 S E Performance 3.5 s (manufacturer claims). Performance trims: GLC43, GLC63, GLC63 S (SUV and Coupe).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: GLC43 M276 tunes and downpipes are common; GLC63 M177 Stage 1/2 packages mirror the C63 scene. Lowering springs/links and 20–21-in wheel swaps are the typical cosmetic route. X254 M139 GLC43 tuning is emerging (ECU flashes already marketed).

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: X253 is the MRA-platform SUV sibling of the W205 C-Class, sharing its powertrains and 9G-Tronic; 4MATIC with a 45/55 planetary center diff on standard cars. X254 moves to MRA II with standard 48V mild-hybrid M254, larger body (+60 mm length), and — on AMGs — the switch from V6/V8 to the M139/M139L four-cylinder hybrid powertrains; US production at Tuscaloosa begins with the 2027 model year.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The X253 GLC63 S is the enthusiast favorite — a 503-hp V8 in a compact SUV footprint, frequently cross-shopped with the Porsche Macan GTS/Turbo. The GLC300 is the volume service car; owners mostly leave it stock. Crabbing complaints dominate forum tech talk on 4MATIC cars.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: BMW X3, Audi Q5, Porsche Macan, Volvo XC60. The GLC splits the difference between the sportier X3/Macan and the comfort-oriented Q5/XC60; its 2025 J.D. Power segment dependability win gives it a stronger late-model reliability story than the X3 or Q5, though early M274 cars carry the piston risk.

mercedes-gle.subtitle: Mid-size luxury SUV (M-Class/ML renamed GLE at the 2015 facelift), 2016–2019 (W166 facelift; Coupe C292) / 2020–2026 (W167; Coupe C167). Built in Tuscaloosa, AL. The 2015 slot was the W166 ML-Class.

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: - 2016–2019 (W166): GLE350 = 3.5L M276 DE35 naturally aspirated V6 (302 hp); GLE400 = 3.0L M276 biturbo V6 (329 hp); GLE550e (2016–2018) = M276 biturbo + 114-hp motor PHEV; AMG GLE43 (2017–2019) = M276 (362 hp, 385 hp from 2019); AMG GLE63 = 5.5L M157 biturbo V8 (518 hp per manufacturer data; US marketing listed 550 hp — confirm by VIN); GLE63 S = M157 (577 hp). GLE300d (2016) = 2.1L OM651 diesel I4 (201 hp). - 2020–2023 (W167 pre-facelift): GLE350 = 2.0L M264 turbo I4 (255 hp); GLE450 = 3.0L M256 turbo I6 + 48V EQ Boost (362 hp); GLE580 = 4.0L M176 biturbo V8 + EQ Boost (483 hp); AMG GLE53 = M256 (429 hp); AMG GLE63 S = 4.0L M177 biturbo V8 + EQ Boost (603 hp). - 2024–2026 (W167 facelift): GLE350 (255 hp) and GLE450 (M256, 381 hp/386 PS per manufacturer data — US MBUSA may list 375 hp, confirm by VIN); GLE450e = M254-based PHEV (381 hp combined); GLE580 (510 hp); AMG GLE53 (434 hp/441 PS); GLE63 S (603 hp). A second facelift was announced for 2027MY (March 2026).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: - W166: 7-speed 7G-Tronic (722.9) on GLE350/GLE400/GLE550e; 9G-Tronic on GLE43; AMG GLE63/S = AMG SPEEDSHIFT Plus 7G-Tronic (MCT). - W167: 9-speed 9G-Tronic (725.0) across the range; AMG variants use AMG SPEEDSHIFT TCT/MCT 9-speed.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: W166: GLE350 RWD or 4MATIC; all others 4MATIC (GLE63/63 S 4MATIC). W167: GLE350 RWD or 4MATIC; GLE450/580 = 4MATIC; GLE53/GLE63 S = 4MATIC+ (variable, RWD-biased). Optional 3rd-row seat new to GLE on W167.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: - W166 GLE: L 189.1 in / W 75.8 in / H 70.7 in / WB 114.8 in; curb ≈4,740 lb. - W167 GLE: L 193.9–194.1 in / W 79.3 in / H 70.7 in / WB 117.9 in; curb ≈4,696 lb (base; AMG/PHEV higher — Not verified per trim — needs OEM source).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: - 2018 GLE350 RWD: 18/23/20; GLE350 4MATIC: 18/22/19; GLE550e PHEV: 43 MPGe / 21 MPG gas-only; AMG GLE43: 17/23/19; AMG GLE63/S: 14/18/15 (fueleconomy.gov). - 2021 GLE350 (RWD & 4MATIC): 19/26/22; GLE450 4MATIC: 21/26/23; GLE580 4MATIC: 17/22/19; AMG GLE53: 18/22/19; AMG GLE63 S: 15/19/16 (fueleconomy.gov). - 2024+ facelift figures (incl. GLE450e) — Not verified — needs OEM source.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: W167 was an IIHS Top Safety Pick+ (2020, vehicles built after July 2019); 5-star Euro NCAP (2019, 91% adult). RepairPal rates the W167 GLE 3.5/5 with ~$807/yr average repair cost. Brand-level: 243 PP100 in 2025 J.D. Power VDS. NHTSA: 26 unique campaigns touching GLE350 (2016–2025) and 15 on GLE450 (2020–2025) — the W167's early build years were recall-heavy.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: - W167: AIRMATIC strut piston-seal recall on certain 2020 builds (20V329000); AIRMATIC fault messages from control-unit software — TSB LI32.22-P-073368 (software fix, not strut replacement); 48V ground cable under passenger seat may loosen → overheating/fire risk, 116,000+ 2019–2024 GLE/GLS recalled (24V207000, March 2024); steering coupling locking screw may loosen (22V936000, 2021–2022); accelerator pedal bracket weld (24V117000, 2024); A/C drain-hose water intrusion into front footwell (2018–2021 builds); "48V Battery Malfunction" warnings/ISG faults on EQ Boost cars; MBUX reboot/startup glitches (2019–2021 software). - W166: water accumulation in the spare-tire well damaging the fuel-pump control module — recall 22V955000 (2016–2019 GLE, plus ML); Continental tire over-cure recall (21V123000, 2018); OM651 diesel (GLE300d) shares the E250 BlueTEC injector/chain-tensioner issues; AIRMATIC air-spring leaks and compressor burnout are the classic age/mileage items on both generations (compressor typically dies after air springs start leaking).

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Service A/B alternating every ~10,000 miles/1 year. Brake fluid every 2 years. All engines timing-chain. On W167 EQ Boost cars, treat 48V battery/ISG warnings as electrical-diagnosis jobs first (ground-cable recall + software). AIRMATIC cars: inspect air springs before condemning the compressor. Spark plug / transmission fluid intervals — Not verified — needs OEM source (dealer practice: plugs ~50–60k; 9G-Tronic fluid ~60k).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: GLE350 (W167) ≈7.6 s 0–100 km/h; GLE450 5.6–5.7 s; GLE580 4.6–4.9 s; GLE53 5.0–5.3 s; GLE63 S 3.8 s (manufacturer claims). W166 GLE63 S was among the quickest SUVs of its era (~4.2 s class). Performance trims: GLE43 → GLE53 → GLE63 S; W167 GLE450 offered E-Active Body Control (camera-scanned, per-wheel active damping).

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: GLE63 S (both M157 and M177) gets the usual AMG treatment — Stage 1/2 ECU tunes, downpipes, 22–23-in wheels. GLE53 M256 tunes are growing. Air-suspension lowering links and calibration tweaks are common on AIRMATIC/E-ABC cars. Off-road mods are rare; this is a street-tuned platform.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: The W166 was the last GLE on the DaimlerChrysler-derived platform shared with the Jeep Grand Cherokee (WK2)/Dodge Durango; the GLE badge arrived with its 2015 facelift plus the new C292 Coupe. W167 is a clean-sheet design: 80 mm longer wheelbase, first 7-seat GLE option, 48V EQ Boost across the range, and available E-Active Body Control that replaces mechanical anti-roll bars. Facelift (2024MY) added the GLE450e PHEV and restyled lighting; a further facelift follows for 2027MY.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Enthusiasts prize the W166 GLE63 S Coupe as an X6 M rival and the W167 GLE63 S as one of the last big V8 SUVs. The GLE450's M256 is considered the sweet spot — tunable, smooth, and one of MB's most respected modern engines. The four-cylinder GLE350 is seen as the fleet/value play.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: BMW X5, Audi Q7, Porsche Cayenne, Volvo XC90, Genesis GV80. The GLE matches the X5 on pace and beats it on ride comfort (E-ABC), but early W167 build quality/recall volume trails the X5's record; the Cayenne out-drives it at a price premium; the GV80 undercuts it on value. Alabama build quality on 2019–2021 W167s is a known talking point among techs.

nissan-altima.subtitle: Midsize sedan; 2015–2018 (L33, 5th gen; facelift for 2016), 2019–2026 (L34, 6th gen; VC-Turbo dropped after 2024, 2.5L only for 2025–2026)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: L33: 2.5L QR25DE NA I4 (port injection, 9.6:1); 3.5L VQ35DE NA V6 (10.3:1). L34: 2.5L PR25DD NA I4 (direct injection, 12.0:1); 2.0L KR20DDET VC-Turbo turbo I4 (world-first variable compression, 8.0:1–14.0:1, DI+port), 2019–2024 only.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: All years: Xtronic CVT only (Jatco CVT8 family; JF016E-type on 4-cyl, JF017E-type on V6). No manual or conventional automatic offered 2015–2026. CVT retuned with D-Step shift logic for 2016+.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: L33: 2.5L 182 hp @ 6,000 rpm (2015), re-rated 179 hp @ 6,000 rpm (2016–2018); 3.5L 270 hp @ 6,000–6,400 rpm. L34: 2.5L FWD 188 hp @ 6,000 rpm; 2.5L AWD 182 hp @ 6,000 rpm; 2.0L VC-Turbo 248 hp @ 5,600 rpm on premium (236 hp on regular).

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: L33: 2.5L 180 lb-ft @ 4,000 rpm (2015), 177 lb-ft (2016–2018); 3.5L 251 lb-ft @ 4,400 rpm. L34: 2.5L FWD 180 lb-ft @ 3,600 rpm; 2.5L AWD 178 lb-ft @ 3,600 rpm; VC-Turbo 273 lb-ft @ 4,000 rpm on premium (267 lb-ft regular).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: L33: FWD only. L34: FWD standard; Intelligent AWD optional with 2.5L (first AWD Altima); VC-Turbo FWD only.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: L33: length 191.5 in / width 72.0 in / height 57.9–58.1 in / wheelbase 109.3 in / curb 3,108–3,355 lb. L34: length 192.9 in / width 72.9 in / height 56.8–57.4 in / wheelbase 111.2 in / curb 3,212–3,459 lb (KBB/press data by trim).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: L33 is defined by CVT failures — 2013–2016 Altima covered by a federal class-action settlement with CVT warranty extension (up to 7 yr/84,000 mi). 2013–2018 Altimas recalled four times for secondary hood-latch corrosion (campaign 20V315, ~1.8M vehicles). L34 2.5 (PR25DD) has been comparatively trouble-free; L34 VC-Turbos (2019–2020) are included in NHTSA recall 25V437000 (443,899 vehicles, engine-bearing manufacturing defects).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: Jatco CVT judder, overheating and premature failure (L33; class action + warranty extension). QR25DE oil consumption at mileage (NHTSA complaints, Nissan TSBs on piston rings). Hood-latch corrosion (recalls 2014–2020). VC-Turbo main/A-link/C-link/L-link bearing failures (recall 25V437000, June 2025). CVT complaints persist on L34 but at much lower rates.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 5,000 mi/6 mo (severe 3,750 mi); manual schedule calls tire rotation at 5,000 mi (2.5L). Iridium spark plugs ~105,000 mi. Long-life coolant ~105,000 mi/7 yr first change. CVT fluid (NS-2/NS-3): no fixed normal-service interval — inspect; shop best practice is drain/fill every 60,000 mi (severe-service schedule). Brake fluid replacement every ~2 yr per Nissan schedule. All engines timing chain (no belt service).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: L33 2.5 ~7.8 sec, L33 3.5 V6 ~6.2 sec (Edmunds instrumented tests); VC-Turbo ~6.5 sec (automobile-catalog estimate); L34 2.5 0–60 — Not verified — needs OEM/publication source. No factory performance trim; SR adds sport-tuned suspension/paddles only.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Light scene — bolt-ons (intake, cat-back) from Stillen/Megan Racing, lowering springs/coilovers, wheel/tire packages. VC-Turbo ECU tuning is limited by CVT torque capacity; no meaningful turbo upgrade path. 3.5 SR (L33) is the "sleeper" pick.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: L33 rides on Nissan's D platform with a high-strength-steel unibody and aluminum hood; 2016 facelift brought aero work (active grille shutters) and the retuned CVT. L34 moved to a new, stiffer platform, added available AWD, ProPILOT Assist, and the world-first production variable-compression engine (multi-link actuator varies piston stroke).

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Regarded as an appliance sedan, not an enthusiast platform; the L33 3.5 V6 has a small sleeper following. Tuner support is thin and concentrated on cosmetic/chassis parts; the CVT discourages power builds.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda Accord, Toyota Camry, Mazda6, Hyundai Sonata, Kia K5. Altima typically undercuts on price and matches on space/MPG, but the CVT's reliability record (L33) and resale lag the Accord/Camry; driving feel is mid-pack — quieter and softer than a Mazda6, less refined powertrain than the 2018+ Accord 2.0T.

nissan-rogue.subtitle: Compact crossover; 2015–2020 (T32; facelift 2017, Hybrid 2017–2019), 2021–2026 (T33; 2.5L for 2021, 1.5L VC-Turbo from 2022; 2024 tech refresh; Rock Creek trim added 2025+)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: T32: 2.5L QR25DE NA I4 (port injection). T32 Hybrid: 2.0L MR20DD + 30 kW electric motor (net 176 hp), 2017–2019. T33: 2.5L PR25DD NA DI I4 (2021 only); 1.5L KR15DDT VC-Turbo turbo I3 (variable compression, 2022–2026).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: All years: Xtronic CVT (Jatco CVT8 family; EPA designation AV-S8 on T33). Hybrid uses CVT with clutch-pack (no torque converter). No manual.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: T32 2.5: 170 hp @ 6,000 rpm. T32 Hybrid: 176 hp net. T33 2.5: 181 hp. T33 1.5 VC-Turbo: 201 hp.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD standard, Intelligent AWD optional on all trims, all generations. Hybrid offered FWD and AWD.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: T32: length 184.5 in / width 72.4 in / height 66.3–68.5 in / wheelbase 106.5 in. T33: length 183.0 in / width 72.4 in / height 66.5–66.9 in / wheelbase 106.5 in. Curb weight: Not verified — needs OEM source (published figures cluster ~3,400–3,700 lb depending on trim/drivetrain).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: T32 FWD 26/33/29, AWD 25/32/27; Hybrid FWD 33/35/34, AWD 31/34/33 (fueleconomy.gov, 2017). T33 2.5 FWD 27/35/30 (CNET/Nissan). T33 1.5T FWD 30/37/33 (SL/Platinum 29/36/32), AWD 28/35/31 (SL/Platinum 28/34/31) (fueleconomy.gov, 2023).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: T32: CVT class action (Stringer v. Nissan, 2014–2018 Rogue) with powertrain warranty extension to 7 yr/84,000 mi. T33: ~1,304 NHTSA complaints and 19 recall campaigns across 2021–2025 (The Weekly Driver tally) — one of the highest complaint rates in segment, dominated by the 1.5L VC-Turbo: recall 25V437000 (June 2025, engine bearings, 2021–2024), Feb 2026 recall (2023–2025, oil-breakdown bearing seizure), plus a 2024–2025 throttle-body gear recall and airbag-inflator recall 24V154000 (2023 Rogue).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: T32: CVT judder/failure (class action); AEB false-activation complaints on 2017–2018 models (NHTSA-documented). T33: VC-Turbo engine-bearing failures (two NHTSA recalls + NHTSA investigation), high engine/cooling/fuel-system complaint counts; electronic throttle-body gear failures (recall). The 2021-only PR25DD 2.5L is the lowest-risk T33 powertrain.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 5,000 mi/6 mo (severe 3,750 mi). Iridium plugs ~105,000 mi. Long-life coolant first change ~105,000 mi. CVT fluid NS-3 — inspect; 60,000-mi drain/fill is shop best practice, especially on T32. Brake fluid ~every 2 yr per Nissan schedule. All engines timing chain.

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: T32 2.5 low-9-sec range (publication tests) — exact figure Not verified — needs OEM/publication source; T33 1.5T ~8 sec (publication tests) — Not verified precisely. Rock Creek (2025+) is an appearance/off-road-trim package, not a power upgrade.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Minimal performance scene — mostly wheels/tires, roof racks, mild lift/level kits on T32, hitch and overlanding accessories. No meaningful engine tuning market (CVT + small turbo).

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: T32 moved Rogue onto the Renault-Nissan CMF-CD architecture, adding a third-row option (2014–2017) and the short-lived Hybrid. T33 rides a new CMF platform with Vehicle Motion Control (chassis-integrated brake/torque control), a stronger body, and the segment-unique 1.5L variable-compression three-cylinder. 2024 refresh added Google built-in infotainment.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Pure family appliance in the community's eyes — valued for space, seats and safety tech, ignored by tuners. The rare Hybrid has a small fuel-sipper following. T33's VC-Turbo is viewed with skepticism after the bearing recalls.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota RAV4, Honda CR-V, Mazda CX-5, Hyundai Tucson. Rogue matches them on space and value and beats some on MPG (1.5T), but its powertrain durability record is now the segment's weakest — RAV4/CR-V hybrids are the low-risk alternatives; CX-5 is the driver's pick.

nissan-sentra.subtitle: Compact sedan; 2015–2019 (B17; facelift 2016, SR Turbo/NISMO 2017–2019), 2020–2025 (B18), 2026 (all-new 9th gen)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: B17: 1.8L MRA8DE NA I4; 1.6L MR16DDT turbo I4 (SR Turbo, NISMO). B18 & 2026: 2.0L MR20DD NA DI I4.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: B17: 6-speed manual (S, SR Turbo, NISMO) or Xtronic CVT (Jatco CVT7). B18: Xtronic CVT (6MT on 2020 S only, then dropped). 2026: retuned Xtronic CVT only, with new Sport mode (SV/SR/SL).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: B17 1.8: 130 hp (124 hp in some CVT ratings). B17 1.6T: 188 hp. B18/2026 2.0: 149 hp @ 6,400 rpm.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: FWD only, all generations. No AWD Sentra sold in the US.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: B17: length 182.1 in / width 69.3 in / height 58.9 in / wheelbase 106.3 in / curb 2,848–2,943 lb (1.8 CVT; turbo/NISMO trims heavier). B18: length 182.7 in / width 71.5 in / height 56.9 in / wheelbase 106.8 in / curb 3,036–3,084 lb. 2026 (9th gen): Not verified — needs OEM source (dimensions not yet published in spec-sheet form).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: B17: 2013–2017 Sentra CVT covered by class-action litigation (Martinez v. Nissan, final approval 2023) with CVT warranty extensions. B18: materially better — Sentra ranked top Compact Car in the J.D. Power 2025 U.S. Initial Quality Study (per Nissan). NHTSA complaint volume on B18 is low; airbag-inflator recall 24V154000 covers 2023–2024 Sentra.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: B17: CVT judder/slipping/failure (class action); premature CVT wear is the dominant shop ticket on 2013–2017 cars. B18: high-mileage timing-chain stretch and CVT longevity are flagged by independent engine analysts (owner-mechanic reports, not TSB-level) — treat as watch items, not confirmed defects. 2026: too new for meaningful data.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Oil/filter 5,000 mi/6 mo (severe 3,750 mi). Iridium plugs ~105,000 mi (MR20DD; MR16DDT similar long-life plug). Coolant long-life ~105,000 mi first change. CVT fluid NS-2/NS-3 — 60,000-mi drain/fill recommended on B17 given CVT history. Brake fluid ~every 2 yr. All engines timing chain (interference).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 2026 Sentra 8.7 sec, 1/4-mile 16.7 @ 86 mph (MotorWeek instrumented test). B17 SR Turbo/NISMO 0–60: Not verified — needs OEM/publication source (publication estimates cluster in the low-7s). NISMO (2017–2019) was the factory performance trim: tuned suspension, 18-in wheels, aero, 215/45 tires — same 188 hp as SR Turbo.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: B17 SR Turbo/NISMO has the only real tuner base — shared MR16DDT with Juke means intakes, downpipes, cat-backs and ECU tunes exist; coilovers (e.g., HIRO) for B18; wheel/tire upgrades common. CVT cars should avoid aggressive tunes.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: B17 sat on Nissan's V platform with a twist-beam rear axle. B18 moved to the CMF-C/D architecture — 2 in lower and wider, with a multi-link independent rear suspension replacing the beam axle, a big rigidity and ride-quality step. The 2026 car keeps the MR20DD but adds 6% more body rigidity, a retuned CVT with Sport mode, acoustic glass and available ProPILOT Assist.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: SE-R/Spec V nostalgia keeps the nameplate warm, but modern Sentras are commuter cars; the B17 NISMO is the only recent trim with cult interest, and its tuner support piggybacks on the Juke 1.6T aftermarket. B18 is respected as a value play, not a build platform.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Honda Civic, Toyota Corolla, Hyundai Elantra, Mazda3, Kia K4. Sentra undercuts on price and offers class-leading front legroom and seats, but its NA 2.0/CVT powertrain trails rivals' turbo and hybrid options on both pace and MPG; B18+ build quality now rates better than its reputation (IQS class win), while B17 CVT history still hurts resale.

nissan-frontier.subtitle: Midsize pickup; 2015–2021 (D40; new 3.8L V6/9AT powertrain for 2020–2021), 2022–2026 (D41)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: D40 (2015–2019): 4.0L VQ40DE NA V6 (port injection); 2.5L QR25DE NA I4 (4x2 King Cab only, through 2019). D40 (2020–2021) & D41 (2022–2026): 3.8L VQ38DD NA DI V6 (93% new parts vs. 4.0L, integrated exhaust manifolds).

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: D40 2015–2019: 5MT (2.5L), 6MT (V6 4x2), or 5-speed automatic. 2020–2021: all-new 9-speed automatic only (manuals discontinued). D41: 9-speed automatic (similar to Titan's unit, per Nissan; supplier code not verified). No CVT ever used.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 2.5L: 152 hp @ 5,200 rpm. 4.0L V6: 261 hp @ 5,600 rpm. 3.8L V6: 310 hp @ 6,400 rpm (best-in-class claim at 2020/2022 launch).

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: RWD (4x2) standard; part-time shift-on-the-fly 4WD (2HI/4HI/4LO) optional; PRO-4X (4x4 only) adds electronic locking rear diff; PRO-X (2022+, 4x2 Crew Cab) is the 2WD off-road-trim counterpart.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: D40: wheelbase 125.9 in (139.9 in LWB) / length 205.5 in (219.4 in LWB) / width 72.8 in / height ~70.1 in / curb ~4,100–4,624 lb by configuration (Crew samples 4,443–4,624 lb; base King Cab 2.5 4x2 below range — exact figure needs OEM source). D41: wheelbase 126.0 in (139.8 in LWB) / length 210.2 in (224.1 in LWB) / width 73.0–74.7 in / height 71.6–72.9 in / curb 4,451–4,773 lb (Nissan spec sheet).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: D40 is regarded as one of Nissan's toughest products — simple proven powertrain, fully boxed frame; Consumer Reports flagged high expected reliability on the newest models (per Motor Verso). Chronic D40 watch items: frame corrosion in salt states (dealers call it the #1 killer), timing-chain guide wear at high mileage, and the radiator/ATF-cooler cross-contamination ("SMOD") issue concentrated on 2005–2010 automatics — still worth pressure-testing on any D40. D41 early build issues are recall-level, not pattern failures: 9-speed parking-pawl rollaway recall (180,176 trucks, 2020–2022 Frontier/Titan) and airbag-inflator recall 24V154000 (2024 Frontier).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: D40: SMOD radiator/trans-cooler failure (mainly pre-2012 autos — verify cooler on any automatic D40); timing-chain guide/tensioner rattle on VQ40DE at 100k+; frame rust-through in rust belt; leaf-spring and secondary air-injection niggles. 2020+: parking pawl may not engage in Park (recall; advise parking brake). No systemic VQ38DD engine defect documented as of mid-2026.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: VQ38DD: 0W-20, 5.3 qt w/filter; platinum-tipped plugs ~105,000 mi; coolant 12.7 qt capacity. Oil/filter 5,000 mi/6 mo (severe 3,750 mi). 4WD transfer case/front diff fluids per severe-service schedule when towing/off-road. All engines timing chain. ATF: Nissan Matic-spec service under severe use; exact OEM interval — Not verified — needs OEM source (consult ESM by VIN).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: 2020 Frontier 3.8 ~8.0 sec, 2021 ~7.4 sec (autofiles compilation); D41 PRO-4X low-7s in publication tests — precise figure Not verified. Max tow 6,720 lb (D41, properly equipped); max payload 1,610 lb. PRO-4X: Bilstein shocks, e-locker, skid plates, all-terrain tires.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: One of Nissan's strongest aftermarket scenes: lift/level kits and 33-in tires, winch bumpers, skid plates, bed racks/rooftop tents, regears, and Stillen supercharger support for the VQ40DE; NISMO factory off-road accessories offered on D41. Long-travel suspension kits exist for desert builds.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: D40 rides the F-Alpha fully boxed ladder frame shared in concept with the first-gen Titan, with double-wishbone front / leaf-sprung solid-axle rear. The 2020–2021 trucks were a rolling powertrain prototype for the D41: the DI 3.8L and 9-speed landed a year before the new body. D41 keeps the same 126.0-in wheelbase and frame architecture but adds hydraulic cab mounts (−80% road vibration), retuned hydraulic steering (+16% ratio), bigger stabilizer bars and a modern interior.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: The D40 has a loyal budget-truck following (ClubFrontier community) — cheap, simple, durable, hugely modifiable; values rose as people discovered it. The D41 PRO-4X is respected as a no-nonsense off-roader and one of the last NA-V6 midsize trucks; tuner support is chassis/overland-focused rather than power-focused.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota Tacoma, Ford Ranger, Chevy Colorado/GMC Canyon, Jeep Gladiator. Frontier's pitch is value and simplicity — the only naturally aspirated V6 left in segment vs. rivals' turbo fours, typically the lowest price, solid reliability; it trails the GM trucks and Ranger on refinement, towing tech and cabin polish, and the Tacoma on resale.

nissan-pathfinder.subtitle: 3-row midsize SUV; 2015–2020 (R52; facelift 2017; no 2021 model year), 2022–2026 (R53; Rock Creek from 2023; refresh for 2026)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: R52 2015–2016: 3.5L VQ35DE NA V6 (port injection). R52 2017–2020 & R53 2022–2026: 3.5L VQ35DD NA DI V6 (11.0:1, mirror-bore coating). (A 250-hp net hybrid existed for 2014 only — outside the 2015+ window.)

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: R52: Xtronic CVT (Jatco CVT8 family; D-Step logic added 2017). R53: all-new 9-speed automatic with paddle shifters (replaced the CVT).

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: R52 2015–2016: 260 hp. R52 2017–2020: 284 hp @ 6,400 rpm. R53: 284 hp @ 6,400 rpm; Rock Creek 295 hp @ 6,400 rpm on premium fuel.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: R52: FWD or 4x4-i selectable 4WD. R53: FWD standard; Intelligent 4WD with direct-coupling clutch and 7-position Drive & Terrain Mode selector (Standard/Sport/Eco/Snow/Sand/Mud-Rut/Tow); Hill Descent Control on 4WD.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: R52 2015–2016: length 197.2 in / width 77.2 in / height ~69.5 in / wheelbase 114.2 in / curb 4,149–4,486 lb. R52 2017–2020: length 198.5 in / width 77.3 in / height 69.5 in / curb 4,292–4,662 lb. R53: length 197.7 in / width 77.9 in / height 69.7–70.9 in / wheelbase 114.2 in / curb 4,317–4,625 lb (Nissan spec sheet).

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: R52 2017–2020: 20/27/22 2WD; 19/26 4WD (Nissan); 2015–2016 rated the same 20/27 2WD, 19/26 4WD (EPA carryover per Nissan's 2017 release). R53: 21/26/23 2WD; 21/27/23 4WD (Platinum 4WD 20/25/22) (Nissan/EPA).

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: R52: 2013–2017 Pathfinders were swept into Nissan's CVT class-action/warranty-extension programs (84 mo/84,000 mi) over CVT judder; 2013–2016 models recalled June 2022 for secondary hood-latch corrosion (322,671 US vehicles). R53: ~295 NHTSA complaints 2022–2025 (The Weekly Driver), top areas brakes, engine, electrical — far below R52 CVT-era levels; airbag-inflator recall 24V154000 covers 2024 Pathfinder. The 9-speed swap removed the single biggest failure point.

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: R52: CVT shudder/judder and torque-converter failure (class actions, multiple TSBs — the model's defining defect); hood-latch corrosion recall; radiator fan and liftgate strut complaints secondary. R53: brake-system complaints (premature wear/feel), scattered electrical issues; the 9-speed rollaway recall that hit Frontier/Titan does NOT apply to Pathfinder (Nissan limited it to pickups). Rock Creek's off-road tires/trims add no known mechanical liabilities.

maintenance.label: Maintenance
maintenance.text: VQ35DD: 0W-20, 5.1 qt w/filter; iridium plugs ~105,000 mi; coolant 2.5 gal capacity. Oil/filter 5,000 mi/6 mo (severe 3,750 mi). R52 CVT fluid NS-3 — 60,000-mi drain/fill strongly advised given the judder history. R53 9AT fluid service per severe schedule — exact OEM interval Not verified — needs OEM source. All engines timing chain. 2025+ models include Nissan Maintenance Care (3 oil changes/2 yr).

performance.label: Performance
performance.text: 0–60: R52 2019 SV FWD ~6.4 sec, 1/4-mile 14.9 @ 93 mph (Motor Matchup); R53 0–60 — Not verified — needs OEM/publication source (publications put it in the high-6s). Max towing: 5,000 lb (R52 2013–2016), 6,000 lb (R52 2017+ and R53, properly equipped, SAE J2807). Rock Creek: 5/8-in lift, off-road-tuned suspension, all-terrain tires, tubular roof rack.

mods.label: Modifications
mods.text: Light aftermarket — all-terrain tires, mild lifts, roof racks and towing accessories dominate; Rock Creek serves as the factory "build." No meaningful engine-tuning scene on either generation.

engineering.label: Engineering
engineering.text: R52 marked Pathfinder's shift from body-on-frame truck to unibody crossover (shared D-platform bones with Murano/Maxima) — better packaging and MPG, but paired to the problem-prone CVT. R53 is a clean-sheet unibody with ~50% more high-strength steel, stiffer suspension mounts (+28% front roll stiffness), dual-pinion EPS, and the CVT replaced by a conventional 9-speed automatic; 8-passenger seating standard, 6,000-lb tow.

enthusiast.label: Enthusiast
enthusiast.text: Enthusiasts revere the R50/R51 truck-based Pathfinders and largely wrote off the R52 as a soft-roader with a bad transmission. The R53 partially rehabbed the nameplate — the 9AT and Rock Creek trim brought back some credibility — but it remains a family hauler, not a build platform.

comparison.label: Comparison
comparison.text: Rivals: Toyota Highlander, Honda Pilot, Kia Telluride, Hyundai Palisade, Ford Explorer. Pathfinder undercuts Telluride/Palisade on price and beats them on max towing (6,000 vs 5,000–5,500 lb), and the 9AT/V6 combo is honest and proven; it trails the Koreans on cabin polish and the Highlander hybrid on MPG, and the R52's CVT reputation still shadows used-car values.

ram-1500.subtitle: Full-size half-ton pickup. 2015–2018 (4th gen, DS platform; continued as "1500 Classic" 2019–2024), 2019–2024 (5th gen, DT platform), 2025–2026 (DT facelift — Hurricane I6 replaces HEMI; 5.7L HEMI returns for 2026)

engine.label: Engine
engine.text: 2015–2018 (DS / Classic): 3.6L Pentastar V6 (ERB, naturally aspirated, DOHC VVT); 5.7L HEMI V8 (EZH, NA, MDS cylinder deactivation, VVT); 3.0L EcoDiesel V6 (VM Motori L630, DOHC 24v turbodiesel) 240 hp/420 lb-ft. 2019–2024 (DT): 3.6L Pentastar V6 eTorque (48V belt motor-generator mild hybrid, standard); 5.7L HEMI V8 (EZH) with or without eTorque; 3.0L EcoDiesel Gen 3 (VM L630, 260 hp/480 lb-ft, 2020–2023); 6.2L supercharged HEMI V8 "Hellcat" (TRX only, 2021–2024). 2025–2026: 3.6L Pentastar eTorque (base); 3.0L Hurricane twin-turbo I6 standard-output (GME-T6); 3.0L Hurricane twin-turbo I6 High-Output (540 hp); 5.7L HEMI V8 eTorque re-added for 2026.

transmission.label: Transmission
transmission.text: All 1500s in scope use ZF-sourced TorqueFlite 8-speed automatics: 845RE behind the 3.6L V6; 8HP70 behind EcoDiesel and 5.7L applications; 8HP75 high-capacity unit behind HEMI/eTorque V8 and the TRX. No manual transmission offered 2015–2026.

horsepower.label: Horsepower
horsepower.text: 3.6L Pentastar: 305 hp @ 6,400 rpm (all years). 5.7L HEMI (with or without eTorque): 395 hp @ 5,600 rpm. EcoDiesel: 240 hp (2014–2019 DS/Classic), 260 hp @ 3,600 rpm (2020–2023 Gen 3). TRX 6.2L supercharged: 702 hp @ 6,100 rpm. Hurricane SO: 420 hp; Hurricane HO: 540 hp.

torque.label: Torque
torque.text: 3.6L Pentastar: 269 lb-ft @ ~4,175–4,800 rpm. 5.7L HEMI: 410 lb-ft @ 3,950 rpm. EcoDiesel: 420 lb-ft (DS) / 480 lb-ft (Gen 3). TRX: 650 lb-ft @ 4,800 rpm. Hurricane SO: 469 lb-ft; Hurricane HO: 521 lb-ft.

drivetrain.label: Drivetrain
drivetrain.text: Rear-wheel drive (4x2) standard on most trims; part-time 4WD optional across the range (Borg Warner transfer cases; auto-4WD full-time mode on upper trims). Rebel, TRX (2021–2024), and RHO (2025+) are 4WD only. Hurricane HO models are 4x4 standard.

dimensions.label: Dimensions
dimensions.text: DS/Classic (2015–2024): length 229 in (Crew Cab 5'7") – 237.9 in (Crew Cab 6'4"); width 79.4 in; height ~74.6–77.2 in (air suspension adds height options); wheelbase 140.5 in (Crew 5'7") / 149.5 in (Crew 6'4"); curb weight ~4,750–5,700 lb depending on cab/engine/4x4. DT (2019–2026): Quad Cab 6'4": length 228.9 in, wheelbase 140.5 in; Crew Cab 5'7": length 232.9 in, wheelbase 144.6 in; Crew Cab 6'4": wheelbase 153.5 in; width 82.1 in; height 77.5–77.7 in (standard suspension; up to ~79.6 in with air suspension Off-Road 2 setting); curb weight ~4,800–5,800 lb; TRX ~6,350 lb.

mpg.label: Fuel economy (MPG)
mpg.text: DS (EPA city/highway/combined): 3.6L 4x2 17/25/20, 4x4 16/23/19; 5.7L 4x2 15/22/17, 4x4 15/21/17; EcoDiesel 4x2 20/27/23, 4x4 19/26/22 (2014–2016 figures revised downward after EPA/CARB emissions-settlement reflash — check fueleconomy.gov for the exact year). DT 2019+: 3.6L eTorque 4x2 20/25/22, 4x4 19/24/21; 5.7L HEMI 4x2 15/22/17, 4x4 15/21/17; HEMI eTorque 4x2 17/23/19; EcoDiesel (2020+) 4x2 22/32/26, 4x4 21/29/24. 2025+: 3.6L eTorque 4x2 20/25/22 (HFE 4x2 20/26/23); Hurricane SO 4x2 18/25/21, 4x4 17/24/19; Hurricane HO 4x4 15/21/17 (RHO similar). TRX: 10/14/12.

reliability.label: Reliability
reliability.text: Mixed reputation by generation. The DS (2015–2018) is mechanically simple and durable in Pentastar/HEMI form, but the 5.7L has a well-documented lifter/camshaft failure pattern ("HEMI tick") and EcoDiesel trucks carry recall and settlement baggage (see Known issues). The DT won MotorTrend Truck of the Year 2019, 2020, 2021 (and 2025 for the facelift) and Ram scored strongly in J.D. Power initial-quality and APEAL studies, but 2019 first-year trucks had above-average electrical/infotainment complaints and Consumer Reports owner-survey data for DT trucks flags in-car electronics and electrical accessories as trouble spots. The 2022 Ram 1500 earned an IIHS Top Safety Pick and a 5-star NHTSA overall crash rating for the crew cab. NHTSA recall-count totals per model year: Not verified — needs OEM source (use nhtsa.gov/recalls per VIN for customer vehicles).

knownIssues.label: Known issues
knownIssues.text: (1) 5.7L HEMI lifter roller seizure scoring the camshaft — "HEMI tick" progressing to misfire; affects DS and DT trucks, debated incidence (owner-forum estimates run high-teens percent); also broken exhaust-manifold bolts mimicking the tick. (2) EcoDiesel EGR cooler thermal-fatigue cracking/fire risk — October 2019 recall of ~107,000+ trucks (2014–2019 Ram 1500 EcoDiesel), followed by a 2025 class-action settlement with extended warranty and reimbursement terms.
